topics.title: Rectangular, Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinate Systems - Coordinate Transformation, Vector Calculus: Gradient of a Scalar Field, Divergence of a Vector Field and Curl of a Vector Field - Their Physical Interpretation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rectangular, Cylindrical and Spherical Coordinate Systems - Coordinate Transformation, Vector Calculus: Gradient of a Scalar Field, Divergence of a Vector Field and Curl of a Vector Field - Their Physical Interpretation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Coordinate Systems — The Mathematical Map of Space

In Electromagnetics, every physical quantity (electric field, magnetic field, potential, charge density) lives somewhere in 3D space. To **locate** a point and **measure** the direction/magnitude of a field at that point, we need a **coordinate system**. KTU 2024 Scheme mandates mastery of three systems, because real engineering problems are rarely "nice" rectangles — they are pipes (cylindrical) or antennas/orbits (spherical).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A student must be able to:
> 1. Convert a point/vector between Rectangular $\leftrightarrow$ Cylindrical $\leftrightarrow$ Spherical coordinates.
> 2. Compute Gradient, Divergence, and Curl in any of the three systems.
> 3. **Physically interpret** what each operator *measures* (rate of change, source/sink strength, rotation).

### 1.1.1 Rectangular (Cartesian) Coordinate System

- **Coordinates of a point:** $P(x, y, z)$
- **Unit vectors:** $\hat{a}_x,\ \hat{a}_y,\ \hat{a}_z$ — mutually perpendicular, constant in direction everywhere in space.
- **Position vector:** $\vec{r} = x\,\hat{a}_x + y\,\hat{a}_y + z\,\hat{a}_z$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a multi-storey building. Each room is uniquely identified by (Floor, Wing, Room No.). Rectangular coordinates work the same way — every point in space has a unique address $(x, y, z)$. The walls (planes), pillars (lines), and corners (points) of the building are like the surfaces of constant $x$, $y$, $z$.

### 1.1.2 Cylindrical Coordinate System

- **Coordinates of a point:** $P(\rho, \phi, z)$
  - $\rho$ = perpendicular distance from the $z$-axis (always $\geq 0$)
  - $\phi$ = angle measured from the positive $x$-axis in the $xy$-plane (azimuth)
  - $z$ = same as rectangular height
- **Unit vectors:** $\hat{a}_\rho,\ \hat{a}_\phi,\ \hat{a}_z$
- **Position vector:** $\vec{r} = \rho\,\hat{a}_\rho + z\,\hat{a}_z$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a drinking straw held vertically. $\rho$ is how far your finger is from the centre of the straw, $\phi$ is the direction you rotated from the front, and $z$ is the height along the straw. The unit vector $\hat{a}_\rho$ **always points radially outward**, so it changes direction as you walk around the straw — unlike $\hat{a}_x$.

### 1.1.3 Spherical Coordinate System

- **Coordinates of a point:** $P(r, \theta, \phi)$
  - $r$ = distance from origin (always $\geq 0$)
  - $\theta$ = polar angle measured **from the positive $z$-axis** (0 to $\pi$)
  - $\phi$ = azimuthal angle in the $xy$-plane from the $+x$ axis (0 to $2\pi$)
- **Unit vectors:** $\hat{a}_r,\ \hat{a}_\theta,\ \hat{a}_\phi$
- **Position vector:** $\vec{r} = r\,\hat{a}_r$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Stand at the centre of a giant football stadium. $r$ is your distance from the speaker at the centre of the field, $\theta$ is the angle your head tilts down from the ceiling (zenith), and $\phi$ is the direction you turn. **All three unit vectors change direction as you move** — they depend on where you are.

---

## 1.2 Vector Calculus Operators — The Three Pillars of Field Theory

### 1.2.1 Gradient of a Scalar Field ($\nabla V$)

**Formal Definition:** The gradient of a scalar field $V(x,y,z)$ is a **vector field** whose magnitude at any point equals the **maximum rate of change** of $V$ at that point, and whose direction is the direction in which $V$ increases most rapidly.

$$\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are standing on a hillside and you are blindfolded. The gradient tells you (a) **which direction to step** to climb the steepest way up, and (b) **how steep** that climb is. If you ever feel flat ground ($\nabla V = 0$), you are at a local peak, valley, or saddle point.

**Physical Interpretation:** In Electromagnetics, $\nabla V$ is the negative of the electric field, $-\vec{E} = \nabla V$, where $V$ is the electric potential. So the gradient maps "voltage hills" to the force that pushes a positive charge.

### 1.2.2 Divergence of a Vector Field ($\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$)

**Formal Definition:** The divergence of a vector field $\vec{F}$ at a point is the **net outward flux per unit volume** as the volume shrinks to that point. It is a **scalar** quantity.

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a fountain in a pool. Water flows *out* of the fountain (source, divergence $> 0$). Now imagine a drain — water flows *into* the drain (sink, divergence $< 0$). If you place a tiny invisible ball near a uniform stream of water, no net water enters or leaves it — divergence is **zero**.

**Physical Interpretation:** In Electromagnetics, **Gauss's Law** states $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$. The divergence of the electric flux density at a point equals the **volume charge density** there. Positive divergence = source of field lines; negative divergence = sink; zero = no monopole at that point (or sources and sinks cancel locally).

### 1.2.3 Curl of a Vector Field ($\nabla \times \vec{F}$)

**Formal Definition:** The curl of a vector field $\vec{F}$ at a point is a **vector** whose magnitude is the **maximum net circulation per unit area** as the area shrinks to that point, and whose direction is the **axis of rotation** (right-hand rule) of the infinitesimal paddle wheel that spins fastest at that point.

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left(\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z}\right)\hat{a}_x + \left(\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x}\right)\hat{a}_y + \left(\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\right)\hat{a}_z$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Drop a tiny paddle wheel into a river. In calm, straight-flowing water, the wheel doesn't spin — curl is zero. In a whirlpool, the wheel spins fastest at the centre, with its axis pointing along the vortex — curl is maximum. The direction (by right-hand rule) tells you the sense of rotation.

**Physical Interpretation:** In Electromagnetics, **Faraday's Law** has the curl of $\vec{E}$ equal to the negative time-rate of change of $\vec{B}$. The curl of $\vec{H}$ equals current density $\vec{J}$ (Ampère's Law). A non-zero curl means the field "swirls" — it cannot be written as the gradient of any scalar potential.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Coordinate System Anatomy — Why Three?

The choice of coordinate system is dictated by **geometry symmetry** of the problem. A coaxial cable is best in cylindrical; a dipole antenna is best in spherical; a parallel-plate capacitor is best in rectangular.

| Feature | Rectangular | Cylindrical | Spherical |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coordinates | $x,\ y,\ z$ | $\rho,\ \phi,\ z$ | $r,\ \theta,\ \phi$ |
| Ranges | $-\infty$ to $\infty$ each | $\rho \geq 0,\ 0 \leq \phi < 2\pi,\ -\infty < z < \infty$ | $r \geq 0,\ 0 \leq \theta \leq \pi,\ 0 \leq \phi < 2\pi$ |
| Unit Vectors | $\hat{a}_x, \hat{a}_y, \hat{a}_z$ (constant) | $\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi, \hat{a}_z$ ($\hat{a}_z$ constant) | $\hat{a}_r, \hat{a}_\theta, \hat{a}_\phi$ (all vary with position) |
| Ideal Geometry | Boxes, slabs | Pipes, rods, wires | Spheres, antennas, planets |
| Differential length $d\vec{l}$ | $dx\,\hat{a}_x + dy\,\hat{a}_y + dz\,\hat{a}_z$ | $d\rho\,\hat{a}_\rho + \rho\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi + dz\,\hat{a}_z$ | $dr\,\hat{a}_r + r\,d\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta + r\sin\theta\,d\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$ |
| Differential volume $dv$ | $dx\,dy\,dz$ | $\rho\,d\rho\,d\phi\,dz$ | $r^2\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Unit vectors in cylindrical ($\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi$) and spherical ($\hat{a}_r, \hat{a}_\theta, \hat{a}_\phi$) are **NOT constant** — they are functions of position. This is why partial derivatives of these unit vectors are **non-zero**, making operator formulas more complex.

---

## 2.2 Coordinate Transformation Cheat Sheet

### 2.2.1 Rectangular $\leftrightarrow$ Cylindrical

The same point $P$ has coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and $(\rho, \phi, z)$. Using the right triangle in the $xy$-plane:

$$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}, \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right), \quad z = z$$

$$x = \rho\cos\phi, \quad y = \rho\sin\phi, \quad z = z$$

Unit vector transformation (derived from chain rule on unit vectors):

$$\hat{a}_\rho = \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y$$
$$\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_y$$
$$\hat{a}_z = \hat{a}_z$$

### 2.2.2 Rectangular $\leftrightarrow$ Spherical

The same point $P$ has coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and $(r, \theta, \phi)$:

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}, \quad \theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z}{\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}}\right), \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

$$x = r\sin\theta\cos\phi, \quad y = r\sin\theta\sin\phi, \quad z = r\cos\theta$$

Unit vector transformation:

$$\hat{a}_r = \sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y + \cos\theta\,\hat{a}_z$$
$$\hat{a}_\theta = \cos\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_y - \sin\theta\,\hat{a}_z$$
$$\hat{a}_\phi = -\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_x + \cos\phi\,\hat{a}_y$$

### 2.2.3 Cylindrical $\leftrightarrow$ Spherical

$$r = \sqrt{\rho^2 + z^2}, \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\rho}{z}\right), \quad \phi = \phi$$

$$\rho = r\sin\theta, \quad \phi = \phi, \quad z = r\cos\theta$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Master Formula Sheet — Vector Operators

### 2.3.1 Rectangular Coordinates

$$\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{a}_x & \hat{a}_y & \hat{a}_z \\ \partial/\partial x & \partial/\partial y & \partial/\partial z \\ F_x & F_y & F_z \end{vmatrix}$$

### 2.3.2 Cylindrical Coordinates

$$\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\hat{a}_\rho + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho F_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left(\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial z}\right)\hat{a}_\rho + \left(\frac{\partial F_\rho}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial \rho}\right)\hat{a}_\phi + \frac{1}{\rho}\left(\frac{\partial(\rho F_\phi)}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial F_\rho}{\partial \phi}\right)\hat{a}_z$$

### 2.3.3 Spherical Coordinates

$$\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\hat{a}_\theta + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial(r^2 F_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial(\sin\theta\, F_\theta)}{\partial \theta} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\left[\frac{\partial(\sin\theta\, F_\phi)}{\partial \theta} - \frac{\partial F_\theta}{\partial \phi}\right]\hat{a}_r + \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{1}{\sin\theta}\frac{\partial F_r}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial(r F_\phi)}{\partial r}\right]\hat{a}_\theta + \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{\partial(r F_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial F_r}{\partial \theta}\right]\hat{a}_\phi$$

### 2.3.4 Laplacian (Second-Order Operator)

The Laplacian $\nabla^2 V = \nabla \cdot (\nabla V)$ is essential for **Poisson's** and **Laplace's equations**.

$$\nabla^2 V = \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} \quad \text{(Rectangular)}$$

$$\nabla^2 V = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial}{\partial \rho}\!\left(\rho\frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\right) + \frac{1}{\rho^2}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} + \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} \quad \text{(Cylindrical)}$$

$$\nabla^2 V = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^2\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2\sin^2\theta}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} \quad \text{(Spherical)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This single Module underpins every problem in Electromagnetics — from deriving **Laplace's equation** for potential in a capacitor, to writing **Maxwell's equations** in their compact differential form. **No KTU question on electrostatics or magnetostatics is solvable without these formulas.**

---

## 2.4 The Three Identities Every KTU Student Must Memorize

| Identity | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $\nabla \times (\nabla V) = 0$ | Gradient fields are **irrotational** (conservative). |
| $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = 0$ | Curl fields are **solenoidal** (no sources/sinks). |
| $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = 0$ and $\nabla \times (\nabla V) = 0$ | Used to convert between potential and field forms. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Gradient in Rectangular Coordinates

**Starting principle:** The total differential of $V(x,y,z)$ is:

$$dV = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}dy + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}dz$$

**Geometric interpretation:** $dV$ is the **dot product** of the gradient vector with the infinitesimal displacement vector $d\vec{l}$.

$$d\vec{l} = dx\,\hat{a}_x + dy\,\hat{a}_y + dz\,\hat{a}_z$$

$$dV = \nabla V \cdot d\vec{l}$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$dV = (\nabla V) \cdot (\hat{a}_x\,dx + \hat{a}_y\,dy + \hat{a}_z\,dz)$$

$$dV = (\nabla V) \cdot \hat{a}_x\,dx + (\nabla V) \cdot \hat{a}_y\,dy + (\nabla V) \cdot \hat{a}_z\,dz$$

**Matching coefficients** of $dx, dy, dz$ on both sides:

$$(\nabla V) \cdot \hat{a}_x = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}, \quad (\nabla V) \cdot \hat{a}_y = \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}, \quad (\nabla V) \cdot \hat{a}_z = \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}$$

**Therefore, the gradient is:**

$$\boxed{\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{a}_x + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{a}_y + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Divergence from the Divergence Theorem

The **divergence theorem** (Gauss's theorem) states:

$$\oint_S \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{F})\,dv$$

**Apply to an infinitesimal rectangular box** centered at $(x, y, z)$ with sides $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$:

**Step 1:** Compute the flux through each of the 6 faces.

Let $\vec{F} = F_x\hat{a}_x + F_y\hat{a}_y + F_z\hat{a}_z$.

**Flux through right face** (at $x + \Delta x/2$, outward normal $+\hat{a}_x$):

$$\Phi_{\text{right}} = F_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y, z\right)\,\Delta y\,\Delta z$$

**Flux through left face** (at $x - \Delta x/2$, outward normal $-\hat{a}_x$):

$$\Phi_{\text{left}} = -F_x\!\left(x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y, z\right)\,\Delta y\,\Delta z$$

**Net flux in x-direction:**

$$\Phi_x = \left[F_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right) - F_x\!\left(x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right)\right]\Delta y\,\Delta z$$

**Step 2:** Apply Taylor expansion around $(x, y, z)$.

$$F_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right) \approx F_x + \frac{\Delta x}{2}\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}$$

$$F_x\!\left(x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right) \approx F_x - \frac{\Delta x}{2}\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}$$

**Subtracting:**

$$F_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right) - F_x\!\left(x - \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}\right) \approx \Delta x\,\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}$$

**So:**

$$\Phi_x \approx \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}\,\Delta x\,\Delta y\,\Delta z = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x}\,\Delta v$$

**Step 3:** Repeat for $y$ and $z$ directions.

$$\Phi_y \approx \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y}\,\Delta v, \quad \Phi_z \approx \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}\,\Delta v$$

**Step 4:** Total flux = sum.

$$\Phi_{\text{total}} = \left(\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}\right)\Delta v$$

**Step 5:** By the divergence theorem, $\Phi_{\text{total}} = (\nabla \cdot \vec{F})\,\Delta v$.

**Therefore, matching coefficients:**

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Curl from Stokes' Theorem

**Stokes' theorem:**

$$\oint_C \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_S (\nabla \times \vec{F}) \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Apply to an infinitesimal loop** in the $xy$-plane, centered at $(x, y, z)$, of area $\Delta x\,\Delta y$, with normal $\hat{a}_z$.

**Step 1:** Compute the line integral around the loop (4 sides):

**Side 1:** $(x, y) \to (x+\Delta x, y)$, $d\vec{l} = \hat{a}_x\,dx'$:

$$\int_{\text{side 1}} \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l} \approx F_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y, z\right)\Delta x$$

**Side 2:** $(x+\Delta x, y) \to (x+\Delta x, y+\Delta y)$, $d\vec{l} = \hat{a}_y\,dy'$:

$$\int_{\text{side 2}} \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l} \approx F_y\!\left(x+\Delta x, y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}, z\right)\Delta y$$

**Side 3:** $(x+\Delta x, y+\Delta y) \to (x, y+\Delta y)$, $d\vec{l} = -\hat{a}_x\,dx'$:

$$\int_{\text{side 3}} \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l} \approx -F_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y+\Delta y, z\right)\Delta x$$

**Side 4:** $(x, y+\Delta y) \to (x, y)$, $d\vec{l} = -\hat{a}_y\,dy'$:

$$\int_{\text{side 4}} \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l} \approx -F_y\!\left(x, y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}, z\right)\Delta y$$

**Step 2:** Sum all 4 sides (group $F_x$ terms and $F_y$ terms):

$$\oint_C \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l} = \left[F_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y\right) - F_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y+\Delta y\right)\right]\Delta x + \left[F_y\!\left(x+\Delta x, y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}\right) - F_y\!\left(x, y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}\right)\right]\Delta y$$

**Step 3:** Apply Taylor expansion in the variable of interest:

$$F_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y\right) - F_x\!\left(x + \tfrac{\Delta x}{2}, y+\Delta y\right) \approx -\Delta y\,\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\bigg|_{\text{at } \left(x+\tfrac{\Delta x}{2},\ y+\tfrac{\Delta y}{2}\right)}$$

$$F_y\!\left(x+\Delta x, y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}\right) - F_y\!\left(x, y + \tfrac{\Delta y}{2}\right) \approx \Delta x\,\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x}\bigg|_{\text{at } \left(x+\tfrac{\Delta x}{2},\ y+\tfrac{\Delta y}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and divide by area $\Delta x\,\Delta y$:

$$\frac{\oint_C \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{l}}{\Delta x\,\Delta y} \approx \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y} = (\nabla \times \vec{F}) \cdot \hat{a}_z$$

**So the $\hat{a}_z$ component of curl is:**

$$(\nabla \times \vec{F})_z = \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}$$

**Step 5:** Repeat for loops in the $yz$-plane (gives $\hat{a}_x$ component) and $xz$-plane (gives $\hat{a}_y$ component):

$$(\nabla \times \vec{F})_x = \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z}, \quad (\nabla \times \vec{F})_y = \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x}$$

**Therefore, the full curl is:**

$$\boxed{\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left(\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_y}{\partial z}\right)\hat{a}_x + \left(\frac{\partial F_x}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial x}\right)\hat{a}_y + \left(\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_x}{\partial y}\right)\hat{a}_z}$$

---

## 3.4 Generalised Gradient, Divergence and Curl in Curvilinear Coordinates

A unified approach uses **generalised coordinates** $(u_1, u_2, u_3)$ with **scale factors** (Lamé coefficients) $h_1, h_2, h_3$.

| System | $h_1$ | $h_2$ | $h_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $1$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| Cylindrical | $1$ | $\rho$ | $1$ |
| Spherical | $1$ | $r$ | $r\sin\theta$ |

The differential arc length: $d\vec{l} = h_1\,du_1\,\hat{a}_1 + h_2\,du_2\,\hat{a}_2 + h_3\,du_3\,\hat{a}_3$.

**Generalised Gradient:**

$$\nabla V = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{1}{h_i}\frac{\partial V}{\partial u_i}\,\hat{a}_i$$

**Generalised Divergence:**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{h_1 h_2 h_3}\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial u_1}(h_2 h_3 F_1) + \frac{\partial}{\partial u_2}(h_3 h_1 F_2) + \frac{\partial}{\partial u_3}(h_1 h_2 F_3)\right]$$

**Generalised Curl** (component $i$):

$$(\nabla \times \vec{F})_i = \frac{1}{h_j h_k}\left[\frac{\partial(h_k F_k)}{\partial u_j} - \frac{\partial(h_j F_j)}{\partial u_k}\right]$$

where $(i, j, k)$ is a cyclic permutation of $(1, 2, 3)$.

### Worked Derivation Example — Divergence in Cylindrical

**Substitute** $u_1 = \rho$, $u_2 = \phi$, $u_3 = z$, so $h_1 = 1$, $h_2 = \rho$, $h_3 = 1$:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{(1)(\rho)(1)}\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial \rho}\!\left(\rho \cdot 1 \cdot F_\rho\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial \phi}\!\left(1 \cdot 1 \cdot F_\phi\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(1 \cdot \rho \cdot F_z\right)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{\rho}\left[\frac{\partial(\rho F_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \rho\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho F_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}}$$

This is precisely the cylindrical divergence formula in Section 2.3.2 — confirming the method.

### Worked Derivation Example — Gradient in Spherical

**Substitute** $u_1 = r$, $u_2 = \theta$, $u_3 = \phi$, so $h_1 = 1$, $h_2 = r$, $h_3 = r\sin\theta$:

$$\nabla V = \frac{1}{1}\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\hat{a}_\theta + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi$$

$$\boxed{\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\hat{a}_\theta + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Pattern)

### Example 1: Convert Point $P(3, 4, 12)$ to Spherical

**Given:** $x = 3,\ y = 4,\ z = 12$.

**Step 1:** Compute $r$.

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\theta$.

$$\cos\theta = \frac{z}{r} = \frac{12}{13} \implies \theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{12}{13}\right) \approx 22.62^\circ$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\phi$.

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \approx 53.13^\circ$$

**Answer:** $P = (13,\ 22.62^\circ,\ 53.13^\circ)$ in spherical.

### Example 2: Compute Divergence of $\vec{F} = \rho^2\,\hat{a}_\rho + z\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi + \cos^2\phi\,\hat{a}_z$ at $(2, \pi/2, 3)$

**Step 1:** Identify components: $F_\rho = \rho^2$, $F_\phi = z\sin\phi$, $F_z = \cos^2\phi$.

**Step 2:** Apply cylindrical divergence formula.

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho F_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3:** Compute each term.

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho \cdot \rho^2)}{\partial \rho} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho^3)}{\partial \rho} = \frac{1}{\rho}(3\rho^2) = 3\rho$$

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(z\sin\phi)}{\partial \phi} = \frac{1}{\rho}(z\cos\phi) = \frac{z\cos\phi}{\rho}$$

$$\frac{\partial(\cos^2\phi)}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Add and substitute $(\rho, \phi, z) = (2, \pi/2, 3)$.

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = 3\rho + \frac{z\cos\phi}{\rho} = 3(2) + \frac{(3)\cos(\pi/2)}{2} = 6 + 0 = 6$$

**Answer:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = 6$ at the given point.

### Example 3: Compute Curl of $\vec{A} = r\sin\theta\,\hat{a}_r + r^2\cos\theta\,\hat{a}_\theta + r\,\hat{a}_\phi$ at $(r, \theta, \phi) = (2, \pi/2, 0)$

**Step 1:** Apply spherical curl formula.

$$\nabla \times \vec{A} = \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\left[\frac{\partial(\sin\theta\, A_\phi)}{\partial \theta} - \frac{\partial A_\theta}{\partial \phi}\right]\hat{a}_r + \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{1}{\sin\theta}\frac{\partial A_r}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial(r A_\phi)}{\partial r}\right]\hat{a}_\theta + \frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{\partial(r A_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial A_r}{\partial \theta}\right]\hat{a}_\phi$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $A_r = r\sin\theta$, $A_\theta = r^2\cos\theta$, $A_\phi = r$.

**$\hat{a}_r$ component:**

$$\frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\left[\frac{\partial(r\sin\theta)}{\partial \theta} - 0\right] = \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}(r\cos\theta) = \frac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \cot\theta$$

**$\hat{a}_\theta$ component:**

$$\frac{1}{r}\left[0 - \frac{\partial(r^2)}{\partial r}\right] = \frac{1}{r}(-2r) = -2$$

**$\hat{a}_\phi$ component:**

$$\frac{1}{r}\left[\frac{\partial(r^3\cos\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial(r\sin\theta)}{\partial \theta}\right] = \frac{1}{r}\left[3r^2\cos\theta - r\cos\theta\right] = 3r\cos\theta - \cos\theta = \cos\theta(3r - 1)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $(r, \theta) = (2, \pi/2)$: $\cot(\pi/2) = 0$, $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$.

$$\nabla \times \vec{A} = 0\,\hat{a}_r - 2\,\hat{a}_\theta + 0 = -2\,\hat{a}_\theta$$

**Answer:** $\nabla \times \vec{A} = -2\,\hat{a}_\theta$ at $(2, \pi/2, 0)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three Coordinate Systems — Schematic Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3D Point P in Space] --> B{Coordinate System}
    B --> C[Rectangular x y z]
    B --> D[Cylindrical rho phi z]
    B --> E[Spherical r theta phi]

    C --> C1[Constant unit vectors]
    C --> C2[Ideal for boxes/slabs]

    D --> D1[rho hat phi hat vary]
    D --> D2[z hat constant]
    D --> D3[Ideal for cylinders/wires]

    E --> E1[All 3 unit vectors vary]
    E --> E2[Ideal for spheres/antennas]

    C <--> D[Transform via cos sin phi]
    C <--> E[Transform via sin cos]
    D <--> E[Transform via r cos theta]
```

## 4.2 Vector Operators — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Quantity]
        SF[Scalar Field V x y z]
        VF[Vector Field F]
    end

    subgraph Operators[Vector Calculus Operators]
        GRAD[Gradient nabla V]
        DIV[Divergence nabla dot F]
        CURL[Curl nabla cross F]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Quantity]
        VF2[Vector Field]
        SC[Scalar]
        VF3[Vector Field]
    end

    SF --> GRAD --> VF2
    VF --> DIV --> SC
    VF --> CURL --> VF3

    GRAD -. Irrotational .-> CURL
    DIV -. Solenoidal .-> CURL
    CURL --> LAP[Laplacian]
    GRAD --> LAP
```

## 4.3 Physical Interpretation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP[Vector Operator] --> PI{Physical Meaning}

    PI --> P1[Gradient nabla V]
    PI --> P2[Divergence nabla dot F]
    PI --> P3[Curl nabla cross F]

    P1 --> M1[Direction and rate of steepest ascent]
    P1 --> E1[Electric Field minus nabla V]

    P2 --> M2[Net outward flux per unit volume]
    P2 --> E2[Charge density per Gauss Law]

    P3 --> M3[Vortex strength at a point]
    P3 --> E3[Current density per Ampere Law]
```

## 4.4 Coordinate Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point in Rectangular x y z] --> Q1{Step 1 Project to xy}
    Q1 --> Q2[Compute rho = sqrt x squared + y squared]
    Q2 --> Q3[Compute phi = tan inverse y over x]
    Q3 --> R1[Point in Cylindrical rho phi z]

    R1 --> S1{Project to z axis}
    S1 --> S2[Compute r = sqrt rho squared + z squared]
    S2 --> S3[Compute theta = tan inverse rho over z]
    S3 --> S4[Point in Spherical r theta phi]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the divergence of a vector field. State its physical significance in Electromagnetics.**

**Model Answer:**

The divergence of a vector field $\vec{F}$ at a point $P$ is defined as the **net outward flux per unit volume** as the volume enclosing $P$ shrinks to zero:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \lim_{\Delta v \to 0} \frac{\oint_S \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S}}{\Delta v}$$

In rectangular coordinates: $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \dfrac{\partial F_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial F_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$.

**Physical Significance:** In Electromagnetics, Gauss's law in point form states $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$. Thus, the divergence of electric flux density at any point equals the **volume charge density** at that point. A positive divergence indicates a **source** of field lines; a negative divergence indicates a **sink**; zero divergence indicates no net source (e.g., the divergence of the magnetic flux density $\vec{B}$ is always zero, indicating the **non-existence of magnetic monopoles**).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define the gradient of a scalar field. Give the expression for $\nabla V$ in cylindrical coordinates.**

**Model Answer:**

The gradient of a scalar field $V$ is a **vector field** whose magnitude at any point equals the **maximum rate of spatial change** of $V$ at that point, and whose direction is along the direction in which $V$ increases most rapidly.

**In cylindrical coordinates:**

$$\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial \rho}\hat{a}_\rho + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{a}_z$$

The gradient always points **normal** to the constant-$V$ surfaces (equipotential surfaces), and is fundamental in the relation $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$ in electrostatics.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT mix up** cylindrical and spherical angles. $\theta$ is the **polar angle from $+z$** (in spherical), while $\phi$ is the **azimuth** in both cylindrical and spherical.
> - **Always include unit vectors** in your final answer; numerical answer without $\hat{a}_r$ etc. loses 1–2 marks.
> - **Sign errors in curl** are extremely common — the cross product order is $\nabla \times \vec{F}$, not $\vec{F} \times \nabla$.
> - For coordinate conversion, **always quote the quadrant** when computing $\tan^{-1}$.

---

### Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** Transform the point $P(3, 4, 12)$ from rectangular to:
   - (i) Cylindrical coordinates (3 marks)
   - (ii) Spherical coordinates (4 marks)

**(b)** A vector field in cylindrical coordinates is given by $\vec{F} = \rho^2\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_\rho + 3z\,\hat{a}_\phi + \rho\sin^2\phi\,\hat{a}_z$. Compute:
   - (i) $\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$ (3 marks)
   - (ii) $\nabla \times \vec{F}$ at the point $\rho = 2,\ \phi = \pi/2,\ z = 1$ (4 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**(a)(i) Rectangular to Cylindrical:** $x = 3,\ y = 4,\ z = 12$.

$$\rho = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \approx 53.13^\circ = 0.9273\text{ rad}$$

$$z = 12$$

**Answer:** $P(\rho, \phi, z) = (5,\ 53.13^\circ,\ 12)$.

**(a)(ii) Rectangular to Spherical:**

$$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$$

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{z}{r}\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{12}{13}\right) \approx 22.62^\circ$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \approx 53.13^\circ$$

**Answer:** $P(r, \theta, \phi) = (13,\ 22.62^\circ,\ 53.13^\circ)$.

**(b)(i) Divergence of $\vec{F}$ in cylindrical:**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho F_\rho)}{\partial \rho} + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial \phi} + \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial z}$$

With $F_\rho = \rho^2\cos\phi$, $F_\phi = 3z$, $F_z = \rho\sin^2\phi$:

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho^3\cos\phi)}{\partial \rho} = \frac{1}{\rho}(3\rho^2\cos\phi) = 3\rho\cos\phi$$

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(3z)}{\partial \phi} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial(\rho\sin^2\phi)}{\partial z} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = 3\rho\cos\phi}$$

**(b)(ii) Curl of $\vec{F}$:**

$$\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left(\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial z}\right)\hat{a}_\rho + \left(\frac{\partial F_\rho}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial \rho}\right)\hat{a}_\phi + \frac{1}{\rho}\left(\frac{\partial(\rho F_\phi)}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial F_\rho}{\partial \phi}\right)\hat{a}_z$$

**$\hat{a}_\rho$ component:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho\sin^2\phi)}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial(3z)}{\partial z} = \frac{1}{\rho}(2\rho\sin\phi\cos\phi) - 3 = \sin(2\phi) - 3$$

**$\hat{a}_\phi$ component:**

$$\frac{\partial(\rho^2\cos\phi)}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial(\rho\sin^2\phi)}{\partial \rho} = 0 - \sin^2\phi = -\sin^2\phi$$

**$\hat{a}_z$ component:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\left[\frac{\partial(\rho \cdot 3z)}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial(\rho^2\cos\phi)}{\partial \phi}\right] = \frac{1}{\rho}\left[3z - \rho^2(-\sin\phi)\right] = \frac{3z}{\rho} + \rho\sin\phi$$

**Substitute** $\rho = 2$, $\phi = \pi/2$, $z = 1$:

- $\hat{a}_\rho$: $\sin(\pi) - 3 = 0 - 3 = -3$
- $\hat{a}_\phi$: $-\sin^2(\pi/2) = -1$
- $\hat{a}_z$: $\frac{3(1)}{2} + 2\sin(\pi/2) = 1.5 + 2 = 3.5$

**Answer:** $\nabla \times \vec{F}\big|_{(2, \pi/2, 1)} = -3\,\hat{a}_\rho - \hat{a}_\phi + 3.5\,\hat{a}_z$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, define the **spherical coordinate system**. Derive the expressions for:
   - (i) The differential volume element $dv$ (3 marks)
   - (ii) The Laplacian $\nabla^2 V$ in spherical coordinates (4 marks)

**(b)** Given a scalar potential $V = 50 r^2 \sin\theta\cos\phi$ in free space, compute:
   - (i) $\nabla V$ at the point $(r, \theta, \phi) = (2, \pi/2, \pi/4)$ (3 marks)
   - (ii) $\nabla^2 V$ at the same point and comment on whether $V$ satisfies Laplace's equation (4 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**(a)(i) Differential volume element in spherical coordinates:**

Consider an infinitesimal cube in $(r, \theta, \phi)$ space with sides $dr$, $r\,d\theta$, $r\sin\theta\,d\phi$ (these are arc lengths because $\theta$ and $\phi$ are angles). The volume is the product:

$$dv = (dr)(r\,d\theta)(r\sin\theta\,d\phi) = r^2\sin\theta\,dr\,d\theta\,d\phi$$

**(a)(ii) Laplacian in spherical coordinates:**

The Laplacian is $\nabla^2 V = \nabla \cdot (\nabla V)$.

In spherical coordinates, the gradient is:

$$\nabla V = \frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\hat{a}_r + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\hat{a}_\theta + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi}\hat{a}_\phi$$

Treat this as a vector field $\vec{F}$ with $F_r = \partial V/\partial r$, $F_\theta = (1/r)\partial V/\partial \theta$, $F_\phi = (1/(r\sin\theta))\partial V/\partial \phi$, and apply the spherical divergence:

$$\nabla^2 V = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^2\frac{\partial V}{\partial r}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2\sin\theta}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\right) + \frac{1}{r^2\sin^2\theta}\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2}$$

**(b)(i) Gradient of $V = 50 r^2 \sin\theta\cos\phi$:**

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial r} = 100r\sin\theta\cos\phi$$

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta} = 50r^2\cos\theta\cos\phi$$

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial \phi} = -50r^2\sin\theta\sin\phi$$

**Substitute into the spherical gradient formula:**

$$\nabla V = 100r\sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_r + \frac{1}{r}(50r^2\cos\theta\cos\phi)\,\hat{a}_\theta + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}(-50r^2\sin\theta\sin\phi)\,\hat{a}_\phi$$

$$= 100r\sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_r + 50r\cos\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{a}_\theta - 50r\sin\phi\,\hat{a}_\phi$$

**At** $(r, \theta, \phi) = (2, \pi/2, \pi/4)$:

- $100(2)(1)(\sqrt{2}/2) = 100\sqrt{2}$
- $50(2)(0)(\sqrt{2}/2) = 0$
- $-50(2)(\sqrt{2}/2) = -50\sqrt{2}$

$$\boxed{\nabla V = 100\sqrt{2}\,\hat{a}_r + 0\,\hat{a}_\theta - 50\sqrt{2}\,\hat{a}_\phi}$$

**(b)(ii) Laplacian of $V$:**

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial r} = 100r\sin\theta\cos\phi \implies \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^2 \cdot 100r\sin\theta\cos\phi\right) = \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(100r^3\sin\theta\cos\phi\right) = 300r^2\sin\theta\cos\phi$$

So the first term: $\dfrac{1}{r^2}\cdot 300r^2\sin\theta\cos\phi = 300\sin\theta\cos\phi$.

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta} = 50r^2\cos\theta\cos\phi \implies \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta \cdot 50r^2\cos\theta\cos\phi\right) = 50r^2\cos\phi\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\!\left(\sin\theta\cos\theta\right) = 50r^2\cos\phi\cos(2\theta)$$

Wait — using $\dfrac{d}{d\theta}(\sin\theta\cos\theta) = \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos(2\theta)$. So:

Second term: $\dfrac{1}{r^2\sin\theta}\cdot 50r^2\cos\phi\cos(2\theta) = \dfrac{50\cos\phi\cos(2\theta)}{\sin\theta}$.

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} = -50r^2\sin\theta\cos\phi \quad \text{(since derivative of } -\sin\phi \text{ is } -\cos\phi \text{, times } -1 = +\cos\phi \text{, but wait)}$$ 

Recompute: $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \phi} = -50r^2\sin\theta\sin\phi$, so $\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial \phi^2} = -50r^2\sin\theta\cos\phi$.

Third term: $\dfrac{1}{r^2\sin^2\theta}\cdot(-50r^2\sin\theta\cos\phi) = \dfrac{-50\cos\phi}{\sin\theta}$.

**Sum:**

$$\nabla^2 V = 300\sin\theta\cos\phi + \frac{50\cos\phi\cos(2\theta)}{\sin\theta} - \frac{50\cos\phi}{\sin\theta}$$

Using $\cos(2\theta) = 1 - 2\sin^2\theta$:

$$\frac{50\cos\phi[1 - 2\sin^2\theta] - 50\cos\phi}{\sin\theta} = \frac{50\cos\phi - 100\sin^2\theta\cos\phi - 50\cos\phi}{\sin\theta} = -100\sin\theta\cos\phi$$

So:

$$\nabla^2 V = 300\sin\theta\cos\phi - 100\sin\theta\cos\phi = 200\sin\theta\cos\phi$$

**At** $(r, \theta, \phi) = (2, \pi/2, \pi/4)$:

$$\nabla^2 V = 200(1)(\sqrt{2}/2) = 100\sqrt{2}$$

**Comment:** Since $\nabla^2 V = 100\sqrt{2} \neq 0$, the potential $V$ does **NOT** satisfy Laplace's equation ($\nabla^2 V = 0$). Instead, it satisfies **Poisson's equation** $\nabla^2 V = -\rho_v/\varepsilon_0$ in a region with charge density.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### ✅ Coordinate Systems
- **Rectangular** $(x, y, z)$: constant unit vectors; use for boxes, slabs, rectangular waveguides.
- **Cylindrical** $(\rho, \phi, z)$: use for wires, pipes, coaxial cables; $\hat{a}_z$ is the only constant unit vector.
- **Spherical** $(r, \theta, \phi)$: use for antennas, planets, point charges; **all three unit vectors vary**.
- **Always remember:** $\theta$ is measured **from $+z$ axis**, $\phi$ is measured **from $+x$ axis** in the $xy$-plane.

### ✅ Coordinate Conversion Quick Reference
- $r^2 = x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = \rho^2 + z^2$
- $\rho = r\sin\theta$, $z = r\cos\theta$
- $\tan\phi = y/x$ (handle quadrants!)
- $\cos\theta = z/r$

### ✅ Gradient ($\nabla V$)
- Result: **Vector** field.
- Meaning: Direction and rate of **steepest ascent**.
- Formula: Divide each partial derivative by its scale factor $h_i$.
- **Engineering use:** $\vec{E} = -\nabla V$.

### ✅ Divergence ($\nabla \cdot \vec{F}$)
- Result: **Scalar** field.
- Meaning: Net **outward flux per unit volume** = source strength density.
- Engineering use: $\nabla \cdot \vec{D} = \rho_v$ (Gauss's Law), $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$ (no monopoles).
- Divergence is **zero** for solenoidal fields (e.g., $\vec{B}$).

### ✅ Curl ($\nabla \times \vec{F}$)
- Result: **Vector** field.
- Meaning: Infinitesimal **circulation** per unit area = local rotation/vorticity.
- Engineering use: $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\partial \vec{B}/\partial t$ (Faraday), $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$ (Ampère).
- Curl is **zero** for irrotational/conservative fields (e.g., $-\nabla V$).

### ✅ Memorise These Identities
- $\nabla \times (\nabla V) = \vec{0}$ — gradient is curl-free.
- $\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{F}) = 0$ — curl is divergence-free.

### ✅ Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid
- Forgetting the factor of $\rho$ or $r\sin\theta$ in cylindrical/spherical formulas.
- Mixing $\theta$ and $\phi$ between spherical and cylindrical.
- Treating $\hat{a}_\rho, \hat{a}_\phi, \hat{a}_r, \hat{a}_\theta$ as constants.
- Sign errors in the curl expansion (always verify with the determinant form first).
- Forgetting to specify the quadrant in $\phi = \tan^{-1}(y/x)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Divergence Theorem, Stokes' Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Divergence Theorem, Stokes' Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Divergence Theorem & Stokes' Theorem

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Divergence Theorem (Gauss's Divergence Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> The Divergence Theorem states that the **net outward flux** of a continuously differentiable vector field $\vec{F}$ through a **closed, orientable surface** $S$ bounding a region $V$ in space is exactly equal to the **volume integral** of the divergence of $\vec{F}$ over the entire region $V$ enclosed by that surface.

Mathematically:

$$
\oint\oint_{S} \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{S} = \iiint_{V} (\nabla \cdot \vec{F}) \, dV
$$

where $d\vec{S} = \hat{n}\,dS$ and $\hat{n}$ is the **outward unit normal vector** to the surface $S$.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Leaky Balloon" Intuition**
> Imagine a closed, porous balloon (the surface $S$) containing some fluid (the vector field $\vec{F}$). Now, suppose fluid is being generated or absorbed *inside* the balloon at some rate that varies from point to point. The Divergence Theorem tells us a beautiful thing: **you don't need to know how much fluid is leaking through every tiny pore of the balloon's surface individually** — you can simply add up the total rate of generation (or loss) of fluid at every point *inside* the balloon. Both totals must match. The left side is "flux out of the surface"; the right side is "total source strength inside."

In simpler words: **local behaviour (divergence at each point) sums up to give global behaviour (flux through the boundary).**

### 1.2 Stokes' Theorem (The Curl Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> Stokes' Theorem relates the **circulation** (line integral) of a vector field $\vec{F}$ around a **closed curve** $C$ to the **flux of the curl** of $\vec{F}$ through **any open, orientable surface** $S$ whose boundary is the curve $C$.

Mathematically:

$$
\oint_{C} \vec{F} \cdot d\vec{r} = \iint_{S} (\nabla \times \vec{F}) \cdot d\vec{S}
$$

where:
- $C$ is the closed boundary curve of $S$
- The orientation of $C$ is related to $\hat{n}$ by the **Right-Hand Rule**: curl the fingers of your right hand along $C$, your thumb points in the direction of $\hat{n}$ (or vice versa depending on convention).

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Spinning Pond" Intuition**
> Picture a small paddle wheel placed at some point on a sheet floating on a pond. The rotation of the wheel measures the *local* swirling tendency (the curl) of the water at that point. Now, Stokes' Theorem says: if you total up all the tiny spin contributions over the entire sheet, it will exactly match the total rotation experienced by walking around the outer edge of the sheet. **Edge circulation = sum of local spins inside.**

In simpler words: **the global swirling around the boundary equals the sum of all the local swirls in the interior.**

### 1.3 Why These Theorems Matter (Engineering Utility)

| Engineering Domain | Application of Divergence / Stokes' Theorem |
|---|---|
| **Electromagnetics** | Deriving Gauss's Law & Ampère's Law in integral form from Maxwell's equations |
| **Fluid Dynamics** | Conservation of mass, momentum, and energy across control volumes |
| **Heat Transfer** | Heat flux divergence in conduction problems |
| **Antenna & RF Design** | Computing radiated power through closed surfaces |
| **Computer Graphics** | Surface integral evaluations, vector field rendering |

**Standard physical constants referenced:** None required directly; both theorems are **coordinate-free geometric identities** valid in $\mathbb{R}^2$, $\mathbb{R}^3$, and any orthogonal curvilinear system.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Divergence Theorem — Operational Breakdown

### Logical Steps to Apply the Theorem

1. **Verify Closedness:** Confirm that the surface $S$ is **closed** (it fully encloses a finite volume $V$ with no holes or gaps). If open, Divergence Theorem does *not* apply.
2. **Identify the Vector Field:** Express $\vec{F} = F_1 \hat{i} + F_2 \hat{j} + F_3 \hat{k}$ in Cartesian, cylindrical, or spherical form depending on geometry.
3. **Compute Divergence:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \dfrac{\partial F_1}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial F_2}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial F_3}{\partial z}$ in Cartesian.
4. **Choose the Easier Side:** Often the **volume integral** (RHS) is much simpler than computing flux over a complicated surface. Use symmetry, divergence-free fields, or known divergence values to your advantage.
5. **Apply the Surface Integral (LHS) when needed:** Break the closed surface into simpler patches if flux computation is required.
6. **Orientation Rule:** $\hat{n}$ must point **outward** from $V$.

### Cylindrical & Spherical Divergence Forms (High-Yield)

In **cylindrical coordinates** $(\rho, \phi, z)$:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\rho F_{\rho})}{\partial \rho} + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial F_{\phi}}{\partial \phi} + \frac{\partial F_{z}}{\partial z}
$$

In **spherical coordinates** $(r, \theta, \phi)$:

$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{F} = \frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial(r^{2} F_{r})}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial(\sin\theta\, F_{\theta})}{\partial \theta} + \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\frac{\partial F_{\phi}}{\partial \phi}
$$

---

## 2.2 Stokes' Theorem — Operational Breakdown

### Logical Steps to Apply the Theorem

1. **Identify a Surface Bounded by the Curve:** Any open surface whose boundary is $C$ works. This is a **superpower** of Stokes' Theorem — you choose the *most convenient* surface.
2. **Orient the Curve:** Using the right-hand rule, decide the direction of traversal for $C$ given the chosen $\hat{n}$ on $S$.
3. **Compute the Curl:** $\nabla \times \vec{F}$ in the appropriate coordinate system.
4. **Choose the Easier Side:** For many problems, choosing a **flat disk or a planar cap** as the surface $S$ makes $(\nabla \times \vec{F}) \cdot \hat{n}$ trivially constant. This often converts a hard surface integral into a simple one.

### Curl in Different Coordinate Systems (High-Yield)

In **Cartesian coordinates**:

$$
\nabla \times \vec{F} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ \partial_x & \partial_y & \partial_z \\ F_1 & F_2 & F_3 \end{vmatrix}
$$

In **cylindrical coordinates** $(\rho, \phi, z)$:

$$
\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left( \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial F_z}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial F_\phi}{\partial z} \right)\hat{\rho} + \left( \frac{\partial F_\rho}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_z}{\partial \rho} \right)\hat{\phi} + \frac{1}{\rho}\left( \frac{\partial(\rho F_\phi)}{\partial \rho} - \frac{\partial F_\rho}{\partial \phi} \right)\hat{z}
$$

In **spherical coordinates** $(r, \theta, \phi)$:

$$
\nabla \times \vec{F} = \frac{1}{r\sin\theta}\left[ \frac{\partial(F_\phi \sin\theta)}{\partial \theta} - \frac{\partial F_\theta}{\partial \phi} \right]\hat{r} + \frac{1}{r}\left[ \frac{1}{\sin\theta}\frac{\partial F_r}{\partial \phi} - \frac{\partial(rF_\phi)}{\partial r} \right]\hat{\theta} + \frac{1}{r}\left[ \frac{\partial(rF_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{\partial F_r}{\partial \theta} \right]\hat{\phi}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the absolute value symbol inside formulas:** In all displayed equations above, $\vert\cdot\vert$ is rendered as a *determinant bar* in the curl expansion, not a markdown table pipe. When this content is placed inside a markdown table cell, use $\lvert\cdot\rvert$ or $\text{abs}(\cdot)$ to avoid breaking the table syntax.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Theorem | Integral Form | Differential Identity Used | Required Conditions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Divergence Theorem** | $\displaystyle\oint\oint_S \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{S}=\iiint_V(\nabla\cdot\vec{F})\,dV$ | $\nabla\cdot\vec{F}$ is a scalar source density | $S$ closed, $\vec{F}\in C^1$, $V$ simply connected | Gauss's Law, continuity equation |
| **Stokes' Theorem** | $\displaystyle\oint_C \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r}=\iint_S(\nabla\times\vec{F})\cdot d\vec{S}$ | $\nabla\times\vec{F}$ is a vector vortex density | $C$ closed, $S$ open with $\partial S = C$, $\vec{F}\in C^1$ | Ampère's Law, Faraday's Law |
| **Vector Identity (Green's)** | $\displaystyle\oint_C \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r}=\iint_S(\nabla\times\vec{F})\cdot\hat{n}\,dS$ | Same as Stokes' in 2D | $C$ closed planar | Circulation in 2D |
| **Special case: $\nabla\times\vec{F}=0$** | $\displaystyle\oint_C \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r}=0$ | Conservative field | Simply connected domain | Potential theory |

### Vector Calculus Pre-Requisites (Must-Know)

$$
\nabla \cdot (\nabla \times \vec{A}) = 0 \quad \text{(divergence of a curl is always zero)}
$$

$$
\nabla \times (\nabla \phi) = 0 \quad \text{(curl of a gradient is always zero)}
$$

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof of the Divergence Theorem (For a Rectangular Box → Generalization)

The standard rigorous proof proceeds in three logical stages: (a) prove for a rectangular parallelepiped, (b) extend to arbitrary regions by subdivision, (c) take the limit.

### Step 1: Set Up the Statement for a Rectangular Box

Consider a box $V = [x_1, x_2] \times [y_1, y_2] \times [z_1, z_2]$ with closed surface $S$. Let $\vec{F} = F_1\hat{i} + F_2\hat{j} + F_3\hat{k}$.

### Step 2: Prove the $x$-Component Identity

We claim:

$$
\oint\oint_S F_1 \, \hat{i} \cdot d\vec{S} = \iiint_V \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial x}\, dV
$$

The left side receives contributions only from the two faces perpendicular to the $x$-axis. The face at $x = x_2$ has outward normal $+\hat{i}$, area element $dy\,dz$, and the face at $x = x_1$ has outward normal $-\hat{i}$.

$$
\oint\oint_S F_1 \hat{i}\cdot d\vec{S} = \iint_{y_1}^{y_2}\iint_{z_1}^{z_2} F_1(x_2,y,z)\,dy\,dz - \iint_{y_1}^{y_2}\iint_{z_1}^{z_2} F_1(x_1,y,z)\,dy\,dz
$$

$$
= \iint_{y_1}^{y_2}\iint_{z_1}^{z_2} \left[\,F_1(x_2,y,z) - F_1(x_1,y,z)\right]\,dy\,dz
$$

By the **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus** in the $x$-direction:

$$
F_1(x_2,y,z) - F_1(x_1,y,z) = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \frac{\partial F_1(x,y,z)}{\partial x}\,dx
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\oint\oint_S F_1 \hat{i}\cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{x_1}^{x_2}\int_{y_1}^{y_2}\int_{z_1}^{z_2} \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial x}\,dz\,dy\,dx = \iiint_V \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial x}\,dV
$$

### Step 3: Repeat for the $y$ and $z$ Components

By identical reasoning (just renaming variables and using the two faces perpendicular to each axis):

$$
\oint\oint_S F_2 \hat{j}\cdot d\vec{S} = \iiint_V \frac{\partial F_2}{\partial y}\,dV
$$

$$
\oint\oint_S F_3 \hat{k}\cdot d\vec{S} = \iiint_V \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial z}\,dV
$$

### Step 4: Add All Three Components

Summing the three identities:

$$
\oint\oint_S (F_1\hat{i} + F_2\hat{j} + F_3\hat{k})\cdot d\vec{S} = \iiint_V \left(\frac{\partial F_1}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_2}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial z}\right) dV
$$

$$
\oint\oint_S \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{S} = \iiint_V (\nabla \cdot \vec{F})\, dV \qquad \blacksquare
$$

### Step 5: Generalization (Sketch)

A general region can be partitioned into small boxes. The flux contributions on interior shared faces cancel (opposite normals), leaving only flux on the outer boundary. Taking the limit as box sizes $\to 0$ extends the result to any region with piecewise-smooth boundary and continuously differentiable $\vec{F}$.

---

## 3.2 Proof of Stokes' Theorem (Cartesian Form)

### Step 1: Set Up the Surface

Let $S$ be a smooth, orientable surface in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with piecewise-smooth closed boundary $C$. Express $S$ locally as $z = g(x,y)$ for a small patch.

### Step 2: Surface Integral (RHS) in Cartesian Form

The surface element in the form $z = g(x,y)$:

$$
d\vec{S} = \left(-\frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\hat{i} - \frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \hat{k}\right) dx\,dy
$$

Compute the curl:

$$
\nabla \times \vec{F} = \left(\frac{\partial F_3}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_2}{\partial z}\right)\hat{i} + \left(\frac{\partial F_1}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial x}\right)\hat{j} + \left(\frac{\partial F_2}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial y}\right)\hat{k}
$$

Then:

$$
(\nabla \times \vec{F})\cdot d\vec{S} = \left[\left(\frac{\partial F_3}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_2}{\partial z}\right)\left(-\frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial F_1}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial x}\right)\left(-\frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial F_2}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial y}\right)\right] dx\,dy
$$

### Step 3: Line Integral (LHS) and Green's Theorem

Parametrize $C$ as the image of a closed curve in the $xy$-plane under the mapping $(x,y) \mapsto (x, y, g(x,y))$. The line integral becomes:

$$
\oint_C \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r} = \oint_C F_1\,dx + F_2\,dy + F_3\left(\frac{\partial g}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial g}{\partial y}dy\right)
$$

Group terms by $dx$ and $dy$:

$$
= \oint_C \left(F_1 + F_3 \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\right) dx + \left(F_2 + F_3 \frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\right) dy
$$

### Step 4: Apply Green's Theorem in the Plane

Green's Theorem states:

$$
\oint_C P\,dx + Q\,dy = \iint_D \left(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial P}{\partial y}\right) dx\,dy
$$

where $D$ is the projection of $S$ onto the $xy$-plane. Setting $P = F_1 + F_3 \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}$ and $Q = F_2 + F_3 \frac{\partial g}{\partial y}$:

$$
\oint_C \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r} = \iint_D \left[\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(F_2 + F_3 \frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(F_1 + F_3 \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\right)\right] dx\,dy
$$

### Step 5: Expand and Match the Surface Integral

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(F_2 + F_3 \frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\right) = \frac{\partial F_2}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial x}\frac{\partial g}{\partial y} + F_3 \frac{\partial^2 g}{\partial x \partial y}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(F_1 + F_3 \frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial y}\frac{\partial g}{\partial x} + F_3 \frac{\partial^2 g}{\partial y \partial x}
$$

The mixed partial terms cancel (assuming $g \in C^2$):

$$
\frac{\partial Q}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial P}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial F_2}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial y}\frac{\partial g}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial x}\frac{\partial g}{\partial y}
$$

Now rearrange this expression to match the surface integral from Step 2:

$$
= \left(\frac{\partial F_2}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial y}\right) - \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial x}\frac{\partial g}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial y}\frac{\partial g}{\partial x}
$$

$$
= \left(\frac{\partial F_2}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial y}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial F_3}{\partial y}\right)\left(-\frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\right)\cdot(-1) + \left(\frac{\partial F_1}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial x}\right)\cdot\left(-\frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\right)
$$

Collecting all terms inside the integral:

$$
\oint_C \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r} = \iint_D \left[\left(\frac{\partial F_3}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial F_2}{\partial z}\right)\left(-\frac{\partial g}{\partial x}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial F_1}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial F_3}{\partial x}\right)\left(-\frac{\partial g}{\partial y}\right) + \left(\frac{\partial F_2}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial y}\right)\right] dx\,dy
$$

$$
\oint_C \vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r} = \iint_S (\nabla \times \vec{F})\cdot d\vec{S} \qquad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix (Proof Techniques)

| Aspect | Divergence Theorem | Stokes' Theorem |
|---|---|---|
| **Integrand type** | Volume integral of a *scalar* | Surface integral of a *vector* |
| **Boundary** | Closed surface $S$ | Open surface $S$ with closed curve $C$ |
| **Reduction technique** | Fundamental Theorem of Calculus per axis | Green's Theorem in the projection plane |
| **Boundary orientation** | $\hat{n}$ outward | Right-hand rule for $\hat{n} \leftrightarrow C$ |
| **Generalization** | Continuous extension by subdivision | Smooth parameterization of $S$ |
| **Failure mode** | Open surface; non-$C^1$ field | Surface not orientable; field not defined on $S$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Divergence Theorem — Geometric Architecture

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **functional architecture** of the Divergence Theorem: how the surface flux (boundary observation) is mathematically equivalent to the volume integral of divergence (interior observation).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Vector Field F defined on Region V with Closed Surface S"] --> B["Two Equivalent Computational Paths"]
    B --> C["Path 1: Surface Flux Path - Integrate F dot n dS over entire closed S"]
    B --> D["Path 2: Volume Source Path - Integrate div F dV over the interior V"]
    C --> E["Choose S: Decompose into simpler surface patches with outward normal"]
    D --> F["Choose V: Use symmetry or coordinate system suited to geometry"]
    E --> G["Sum all outward flux contributions - careful with orientation"]
    F --> H["Evaluate div F scalar field - often much easier analytically"]
    G --> I["Result: Surface Flux Value"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Conclusion: Both paths must yield the same scalar value"]
    J --> K["Key Condition: S must be closed and F must be C1 continuous on V"]
```

## 4.2 Stokes' Theorem — Geometric Architecture

This Mermaid block illustrates the **functional architecture** of Stokes' Theorem, emphasizing the *freedom of choice* for the auxiliary surface $S$ bounded by the curve $C$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Given a closed curve C and a vector field F"] --> B["Step 1: Choose ANY open surface S with boundary C"]
    B --> C["Step 2A: Compute line integral of F along C using right-hand rule"]
    B --> D["Step 2B: Compute flux of curl F through the chosen S"]
    C --> E["Parametrize C and integrate F dot dr"]
    D --> F["Choose S wisely: a flat disk or planar cap often simplifies curl dot n"]
    E --> G["Result: Circulation Value"]
    F --> H["Compute curl F and dot with normal vector n over S"]
    G --> I["Conclusion: Both must be equal by Stokes Theorem"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Key Condition: F must be C1 on a neighborhood of S and S must be orientable"]
    J --> K["Special Case: If curl F is zero everywhere, the circulation around C is zero"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C["Curl the fingers of your right hand in the direction of traversal of C"]
```
