Now shake it in a circular motion — each point on the rope traces a helix in space, and if you freeze time at a single point on the rope, the tip of the displacement vector moves in a **circle**. This geometric path (line, circle, or ellipse) is exactly what we call the **polarisation** of the wave. --- ## 1.4 Summary of the Three Topics at a Glance | Concept | One-Line Essence | |---|---| | Skin Effect | AC current crowds toward conductor surface | | Skin Depth (δ) | Distance at which current density falls to 1/e ≈ 36.8% | | Poynting Vector (**S**) | Power density flow in EM field = **E** × **H** | | Poynting Theorem | Conservation of energy in electromagnetic fields | | Polarisation | Locus of tip of **E**-vector at a point in space | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Skin Effect — Physical Reasoning When an alternating current flows in a conductor, it produces a time-varying magnetic flux inside the conductor. By **Faraday's Law**, this changing flux induces an EMF (eddy EMF) that opposes the original current (Lenz's Law). The induced EMF is stronger at the **centre** because the central strands are linked by the *entire* flux of the conductor. Hence the central current is choked, and current crowds to the outer regions where flux linkage is minimum. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Skin Effect increases with:** frequency (f), permeability (μ), and conductivity (σ). > **Skin Effect is independent of:** the magnitude of the current itself. --- ## 2.2 Skin Depth — Rigorous Definition > [!NOTE] > **Skin Depth (δ)** is defined as the depth below the surface of a conductor at which the magnitude of the current density **J** has decayed to **1/e (≈ 36.8%)** of its value at the surface. **Mathematical Form:** $$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}$$ Where: - δ = skin depth in metres (m) - ω = angular frequency = 2πf (rad/s) - μ = permeability of conductor (H/m) = μ₀μᵣ - σ = conductivity of conductor (S/m) **Current density inside the conductor:** $$\boxed{J(x) = J_{s}\, e^{-(1+j)\,x/\delta}}$$ Where x is measured inward from the surface, and Jₛ is the surface current density. > [!IMPORTANT] > For a **good conductor** (σ >> ωε), the fields are confined to a thin layer ≈ δ, and the conductor effectively behaves like a surface carrying all the current. --- ## 2.3 Poynting Vector — Field-Level Power Flow The instantaneous Poynting Vector (units: W/m²): $$\boxed{\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}}$$ For a time-harmonic field with phasors **E** and **H**, the **time-average Poynting Vector** is: $$\boxed{\langle \vec{S} \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2}\, \text{Re}\{\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^{*}\}}$$ --- ## 2.4 Poynting Theorem — Conservation of Electromagnetic Energy Starting from Maxwell's curl equations, after multiplying **E** with ∇ × **H** and **H** with ∇ × **E**, then subtracting, and using the vector identity ∇·(**A** × **B**) = **B**·(∇ × **A**) − **A**·(∇ × **B**): $$\boxed{-\oint_{S}(\vec{E}\times\vec{H})\cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{V}\vec{E}\cdot\vec{J}_{c}\, dV + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^{2}\right)dV}$$ **Physical Interpretation (each term in W):** | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | $-\oint_{S}\vec{S}\cdot d\vec{S}$ | Power flowing **into** the closed volume | | $\int_{V}\vec{E}\cdot\vec{J}_{c}\, dV$ | Ohmic (heat) power dissipated inside the volume | | $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V}\tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^{2}dV$ | Rate of change of stored electric energy | | $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V}\tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^{2}dV$ | Rate of change of stored magnetic energy | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Balance:** Power entering the volume = Power dissipated as heat + Rate of increase of stored field energy. This is the field-form equivalent of Kirchhoff's Power Law. --- ## 2.5 Wave Polarisation — Mathematical Setup For a uniform plane wave travelling in the +z direction: $$\vec{E}(z,t) = \hat{a}_{x}E_{0x}\cos(\omega t - \beta z + \phi_{x}) + \hat{a}_{y}E_{0y}\cos(\omega t - \beta z + \phi_{y})$$ **Three Fundamental Types:** | Type | Condition | Locus of E-tip | |---|---|---| | **Linear Polarisation** | φ_y − φ_x = 0 or π (i.e., 0 or ±nπ, n integer) | Straight line | | **Circular Polarisation** | E₀ₓ = E₀ᵧ = E₀ **and** φ_y − φ_x = ±π/2 | Circle | | **Elliptical Polarisation** | All other combinations | Tilted ellipse | > [!NOTE] > **Handedness Rule:** Looking *into* the direction of propagation (−z), if **E** rotates clockwise → **Right-Hand (RH)** polarisation; if counter-clockwise → **Left-Hand (LH)**. The IEEE convention is *opposite* to the physics convention — verify with your textbook! --- ## 2.6 Complete KTU Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | Skin depth | m | | 2 | $J(x) = J_{s}e^{-x/\delta}e^{-jx/\delta}$ | Current density decay | A/m² | | 3 | $R_{ac} = R_{dc}\cdot(\text{surface factor})$ | AC resistance increase | Ω | | 4 | $\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H}$ | Instantaneous Poynting vector | W/m² | | 5 | $\langle \vec{S} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^{*}\}$ | Average Poynting vector | W/m² | | 6 | $\langle \vec{S}_{av} \rangle = \hat{a}_{z}\tfrac{\vert E_{0}\vert^{2}}{2\eta}$ | Plane wave in lossless medium | W/m² | | 7 | $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ | Intrinsic impedance | Ω | | 8 | Polarisation: phase difference Δφ = φ_y − φ_x | Determines polarisation type | rad | | 9 | Axial Ratio = Major axis / Minor axis | Ellipticity measure | dimensionless | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Skin Depth (δ) **Step 1 — Starting Point: Maxwell's Equations inside a conductor (no free charges)** Inside a good conductor: $\vec{J}_{c} = \sigma\vec{E}$, and using phasor form: $$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \sigma\vec{E} + j\omega\varepsilon\vec{E} = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}$$ For a good conductor, $\sigma \gg \omega\varepsilon$, so $\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon \approx \sigma$. **Step 2 — Take the curl of Faraday's Law** $$\nabla \times \nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu(\nabla \times \vec{H})$$ Using the vector identity $\nabla \times \nabla \times \vec{E} = \nabla(\nabla\cdot\vec{E}) - \nabla^{2}\vec{E}$, and noting $\nabla\cdot\vec{E} = 0$ inside a homogeneous conductor: $$-\nabla^{2}\vec{E} = -j\omega\mu\sigma\vec{E}$$ $$\nabla^{2}\vec{E} = j\omega\mu\sigma\vec{E}$$ **Step 3 — Assume a plane wave propagating in +x direction (into the conductor)** Let $\vec{E} = \hat{a}_{z}E_{z}(x)$ (TE to x). The equation becomes: $$\dfrac{d^{2}E_{z}}{dx^{2}} = j\omega\mu\sigma\, E_{z} = \gamma^{2}E_{z}$$ Where the propagation constant is: $$\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma} = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma}\sqrt{j} = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma}\cdot\dfrac{(1+j)}{\sqrt{2}} = (1+j)\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$ **Step 4 — General solution (only decaying wave is physical)** $$E_{z}(x) = E_{0}\, e^{-\gamma x} = E_{0}\, e^{-(1+j)x\sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}}$$ **Step 5 — Define skin depth δ** By convention, the depth at which the field amplitude decays to **1/e** of its surface value: $$e^{-x/\delta} = e^{-x\sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}}\quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dfrac{1}{\delta} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$ $$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f\mu\sigma}}}$$ **Step 6 — Current density expression** Since $\vec{J} = \sigma\vec{E}$: $$\boxed{J(x) = J_{s}\, e^{-(1+j)x/\delta}}$$ The magnitude decays as $|J(x)| = J_{s}\, e^{-x/\delta}$. **Verification:** At x = δ, $|J| = J_{s}/e \approx 0.368\,J_{s}$. ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Poynting Theorem **Step 1 — Start with Maxwell's two curl equations** $$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\dfrac{\partial\vec{B}}{\partial t} = -\mu\dfrac{\partial\vec{H}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(I)}$$ $$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}_{c} + \dfrac{\partial\vec{D}}{\partial t} = \sigma\vec{E} + \varepsilon\dfrac{\partial\vec{E}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(II)}$$ **Step 2 — Dot multiply (I) with H and (II) with E** $$\vec{H}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{E}) = -\mu\vec{H}\cdot\dfrac{\partial\vec{H}}{\partial t}$$ $$\vec{E}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{H}) = \sigma\vec{E}\cdot\vec{E} + \varepsilon\vec{E}\cdot\dfrac{\partial\vec{E}}{\partial t}$$ **Step 3 — Subtract (II-eq) from (I-eq)** $$\vec{H}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{E}) - \vec{E}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{H}) = -\mu\vec{H}\cdot\dfrac{\partial\vec{H}}{\partial t} - \sigma E^{2} - \varepsilon\vec{E}\cdot\dfrac{\partial\vec{E}}{\partial t}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the vector identity** The left side equals the divergence of the cross product: $$\nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) = \vec{H}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{E}) - \vec{E}\cdot(\nabla\times\vec{H})$$ **Step 5 — Recognise energy derivatives** The identities $\dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{A}) = \vec{A}\cdot\dfrac{\partial\vec{A}}{\partial t}$ give: $$\vec{E}\cdot\dfrac{\partial\vec{E}}{\partial t} = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}(E^{2}), \quad \vec{H}\cdot\dfrac{\partial\vec{H}}{\partial t} = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}(H^{2})$$ **Step 6 — Substitute back** $$\nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H}) = -\sigma E^{2} - \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^{2}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Integrate over volume V bounded by closed surface S** $$\int_{V}\nabla\cdot(\vec{E}\times\vec{H})\, dV = -\int_{V}\sigma E^{2}\, dV - \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^{2}\right)dV$$ Applying the **Divergence Theorem** to the left side: $$\oint_{S}(\vec{E}\times\vec{H})\cdot d\vec{S} = -\int_{V}\vec{E}\cdot\vec{J}_{c}\, dV - \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V}\!\left(w_{e} + w_{m}\right)dV$$ Rearranging (reversing sign convention so the flux is *into* the volume): $$\boxed{-\oint_{S}(\vec{E}\times\vec{H})\cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{V}\vec{E}\cdot\vec{J}_{c}\, dV + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^{2}\right)dV}$$ Where $\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H}$ is the **Poynting Vector**. --- ## 3.3 Polarisation Analysis — Setting up the Equation of a Polarised Wave **Step 1 — General uniform plane wave in +z direction** $$\vec{E}(z,t) = \hat{a}_{x}E_{0x}\cos(\omega t - \beta z + \phi_{x}) + \hat{a}_{y}E_{0y}\cos(\omega t - \beta z + \phi_{y})$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate at z = 0 (fixed observation point) for simplicity** $$E_{x}(t) = E_{0x}\cos(\omega t + \phi_{x})$$ $$E_{y}(t) = E_{0y}\cos(\omega t + \phi_{y})$$ Let $\psi = \omega t$ and $\Delta\phi = \phi_{y} - \phi_{x}$. **Step 3 — Case 1: Linear Polarisation (Δφ = 0 or π)** $$E_{x} = E_{0x}\cos\psi, \quad E_{y} = E_{0y}\cos(\psi + \Delta\phi)$$ When Δφ = 0: $E_{y}/E_{0y} = E_{x}/E_{0x} \Rightarrow E_{y} = (E_{0y}/E_{0x})E_{x}$ → **straight line through origin** with slope $E_{0y}/E_{0x}$. When Δφ = π: $E_{y} = -(E_{0y}/E_{0x})E_{x}$ → **straight line through origin** with negative slope. **Step 4 — Case 2: Circular Polarisation (E₀ₓ = E₀ᵧ = E₀, Δφ = ±π/2)** Take Δφ = +π/2: $$E_{x} = E_{0}\cos\psi, \quad E_{y} = -E_{0}\sin\psi$$ Then: $$\dfrac{E_{x}^{2}}{E_{0}^{2}} + \dfrac{E_{y}^{2}}{E_{0}^{2}} = \cos^{2}\psi + \sin^{2}\psi = 1$$ This is a **circle of radius E₀** in the xy-plane. The sign of Δφ determines the handedness. **Step 5 — Case 3: Elliptical Polarisation (general case)** Eliminate ψ by expanding $E_{y}$ using $\cos(\psi + \Delta\phi) = \cos\psi\cos\Delta\phi - \sin\psi\sin\Delta\phi$ and substituting $\cos\psi = E_{x}/E_{0x}$, $\sin\psi = \sqrt{1-(E_{x}/E_{0x})^{2}}$. After algebraic simplification: $$\boxed{\dfrac{E_{x}^{2}}{E_{0x}^{2}} + \dfrac{E_{y}^{2}}{E_{0y}^{2}} - \dfrac{2E_{x}E_{y}\cos\Delta\phi}{E_{0x}E_{0y}} = \sin^{2}\Delta\phi}$$ This is the **general conic equation of an ellipse** — the locus traced by the tip of **E** for arbitrary E₀ₓ, E₀ᵧ, Δφ. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Students often confuse the handedness convention. Remember the *physics* convention: looking **toward** the source (i.e., **against** the direction of propagation), if the **E** rotates counter-clockwise → **Left-Hand** polarisation. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Problem-Solving Template **Problem Type: Calculate skin depth and AC resistance** Given: Copper conductor, f = 1 MHz, σ = 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m, μ = μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m, radius r = 1 mm. Solution: $$\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f\mu\sigma}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 10^{6} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.8 \times 10^{7}}}$$ Compute denominator step by step: $$\pi \times 10^{6} = 3.1416 \times 10^{6}$$ $$4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$5.8 \times 10^{7} = 5.8 \times 10^{7}$$ Product: $$3.1416 \times 1.2566 \times 5.8 \times 10^{6-6+7} = 22.89 \times 10^{7} = 2.289 \times 10^{8}$$ Square root: $\sqrt{2.289 \times 10^{8}} = 1.513 \times 10^{4}$ $$\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{1}{1.513 \times 10^{4}} \approx 66.1\ \mu m}$$ Since r = 1000 μm >> δ = 66.1 μm, the conductor is **thick** compared to skin depth, confirming strong skin effect. AC resistance becomes very large compared to DC. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Skin Effect — Block Diagram of Field Decay ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Conductor Surface x equals 0] -->|Max current Js| B[Depth x equals delta] B -->|Current equals Js divided by e equals 36.8 percent| C[Depth x equals 2 delta] C -->|Current equals 13.5 percent| D[Depth x equals 3 delta] D -->|Current equals 5 percent negligible| E[Conductor Core x equals r] A2[Source AC current] --> A E --> F[Almost zero current at centre] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333 style B fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333 style C fill:#FFC480,stroke:#333 style D fill:#FFB380,stroke:#333 style E fill:#FFA280,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Poynting Vector and Poynting Theorem — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Sources P1[Power Input from Source] P2[Power Dissipated as Heat via sigma E squared] P3[Stored Electric Energy epsilon E squared over 2] P4[Stored Magnetic Energy mu H squared over 2] end subgraph Fields F1[Electric Field E vector] F2[Magnetic Field H vector] end subgraph FlowPath S1[Poynting Vector S equals E cross H] S2[Surface Integral over closed S] S3[Volume Integral over V] end P1 --> S2 F1 --> S1 F2 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> P2 S3 --> P3 S3 --> P4 style S1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000 style P1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style P2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333 style P3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style P4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Polarisation Classification — Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[EM Wave Travelling in plus z direction] --> B{E0x equals E0y?} B -->|No| C{Delta phi equals 0 or pi?} B -->|Yes| D{Delta phi equals plus pi by 2?} C -->|Yes| E[LINEAR POLARISATION
Straight line locus] C -->|No| F[ELLIPTICAL POLARISATION
Tilted ellipse locus] D -->|Yes| G{Handedness?} D -->|No| H[ELLIPTICAL POLARISATION
since E0x equals E0y but phase not 90] G -->|RH| I[RIGHT HAND CIRCULAR] G -->|LH| J[LEFT HAND CIRCULAR] style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style F fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style J fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.4 Power Flow Through a Transmission Line — Conceptual Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Source_End SRC[Source V and I] end subgraph Line_Medium E1[E field between conductors] H1[H field around conductors] S1[S equals E cross H
Points along line
carrying power] end subgraph Load_End LD[Load R consuming power] end SRC --> E1 SRC --> H1 E1 --> S1 H1 --> S1 S1 --> LD style S1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style SRC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style LD fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight from Diagrams:** Notice in the Poynting flow diagram that power travels in the **dielectric region between** the conductors of a transmission line, **not through** the conductors themselves. The conductors merely guide the fields. This is a classic KTU conceptual question. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]** > **Q1.** Define skin effect. What is skin depth? Write the expression for skin depth in a good conductor and explain how it varies with frequency and conductivity. **Model Answer:** **Skin Effect:** When a high-frequency alternating current flows through a conductor, the current density is not uniformly distributed across the cross-section. Instead, it tends to concentrate near the surface of the conductor. This phenomenon is known as the skin effect. **Skin Depth (δ):** It is defined as the depth measured from the surface of the conductor at which the current density decays to **1/e (≈ 36.8%)** of its value at the surface. $$\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}}$$ - δ ∝ 1/√f → as frequency **increases**, skin depth **decreases** (current crowds more tightly to surface). - δ ∝ 1/√σ → as conductivity **increases**, skin depth **decreases**. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]** > **Q2.** Define the Poynting vector. What is its physical significance? Write its mathematical expression for a time-harmonic field. **Model Answer:** The **Poynting Vector** $\vec{S}$ is defined as the cross product of the electric field intensity $\vec{E}$ and the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$. $$\vec{S} = \vec{E} \times \vec{H}\quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$ **Physical Significance:** 1. It represents the **instantaneous power density** (power per unit area) flowing through a surface. 2. Its direction gives the **direction of propagation** of electromagnetic energy. 3. For a time-harmonic field with phasors $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$, the time-average power density is: $$\langle \vec{S} \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\{\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^{*}\}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, 14 Marks]** > **Q.A.** **(a)** State and derive the Poynting theorem. Starting from Maxwell's equations, clearly identify each term in the final equation and state its physical meaning. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** For a lossless transmission line carrying a forward-traveling wave with $E_{0}^{+} = 100\, V/m$ at 1 GHz, calculate the average power density. Given: $\eta_{0} = 377\,\Omega$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the complete derivation. Starting from Maxwell's curl equations: $$\nabla\times\vec{E} = -\mu\dfrac{\partial\vec{H}}{\partial t}, \quad \nabla\times\vec{H} = \sigma\vec{E} + \varepsilon\dfrac{\partial\vec{E}}{\partial t}$$ Dot-multiplying, subtracting, applying vector identity, and integrating over volume V bounded by surface S, we obtain: $$-\oint_{S}(\vec{E}\times\vec{H})\cdot d\vec{S} = \int_{V}\vec{E}\cdot\vec{J}_{c}\,dV + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}\mu H^{2}\right)dV$$ **Term-by-term identification:** | Term | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---| | $-\oint_{S}\vec{S}\cdot d\vec{S}$ | Total EM power entering the volume per second | W | | $\int_{V}\vec{E}\cdot\vec{J}_{c}\,dV$ | Ohmic (heat) power dissipated | W | | $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V}\tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^{2}dV$ | Rate of change of electric energy stored | W | | $\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{V}\tfrac{1}{2}\mu H^{2}dV$ | Rate of change of magnetic energy stored | W | **Physical Statement:** *Power entering a closed volume = Power dissipated as heat + Rate of increase of total stored electromagnetic energy.* **[7 Marks — Full credit for derivation + identification]** --- **Model Solution (b):** Given: $|E_{0}^{+}| = 100$ V/m, $f = 1$ GHz, $\eta_{0} = 377\,\Omega$. For a plane wave in lossless medium, the average Poynting vector magnitude is: $$\langle S \rangle = \dfrac{|E_{0}|^{2}}{2\eta_{0}}$$ $$\langle S \rangle = \dfrac{(100)^{2}}{2 \times 377} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{754}$$ $$\boxed{\langle S \rangle \approx 13.26\ \text{W/m}^2}$$ **[7 Marks]** --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, 14 Marks]** > **Q.B.** **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of skin effect. Derive the expression for skin depth in a good conductor, starting from Maxwell's equations. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** An electromagnetic wave in free space has electric field components: > $E_{x} = 5\cos(\omega t - \beta z)$ V/m and $E_{y} = 5\cos(\omega t - \beta z + \pi/2)$ V/m. > Identify the type of polarisation. Find the Poynting vector. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete derivation. **Physical explanation:** Alternating current produces time-varying magnetic flux inside the conductor. The central strands are linked by the entire flux, inducing a stronger back-EMF than the outer strands. This causes current to crowd near the surface. **Derivation summary:** Starting from $\nabla\times\nabla\times\vec{E} = -j\omega\mu(\nabla\times\vec{H}) = -j\omega\mu\sigma\vec{E}$, leading to: $$\dfrac{d^{2}E_{z}}{dx^{2}} = j\omega\mu\sigma\,E_{z}$$ Solution: $E_{z} = E_{0}\,e^{-\gamma x}$ with $\gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}$. Defining $1/\delta = \sqrt{\omega\mu\sigma/2}$: $$\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f\mu\sigma}}}$$ **[7 Marks — Full credit for derivation]** --- **Model Solution (b):** Given: $$E_{x} = 5\cos(\omega t - \beta z)$$ $$E_{y} = 5\cos\!\left(\omega t - \beta z + \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) = -5\sin(\omega t - \beta z)$$ **Step 1: Compare amplitudes and phases.** - $E_{0x} = 5$ V/m, $E_{0y} = 5$ V/m → **amplitudes are equal**. - Phase difference: $\Delta\phi = \phi_{y} - \phi_{x} = +\pi/2$. Since both conditions for **circular polarisation** are met, the wave is **circularly polarised**. **Step 2: Determine handedness.** At z = 0 and ωt = 0: $E_{x} = 5$, $E_{y} = 0$ → point on +x axis. At z = 0 and ωt = π/2: $E_{x} = 0$, $E_{y} = -5$ → point on −y axis. The rotation goes from +x → −y → −x → +y. Looking *into* +z (i.e., looking toward the source from the receiver side, opposite to propagation), the rotation is **counter-clockwise**. Wait — rechecking: propagation is in +z, so we look *from behind* (from +z looking back). The rotation +x → −y is **clockwise** when viewed against propagation. By the *physics* convention, this is **Left-Hand Circular Polarisation**. **Step 3: Compute the Poynting vector.** For a plane wave in free space: $\eta_{0} = 120\pi \approx 377\,\Omega$. $|H_{0}| = |E_{0}|/\eta_{0} = 5/377 = 0.01326$ A/m. $$\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H}$$ The instantaneous Poynting vector magnitude (since E and H are perpendicular and in phase for circular polarisation): $$|\vec{S}| = E_{x}H_{y} - E_{y}H_{x}$$ But easier: since $|E| = \sqrt{E_{x}^{2}+E_{y}^{2}} = 5\sqrt{2}\cdot|\cos(\omega t - \beta z)|$, we have: $$\langle\vec{S}\rangle = \hat{a}_{z}\,\dfrac{|E_{0x}|^{2} + |E_{0y}|^{2}}{2\eta_{0}} = \hat{a}_{z}\,\dfrac{25+25}{2 \times 377}$$ $$\boxed{\langle\vec{S}\rangle = \hat{a}_{z}\,\dfrac{50}{754} \approx \hat{a}_{z}\, 0.0663\ \text{W/m}^2}$$ **[7 Marks]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. **Sign convention in Poynting theorem:** Many students write $+\oint$ instead of $-\oint$. The minus sign denotes *power flowing INTO* the volume. Examiner will deduct 2 marks. > 2. **Handedness confusion:** KTU strictly tests this. Wrong handedness = 1–2 marks lost. Always state the *convention* you're using (IEEE vs. physics). > 3. **Skin depth units:** Forgetting √π or using ω instead of f loses 1 mark. Always write the final formula in *both* forms. > 4. **Polarisation identification:** A wave with E₀ₓ ≠ E₀ᵧ AND arbitrary phase is *elliptical*, NOT linear. Students wrongly call it "elliptical linear" — the correct term is **elliptical**. > 5. **Boundary assumption:** In Poynting derivation, assuming $\nabla\cdot\vec{E} = 0$ inside a homogeneous conductor without stating it loses a step-mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - [x] **Skin Effect:** AC current crowds toward conductor surface due to self-induced eddy EMFs. - [x] **Skin Depth Formula:** $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ — **memorise in two forms** (with f and with ω). - [x] **Skin Depth Variation:** δ ∝ 1/√f, δ ∝ 1/√μ, δ ∝ 1/√σ. - [x] **At x = δ:** Current density = 36.8% of surface value. **At x = 3δ:** Current density ≈ 5% (often neglected). - [x] **Poynting Vector:** $\vec{S} = \vec{E}\times\vec{H}$ — instantaneous, units W/m². - [x] **Average Poynting Vector:** $\langle\vec{S}\rangle = \frac{1}{2}\text{Re}\{\vec{E}\times\vec{H}^{*}\}$ — for phasors. - [x] **Plane wave in lossless medium:** $\langle\vec{S}\rangle = \hat{a}_{z}|E_{0}|^{2}/(2\eta)$. - [x] **Poynting Theorem:** Power in = Heat dissipated + Rate of change of stored EM energy. - [x] **Energy storage terms:** $w_{e} = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^{2}$ (electric), $w_{m} = \frac{1}{2}\mu H^{2}$ (magnetic). - [x] **Three polarisations:** Linear (Δφ = 0 or π), Circular (equal amplitudes + Δφ = ±π/2), Elliptical (everything else). - [x] **Handedness:** Look *against* direction of propagation. RH = clockwise (physics convention), LH = counter-clockwise. - [x] **KTU High-Yield Constants:** $\eta_{0} = 120\pi \approx 377\,\Omega$, $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. - [x] **Common exam trap:** "Poynting vector points along propagation" — **yes for plane waves in lossless media**, but **not always** in general. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Electromagnetics (PERUT634)**

topics.title: Uniform Lossless Transmission Line - Line Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Uniform Lossless Transmission Line - Line Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
A **Uniform Lossless Transmission Line** is a two-conductor guiding structure with constant cross-sectional geometry along its entire length (uniformity) and with negligible resistive and dielectric losses (lossless). In an ideal lossless line, the series resistance $R$ and shunt conductance $G$ per unit length are both zero ($R = 0$, $G = 0$). Such a line is characterized solely by its **inductance per unit length $L$ (H/m)** and **capacitance per unit length $C$ (F/m)** — these are called the **primary line constants** or **line parameters**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The four primary line constants are:
> - $R$ = Series resistance per unit length (Ω/m)
> - $L$ = Series inductance per unit length (H/m)
> - $G$ = Shunt conductance per unit length (S/m)
> - $C$ = Shunt capacitance per unit length (F/m)
>
> For a **lossless** line: $R = 0$ and $G = 0$, leaving only $L$ and $C$.

### Physical Origin of the Four Line Parameters

Every transmission line, regardless of geometry, exhibits these four distributed effects because of the physical laws of electromagnetism:

| Parameter | Physical Cause | Distribution |
|-----------|----------------|--------------|
| $R$ | Finite conductivity of conductors → Ohmic heating | Series (longitudinal) |
| $L$ | Time-varying magnetic flux linking the conductors (Faraday's Law) | Series (longitudinal) |
| $G$ | Finite conductivity of dielectric between conductors → leakage current | Shunt (transverse) |
| $C$ | Displacement current between conductors separated by dielectric | Shunt (transverse) |

> [!NOTE]
> **$L$ and $C$ are inseparable companions** — they always exist together in any two-conductor geometry because the same geometry that stores electric energy (capacitance) also stores magnetic energy (inductance). This is why even an "ideal" lossless line still has $L \neq 0$ and $C \neq 0$.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Highway of Electromagnetic Waves

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Water Hammer in a Long Pipe:**
> Imagine a very long, thin, frictionless water pipe with an elastic membrane separating the water into tiny segments. If you push a small pulse of pressure at one end:
> - The **water mass** (analogous to $L$) gives the pulse **inertia** → it cannot arrive instantly.
> - The **elastic membrane** (analogous to $C$) gives the pulse **springiness** → it deforms and recovers.
> - Together, $L$ and $C$ determine the **speed of the pulse** in the pipe.
> - In a lossless system, there is **no friction** ($R = 0$) and **no leak** ($G = 0$), so the pulse propagates with no attenuation, only a finite delay.
>
> The transmission line is exactly this: a distributed $L$-$C$ network where energy sloshes back and forth between magnetic (inductor) and electric (capacitor) storage, propagating the signal down the line at a finite velocity.

### Why "Distributed" Parameters?

At low frequencies (DC to a few kHz), the wavelength $\lambda$ is enormous compared to the wire length, so we lump the entire line as a single $R$, $L$, $G$, $C$ in a circuit diagram. At **high frequencies** (RF, microwave — typical of KTU problems), $\lambda$ becomes comparable to or smaller than the line length, and we must treat $L$ and $C$ as **continuously distributed** along the line. The total line of length $\ell$ behaves as an infinite cascade of infinitesimal sections $dx$, each carrying $L\,dx$ in series and $C\,dx$ in shunt.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that $L$ and $C$ are **per unit length** quantities. The total inductance of a line of length $\ell$ is $L_{\text{total}} = L\ell$ (henries), and the total capacitance is $C_{\text{total}} = C\ell$ (farads).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Distributed Model — A Cascade of Infinitesimal L-C Sections

A lossless line of total length $\ell$ is modeled as a continuum of identical sections, each of length $dx$:

```
For length dx:
   Series element:  L·dx  (Henries)
   Shunt element:   C·dx  (Farads)
```

These elements do **not** act independently — they are coupled through the wave equation, leading to propagation phenomena.

### Step-by-Step Conceptual Logic

1. **Signal injection** at $x = 0$ sets up a voltage $V(x,t)$ and current $I(x,t)$ that vary with position and time.
2. **Faraday's Law** applied across the series inductance $L\,dx$ produces a voltage drop:
   $$-\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = L\frac{\partial I}{\partial t}$$
3. **Ampère-Maxwell Law** applied across the shunt capacitance $C\,dx$ produces a current drop:
   $$-\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = C\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$$
4. Coupling the two first-order PDEs gives the **second-order wave equations** (one for $V$, one for $I$) with solutions of the form $e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$ — pure travelling waves with **no attenuation**.
5. For the lossless case, the propagation constant $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ reduces to $\gamma = j\beta$, meaning $\alpha = 0$ (no attenuation) and $\beta \neq 0$ (finite phase shift per metre).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula (Lossless Line) | Unit |
|---|----------|--------|--------------------------|------|
| 1 | Series resistance per unit length | $R$ | $0$ (by lossless definition) | Ω/m |
| 2 | Series inductance per unit length | $L$ | Geometry-dependent (given or derived) | H/m |
| 3 | Shunt conductance per unit length | $G$ | $0$ (by lossless definition) | S/m |
| 4 | Shunt capacitance per unit length | $C$ | Geometry-dependent (given or derived) | F/m |
| 5 | Propagation constant | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = j\beta = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$ | rad/m |
| 6 | Phase constant | $\beta$ | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ | rad/m |
| 7 | Attenuation constant | $\alpha$ | $\alpha = 0$ | Np/m |
| 8 | Characteristic (surge) impedance | $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | Ω |
| 9 | Phase velocity | $u_p$ | $u_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | m/s |
| 10 | Wavelength | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta = u_p/f$ | m |
| 11 | Wave number | $k$ | $k = \beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ | rad/m |
| 12 | Input impedance (line length $\ell$) | $Z_{\text{in}}$ | $Z_{\text{in}} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta\ell)$ (SC) / $-jZ_0\cot(\beta\ell)$ (OC) | Ω |
| 13 | Voltage along line | $V(x)$ | $V(x) = V^+ e^{-j\beta x} + V^- e^{j\beta x}$ | V |
| 14 | Current along line | $I(x)$ | $I(x) = \tfrac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta x} - \tfrac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{j\beta x}$ | A |
| 15 | Phase shift across line length $\ell$ | $\phi$ | $\phi = \beta\ell$ | rad |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX in Tables — Critical Rule:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ and $\mid$ are used (NOT the pipe character `|`) so that markdown tables do not break. This is the official KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE formatting rule.

### Engineering Utility of the Lossless Line Model

- **Designing high-speed digital interconnects** (PCB traces, ribbon cables): the lossless model gives an excellent first-order estimate of signal delay and impedance matching for $R \ll \omega L$ and $G \ll \omega C$.
- **Coaxial cables and striplines in RF/microwave engineering** are engineered to be near-lossless in their operating band.
- **Characteristic impedance matching** between source and load to eliminate reflections (a 50 Ω line matched to a 50 Ω source/load is the universal standard).
- **Designing stub filters, quarter-wave transformers, and resonators** — all rely on the lossless line formulas above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — The Lossless Telegrapher's Equations

Consider an infinitesimal section of length $dx$ of a lossless line. Apply KVL around the series loop and KCL at the shunt node.

**KVL across series inductor:**

The voltage at the left of the section is $V(x,t)$, and at the right is $V(x+dx,t) = V(x,t) + \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial x}dx$. The voltage drop across the inductor $L\,dx$ is $L\,dx\,\dfrac{\partial I}{\partial t}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(x,t) - V(x+dx,t) &= L\,dx\,\frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \\[4pt]
-\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\,dx &= L\,dx\,\frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,-\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = L\,\frac{\partial I}{\partial t}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**KCL at the shunt node:**

The current entering is $I(x,t)$, leaving is $I(x+dx,t)$. The current into the capacitor is $C\,dx\,\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x,t) - I(x+dx,t) &= C\,dx\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} \\[4pt]
-\frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\,dx &= C\,dx\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,-\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = C\,\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation 2 — The Wave Equation for Voltage

Differentiate the first telegrapher equation with respect to $x$, and the second with respect to $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} &= L\,\frac{\partial^2 I}{\partial x\,\partial t} \\[4pt]
-\frac{\partial^2 I}{\partial x\,\partial t} &= C\,\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute the second into the first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} &= L\left(C\,\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}\right) \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} &= LC\,\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - LC\,\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} = 0\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **1-D classical wave equation** with wave speed $u_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$.

### Derivation 3 — Propagation Constant and Phase Constant

For a sinusoidal steady-state source with phasor form $V(x,t) = \text{Re}\{V(x)\,e^{j\omega t}\}$ and $I(x,t) = \text{Re}\{I(x)\,e^{j\omega t}\}$, the time derivatives become multiplications by $j\omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{dV}{dx} &= j\omega L\,I \\[4pt]
-\frac{dI}{dx} &= j\omega C\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating the first with respect to $x$ and substituting the second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} &= j\omega L\,\frac{dI}{dx} = j\omega L\,(j\omega C)\,V \\[4pt]
\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} &= -\omega^2 LC\,V \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} - \gamma^2 V = 0\,,\qquad \gamma^2 = -\omega^2 LC\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the **propagation constant** is:

$$
\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{LC} = j\beta \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boxed{\,\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** For a lossless line, the propagation constant is **purely imaginary** ($\alpha = 0$), which is the most-asked single fact about lossless lines. Always write $\gamma = j\beta$, never $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ with a non-zero $\alpha$.

### Derivation 4 — Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$

The general definition is $Z_0 = V^+/I^+$ (ratio of forward-travelling voltage to forward-travelling current). Substitute the assumed solution $V(x) = V^+ e^{-\gamma x}$ into the first telegrapher equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{d}{dx}\bigl(V^+ e^{-\gamma x}\bigr) &= j\omega L\,I(x) \\[4pt]
\gamma V^+ e^{-\gamma x} &= j\omega L\,I(x) \\[4pt]
I(x) &= \frac{\gamma}{j\omega L}\,V^+ e^{-\gamma x} = \frac{V^+ e^{-\gamma x}}{Z_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now substitute $\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_0 &= \frac{j\omega L}{\gamma} = \frac{j\omega L}{j\omega\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{L}{\sqrt{LC}} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $Z_0$ is the ratio of "magnetic-stored energy per unit length" to "electric-stored energy per unit length". For a lossless line, $Z_0$ is **purely real** — this is why an infinite line (or a matched line) draws a finite real input impedance even though it stores reactive energy.

### Derivation 5 — Phase Velocity and Wavelength

The general wave speed in 1-D is $u_p = \omega/\beta$. Substituting $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_p &= \frac{\omega}{\omega\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,u_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $L$ and $C$ are functions only of the **geometry** and **medium**, $u_p$ is independent of frequency — the line is **non-dispersive**. The wavelength is:

$$
\boxed{\,\lambda = \frac{u_p}{f} = \frac{2\pi}{\beta}\,}
$$

### Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A lossless transmission line has $L = 0.5\,\mu\text{H/m}$ and $C = 200\,\text{pF/m}$. It is operated at $f = 100\,\text{MHz}$. Calculate (i) characteristic impedance, (ii) phase velocity, (iii) phase constant, and (iv) wavelength.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**(i) Characteristic Impedance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_0 &= \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-12}}} \\[4pt]
Z_0 &= \sqrt{2500} = \boxed{50\ \Omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Phase Velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_p &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(0.5 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{-12})}} \\[4pt]
u_p &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-16}}} = \frac{1}{10^{-8}} = \boxed{10^8\ \text{m/s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly $c/3$, slower than free-space light speed — typical for transmission lines because the dielectric slows the wave.

**(iii) Phase Constant:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \omega\sqrt{LC} = (2\pi \times 10^8)\sqrt{10^{-16}} \\[4pt]
\beta &= (2\pi \times 10^8)(10^{-8}) = \boxed{2\pi\ \text{rad/m} \approx 6.283\ \text{rad/m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Wavelength:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi}{2\pi} = \boxed{1\ \text{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Cross-Check:** $u_p = f\lambda \Rightarrow 10^8 = 10^8 \times 1$ ✓. Always verify your answers with this dimensional identity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Distributed Parameter Model of a Lossless Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Vg Zg] --> NODE1
    NODE1(( )) --> L1[L per dx]
    L1 --> NODE2(( ))
    NODE2 --> C1[C per dx]
    C1 --> GND1((GND))
    NODE2 --> L2[L per dx]
    L2 --> NODE3(( ))
    NODE3 --> C2[C per dx]
    C2 --> GND2((GND))
    NODE3 --> Ln[L per dx ... repeated]
    Ln --> NODEN(( ))
    NODEN --> Cn[C per dx]
    Cn --> GNDN((GND))
    NODEN --> LOAD[Load ZL]

    classDef series fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef shunt fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    classDef term fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    class L1,L2,Ln series
    class C1,C2,Cn shunt
    class SRC,LOAD term
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The line is modelled as an infinite ladder of series $L\,dx$ sections interleaved with shunt $C\,dx$ sections to ground. The repeating ladder continues to the load $Z_L$. This is the canonical **distributed parameter model**.

### Diagram 2 — Functional Block Architecture of Lossless Line Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Signal Injection]
        A1[Source Vg at angular frequency omega]
    end

    subgraph PROPAG[Propagation Block]
        B1[Wave equation: d2V/dx2 + beta squared V = 0]
        B2[Solve: Vx = V plus e to power minus j beta x plus V minus e to power plus j beta x]
        B3[Current coupled via Z0]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Behaviour]
        C1[If line matched: pure forward wave, V minus = 0]
        C2[If line open or short: standing wave formed by V plus and V minus]
        C3[Power delivered = V plus squared divided by 2 Z0 per unit length]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3

    classDef inblk fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000000
    classDef problk fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
    classDef outblk fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    class A1 inblk
    class B1,B2,B3 problk
    class C1,C2,C3 outblk
```

### Diagram 3 — Decision Tree for Solving KTU Lossless Line Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Given L and C per unit length?}
    Q2{Frequency also given?}
    Q3{Line length given?}
    Q4{Load condition specified?}

    A1[Compute Z0 = sqrt of L over C]
    A2[Compute beta = omega times sqrt of LC]
    A3[Compute up = 1 over sqrt of LC]
    A4[Compute lambda = 2 pi over beta]
    A5[Compute Zin for SC or OC termination]
    A6[Compute phase shift phi = beta times l]
    A7[Compute standing wave pattern if SC or OC]

    Q1 -->|Yes| A1
    A1 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    Q3 -->|Yes| A6
    Q4 -->|SC| A5
    Q4 -->|OC| A5
    Q4 -->|Matched| C1[Zin = Z0, no reflection]
    A6 --> Q4
    A5 --> A7

    classDef qbox fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000
    classDef abox fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000000
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4 qbox
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7,C1 abox
```

### Diagram 4 — Phasor Snapshot of Forward and Reflected Waves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FW[Forward Wave at x = 0]
        F1[V plus e to power j omega t]
    end
    subgraph BW[Reflected Wave at x = ell]
        B1[V minus e to power j omega t plus j pi]
    end
    subgraph RESULT[Standing Wave Pattern]
        R1[Voltage maxima where two waves add in phase]
        R2[Voltage minima where two waves cancel]
    end
    FW --> R1
    BW --> R1
    FW --> R2
    BW --> R2
    classDef fbox fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef bbox fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    classDef rbox fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    class F1 fbox
    class B1 bbox
    class R1,R2 rbox
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading guide:** In a lossless line, the forward wave $\mathbf{V^+}$ and reflected wave $\mathbf{V^-}$ travel with equal magnitude (no attenuation). Where they add in phase, voltage maxima form; where they cancel, voltage minima (nodes) form. The spacing between successive maxima is exactly $\lambda/2$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the four primary line constants of a transmission line. State the conditions under which the line is termed "lossless".**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four primary line constants per unit length are:

1. **$R$ (Ω/m):** Series resistance of the conductors, representing Ohmic loss.
2. **$L$ (H/m):** Series inductance, representing the magnetic flux linkage per unit current.
3. **$G$ (S/m):** Shunt conductance, representing dielectric leakage loss.
4. **$C$ (F/m):** Shunt capacitance, representing displacement current between conductors.

A line is termed **lossless** when $R = 0$ and $G = 0$. In practice, this condition is approximated when $R \ll \omega L$ and $G \ll \omega C$ (high-frequency operation). (3 marks)

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why does a lossless transmission line have a purely real characteristic impedance?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The characteristic impedance is given by:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}
$$

For a lossless line, $R = 0$ and $G = 0$, so:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega L}{j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \in \mathbb{R}
$$

Since $L$ and $C$ are real positive quantities, $Z_0$ is **purely real**. Physically, no real power is dissipated per wavelength in a lossless line, so the impedance ratio of voltage to current (which carries the reactive energy) becomes purely resistive in nature. (3 marks)

---

### PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Derive the wave equation for voltage on a uniform lossless transmission line. Clearly state the telegrapher's equations used and identify the wave propagation speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lossless line has $L = 0.4\,\mu\text{H/m}$ and $C = 160\,\text{pF/m}$, operating at $f = 50\,\text{MHz}$. Compute (i) $Z_0$, (ii) $\beta$, (iii) $u_p$, and (iv) $\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark): State the lossless telegrapher's equations.**

For a lossless line ($R = 0$, $G = 0$):

$$
-\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = L\frac{\partial I}{\partial t}, \qquad -\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = C\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Combine to form the wave equation.**

Differentiate the first with respect to $x$:

$$
-\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = L\frac{\partial^2 I}{\partial x\,\partial t}
$$

Differentiate the second with respect to $t$:

$$
-\frac{\partial^2 I}{\partial x\,\partial t} = C\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = LC\,\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} - LC\,\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} = 0
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Identify the wave speed.**

Comparing with the standard 1-D wave equation $\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{1}{u_p^2}\,\dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2}$:

$$
u_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): For sinusoidal steady-state, derive the phasor form.**

With $V(x,t) = \text{Re}\{V(x)\,e^{j\omega t}\}$, the wave equation reduces to:

$$
\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} + \omega^2 LC\,V = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{d^2V}{dx^2} + \beta^2 V = 0,\quad \beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}
$$

The general solution is $V(x) = V^+ e^{-j\beta x} + V^- e^{j\beta x}$ — forward and backward travelling waves.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $L = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}$ H/m, $C = 160 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $f = 50 \times 10^6$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Compute $LC$ (1 mark):**

$$
LC = (0.4 \times 10^{-6})(160 \times 10^{-12}) = 64 \times 10^{-18} = 6.4 \times 10^{-17}\ \text{s}^2/\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — (i) Characteristic Impedance (2 marks):**

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{160 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{2500} = \boxed{50\ \Omega}
$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Phase Constant (2 marks):**

$$
\beta = 2\pi f \sqrt{LC} = 2\pi(50 \times 10^6)\sqrt{6.4 \times 10^{-17}}
$$
$$
\beta = 2\pi(50 \times 10^6)(8 \times 10^{-9}) = 2\pi \times 0.4 = \boxed{0.8\pi \approx 2.513\ \text{rad/m}}
$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Phase Velocity (1 mark):**

$$
u_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-9}} = \boxed{1.25 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}}
$$

**Step 5 — (iv) Wavelength (1 mark):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{u_p}{f} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^8}{50 \times 10^6} = \boxed{2.5\ \text{m}}
$$

**Cross-check:** $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta = 2\pi/0.8\pi = 2.5$ m ✓

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain why the line parameters $L$ and $C$ are described as "distributed" and derive the expression for the characteristic impedance of a lossless line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a lossless line with $Z_0 = 75\ \Omega$ and phase velocity $u_p = 2 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$, find $L$ and $C$. If the line is short-circuited at the load, find the input impedance for a line of length $\lambda/4$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Concept and Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (3 marks): Distributed nature.**

At high frequencies, the wavelength of the propagating signal becomes comparable to the physical length of the line. Therefore, voltage and current cannot be assumed uniform along the line; they vary continuously with position $x$ and time $t$. The line is therefore modelled as an infinite chain of infinitesimal sections, each of length $dx$, containing a series inductance $L\,dx$ and a shunt capacitance $C\,dx$. The parameters are "distributed" because they are smeared uniformly along the line, not lumped at a single point.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Use telegrapher's equations.**

In phasor form:

$$
\frac{dV}{dx} = -j\omega L\,I, \qquad \frac{dI}{dx} = -j\omega C\,V
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Derive $Z_0$.**

Combining:

$$
\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = -\omega^2 LC\,V
$$

The general solution is $V(x) = V^+ e^{-j\beta x} + V^- e^{j\beta x}$ with $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$. From the first telegrapher equation:

$$
I(x) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta x} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{j\beta x}, \qquad Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $Z_0 = 75\ \Omega$, $u_p = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Find $L$ and $C$ (4 marks).**

Two equations:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = 75 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad L = 75^2 C = 5625\,C
$$
$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = 2 \times 10^8 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \sqrt{LC} = 5 \times 10^{-9}
$$

Squaring the second: $LC = 25 \times 10^{-18}$.

Substitute $L = 5625\,C$:

$$
5625\,C^2 = 25 \times 10^{-18} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C^2 = 4.444 \times 10^{-21}
$$
$$
C = 6.667 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m} = \boxed{66.67\ \text{pF/m}}
$$
$$
L = 5625 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-11} = \boxed{3.75 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m} = 0.375\ \mu\text{H/m}}
$$

**Step 2 — Input impedance of short-circuited line of length $\lambda/4$ (3 marks).**

For a short-circuited lossless line:

$$
Z_{\text{in}} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta\ell)
$$

With $\ell = \lambda/4$, $\beta\ell = (2\pi/\lambda)(\lambda/4) = \pi/2$, so $\tan(\pi/2) \to \infty$.

$$
Z_{\text{in}} = j(75)(\infty) \to \boxed{\infty\ \Omega \text{ (open circuit)}}
$$

This is the famous **quarter-wave short-circuited stub behaves as an open circuit** — a heavily-tested KTU fact.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mix-up:** $L$ is in **H/m** and $C$ in **F/m** — do not use mH or μF directly inside $\sqrt{LC}$ without conversion. A 1-mark penalty is common for wrong units.
> 2. **Confusing $u_p$ with $c$:** The phase velocity $u_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ is **not** necessarily the speed of light. It equals $c$ only for a line in free space. Always compute it.
> 3. **Forgetting $2\pi$ in $\beta$:** The phase constant is $\beta = 2\pi f\sqrt{LC} = \omega\sqrt{LC}$. Skipping the $2\pi$ leads to wrong wavelength.
> 4. **Writing $Z_0$ as complex:** On a lossless line, $Z_0$ is **purely real**. Writing it as complex loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Input impedance of SC vs OC confusion:** For SC: $Z_{\text{in}} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta\ell)$. For OC: $Z_{\text{in}} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta\ell)$. Many students interchange these — losing up to 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Uniform Lossless Transmission Line Parameters**

- **Four primary constants per unit length:** $R$ (Ω/m), $L$ (H/m), $G$ (S/m), $C$ (F/m).
- **Lossless condition:** $R = 0$ and $G = 0$. Only $L$ and $C$ remain.
- **Distributed model:** line = infinite cascade of $L\,dx$ series + $C\,dx$ shunt sections.
- **Lossless telegrapher's equations (time domain):**
  - $-\partial V/\partial x = L\,\partial I/\partial t$
  - $-\partial I/\partial x = C\,\partial V/\partial t$
- **Wave equation:** $\partial^2 V/\partial x^2 - LC\,\partial^2 V/\partial t^2 = 0$.
- **Phasor form:** $d^2V/dx^2 + \beta^2 V = 0$ with $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$.
- **Propagation constant:** $\gamma = j\beta$ (purely imaginary, $\alpha = 0$).
- **Characteristic impedance:** $\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}}$ — purely real for lossless line.
- **Phase velocity:** $\boxed{u_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}}$ — geometry- and medium-dependent.
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta = u_p/f$.
- **Voltage and current phasors:** $V(x) = V^+ e^{-j\beta x} + V^- e^{+j\beta x}$ and $I(x) = \dfrac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-j\beta x} - \dfrac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+j\beta x}$.
- **Input impedance formulas:**
  - SC: $Z_{\text{in}} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta\ell)$
  - OC: $Z_{\text{in}} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta\ell)$
  - Matched: $Z_{\text{in}} = Z_0$
- **Special lengths to memorize (high-yield KTU):**
  - $\ell = \lambda/4$ SC → $Z_{\text{in}} = \infty$ (open)
  - $\ell = \lambda/4$ OC → $Z_{\text{in}} = 0$ (short)
  - $\ell = \lambda/2$ SC → $Z_{\text{in}} = 0$
  - $\ell = \lambda/2$ OC → $Z_{\text{in}} = \infty$
- **Numerical cross-check identity:** $u_p = f\lambda$ must always hold — use to verify answers.
- **Energy storage ratio:** $L$ relates to magnetic energy per unit length, $C$ to electric energy per unit length. $Z_0$ expresses their ratio.
- **Non-dispersive nature:** Since $L$ and $C$ do not depend on frequency, $u_p$ is independent of $f$ — no pulse spreading in a lossless line.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Line Equations and Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Transmission Line Equations and Solutions ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Transmission Line:** A transmission line is a distributed parameter network used to guide electromagnetic energy or signals from one point to another. It is characterized by distributed **resistance (R)**, **inductance (L)**, **conductance (G)**, and **capacitance (C)** per unit length, such that the voltage and current vary both in time and position along the line. The standard two-wire transmission line is modeled as a cascade of infinitesimal sections, each of length $\Delta z$, comprising the four primary distributed parameters: | Symbol | Parameter | Unit (SI) | |--------|-----------|-----------| | $R$ | Resistance per unit length | $\Omega/\text{m}$ | | $L$ | Inductance per unit length | $\text{H/m}$ | | $G$ | Conductance per unit length | $\text{S/m}$ | | $C$ | Capacitance per unit length | $\text{F/m}$ | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Model:** > Imagine a long garden hose carrying water (the signal). If you push water in at one end, the water does not arrive instantly at the other end — it takes a small but finite time because the hose is elastic (it stretches slightly). The transmission line behaves similarly: > - **L (inductance)** → the *magnetic inertia* resisting sudden changes in current (like the hose's mass resisting sudden flow changes). > - **C (capacitance)** → the *electric storage* between the two conductors (like the elasticity/stretching of the hose walls storing energy). > - **R (resistance)** → the *conductor loss* (like friction inside the hose). > - **G (conductance)** → the *dielectric leakage* between conductors (like small holes letting water seep between inner and outer walls). > > Because these effects are **spread out** along the entire length, we cannot lump them into one resistor and one capacitor — we must treat them as **distributed** quantities. ### 1.3 When Does a Line Behave as a Transmission Line? > [!TIP] > A circuit must be analyzed using transmission line theory when the **physical length of the line is comparable to (or greater than) one-tenth of the wavelength** ($\lambda/10$) of the propagating signal. At $50\;\text{Hz}$ power frequencies, the wavelength is ~6000 km, so transmission line theory is rarely needed. At $1\;\text{GHz}$ microwave frequencies, $\lambda = 30\;\text{cm}$, so even a $3\;\text{cm}$ PCB trace qualifies. ### 1.4 The Generic Two-Conductor Model For analysis, any two-conductor line (parallel-wire, coaxial, microstrip, etc.) is abstracted into a single equivalent line with voltage $V(z,t)$ between the conductors and current $I(z,t)$ flowing along one of them. The reference for voltage is one conductor; the other acts as the return path. ### 1.5 Why We Need Differential Equations Because the line parameters are distributed, the voltage and current change continuously along $z$. A **finite** length $\Delta z$ will produce a **finite** change in $V$ and $I$. As $\Delta z \to 0$, these changes become **differentials**, yielding a pair of coupled first-order PDEs known as the **Telegrapher's Equations**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Building the Differential Model Consider an infinitesimal section of the line of length $\Delta z$ located between positions $z$ and $z + \Delta z$. Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) and Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) to this elementary cell: - The series impedance of the cell is $R\Delta z$ and $L\Delta z$. - The shunt admittance of the cell is $G\Delta z$ and $C\Delta z$. Applying **KVL** around the loop yields the relationship between input and output voltages through the series elements. Applying **KCL** at the central node yields the relationship between input and output currents through the shunt elements. In the limit $\Delta z \to 0$, these become the coupled first-order **Telegrapher's Equations** in the time domain. ### 2.2 Telegrapher's Equations — Time Domain Form > [!IMPORTANT] > **General Time-Domain Telegrapher's Equations:** > $$\frac{\partial v(z,t)}{\partial z} = -R\,i(z,t) - L\,\frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial t}$$ > $$\frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial z} = -G\,v(z,t) - C\,\frac{\partial v(z,t)}{\partial t}$$ **Physical interpretation of each term:** - $-Ri$ : ohmic drop in the conductor. - $-L\,\partial i/\partial t$ : inductive (magnetic) back-EMF. - $-Gv$ : leakage current through the dielectric. - $-C\,\partial v/\partial t$ : displacement (capacitive) current. ### 2.3 Phasor (Frequency-Domain) Form For sinusoidal steady-state excitation of angular frequency $\omega$, the time-domain fields are written as $v(z,t) = \text{Re}\{V(z)\,e^{j\omega t}\}$ and $i(z,t) = \text{Re}\{I(z)\,e^{j\omega t}\}$. The time derivatives $\partial/\partial t$ become $j\omega$. The Telegrapher's equations collapse to a pair of **first-order ODEs in $z$**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Phasor Telegrapher's Equations:** > $$\frac{dV(z)}{dz} = -(R + j\omega L)\,I(z)$$ > $$\frac{dI(z)}{dz} = -(G + j\omega C)\,V(z)$$ ### 2.4 Second-Order Wave Equations Differentiating the first phasor equation with respect to $z$ and substituting the second gives the uncoupled **wave equation for voltage**: $$\frac{d^2 V(z)}{dz^2} = \gamma^2\,V(z)$$ $$\frac{d^2 I(z)}{dz^2} = \gamma^2\,I(z)$$ where $\gamma$ is the **complex propagation constant**: $$\gamma = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)} = \alpha + j\beta$$ ### 2.5 General Solutions of the Wave Equation The general solution to the second-order ODE $d^2V/dz^2 = \gamma^2 V$ is a linear combination of two exponentials representing **forward (+z)** and **backward (-z)** travelling waves: > [!IMPORTANT] > **General Voltage and Current Solutions:** > $$V(z) = V_0^+\,e^{-\gamma z} + V_0^-\,e^{+\gamma z}$$ > $$I(z) = I_0^+\,e^{-\gamma z} + I_0^-\,e^{+\gamma z}$$ > > $$I(z) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0}\,e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0}\,e^{+\gamma z}$$ > > where $V_0^+, V_0^-$ are the complex amplitudes of the forward and backward voltage waves, and $Z_0$ is the **characteristic impedance**. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU ESE** | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Description | |----------|---------|--------------------| | Propagation constant | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$ | $\Omega$ | | Phase velocity | $v_p = \dfrac{\omega}{\beta}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi}{\beta} = \dfrac{v_p}{f}$ | $\text{m}$ | | Attenuation constant | $\alpha = \text{Re}\{\gamma\}$ | $\text{Np/m}$ | | Phase constant | $\beta = \text{Im}\{\gamma\}$ | $\text{rad/m}$ | | Input impedance | $Z_{in}(z) = Z_0\,\dfrac{Z_L + Z_0\tanh(\gamma z)}{Z_0 + Z_L\tanh(\gamma z)}$ | $\Omega$ | | Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma(z) = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}\,e^{2\gamma z}$ | dimensionless | | VSWR | $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1+\vert\Gamma\vert}{1-\vert\Gamma\vert}$ | dimensionless | | Lossless line condition | $R = 0,\;G = 0$ | simplifies $\alpha$ to 0 | | Distortionless condition | $\dfrac{R}{L} = \dfrac{G}{C}$ | makes $\alpha$ freq.-independent | | Lossless $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | purely real | | Lossless $\gamma$ | $\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$ | purely imaginary | | Lossless $v_p$ | $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | equal to speed of light in medium | > [!CAUTION] > **Units pitfall:** $\alpha$ in Np/m is convertible to dB/m by $1\;\text{Np} = 8.686\;\text{dB}$. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Telecommunication trunk lines and undersea cables** — engineers use $Z_0$ to match transmitters/receivers, minimizing reflections that would corrupt data. - **PCB microstrip design at GHz frequencies** — transmission line equations are essential to avoid signal-integrity issues. - **Power transmission lines** — although quasi-static, the same equations give voltage regulation, corona loss and surge impedance loading. - **Radar and RF antenna feed networks** — $\beta$ and $v_p$ govern the electrical length and phase of antenna arrays. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Telegrapher's Equations (Time Domain) **Step 1 — Model the elementary section.** Consider a section of length $\Delta z$ at coordinate $z$. The series elements $R\Delta z$ and $L\Delta z$ are between the input terminals; the shunt elements $G\Delta z$ and $C\Delta z$ are between the two conductors at the centre of the section. **Step 2 — Apply KVL around the loop.** The voltage at the input is $v(z,t)$; at the output it is $v(z+\Delta z, t)$. The current through the series branch is $i(z,t)$. Summing voltage drops around the loop: $$v(z,t) - v(z+\Delta z,t) = R\,\Delta z\,i(z,t) + L\,\Delta z\,\frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial t}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange and take the limit $\Delta z \to 0$.** $$\frac{v(z+\Delta z,t) - v(z,t)}{\Delta z} = -R\,i(z,t) - L\,\frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial t}$$ In the limit $\Delta z \to 0$, the LHS becomes the partial derivative $\partial v/\partial z$: $$\boxed{\frac{\partial v(z,t)}{\partial z} = -R\,i(z,t) - L\,\frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial t}}$$ **Step 4 — Apply KCL at the central node.** The current entering the node is $i(z,t)$; the current leaving is $i(z+\Delta z,t)$. The shunt current consists of conduction $(G\Delta z)\,v$ and displacement $C\Delta z\,\partial v/\partial t$. By KCL: $$i(z,t) - i(z+\Delta z,t) = G\,\Delta z\,v\!\left(z+\tfrac{\Delta z}{2},t\right) + C\,\Delta z\,\frac{\partial v}{\partial t}\!\left(z+\tfrac{\Delta z}{2},t\right)$$ **Step 5 — Take the limit $\Delta z \to 0$.** $$\boxed{\frac{\partial i(z,t)}{\partial z} = -G\,v(z,t) - C\,\frac{\partial v(z,t)}{\partial t}}$$ These two boxed equations are the **time-domain Telegrapher's equations**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Second-Order Wave Equation for $V(z)$ **Step 1 — Start with the phasor form.** Assume sinusoidal steady state with phasors $V(z)$ and $I(z)$: $$\frac{dV}{dz} = -(R + j\omega L)\,I \quad \text{...(1)}$$ $$\frac{dI}{dz} = -(G + j\omega C)\,V \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate (1) with respect to $z$:** $$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} = -(R + j\omega L)\,\frac{dI}{dz}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute (2) into the right-hand side:** $$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} = -(R + j\omega L)\,\bigl[-(G + j\omega C)\,V\bigr]$$ $$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} = (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)\,V$$ **Step 4 — Define the complex propagation constant $\gamma$:** $$\gamma^2 \equiv (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)$$ $$\boxed{\frac{d^2 V(z)}{dz^2} = \gamma^2\,V(z)}$$ A similar derivation (differentiating (2) and substituting (1)) yields $\dfrac{d^2 I(z)}{dz^2} = \gamma^2\,I(z)$. --- ### 3.3 Solution of the Second-Order ODE The characteristic equation of $d^2V/dz^2 - \gamma^2 V = 0$ is $s^2 - \gamma^2 = 0$, with roots $s = \pm\gamma$. The general solution is therefore a linear combination of $e^{-\gamma z}$ and $e^{+\gamma z}$: $$V(z) = V_0^+\,e^{-\gamma z} + V_0^-\,e^{+\gamma z}$$ **Step A — Identify each term physically.** - $V_0^+\,e^{-\gamma z}$: a wave travelling in the **+z direction** (forward wave), whose amplitude decays as $e^{-\alpha z}$ and whose phase advances as $e^{-j\beta z}$. - $V_0^-\,e^{+\gamma z}$: a wave travelling in the **-z direction** (backward/reflected wave). **Step B — Obtain $I(z)$ by substituting $V(z)$ into (1):** $$\frac{dV}{dz} = -\gamma V_0^+\,e^{-\gamma z} + \gamma V_0^-\,e^{+\gamma z}$$ Setting this equal to $-(R+j\omega L)\,I$ and using $Z_0 \equiv \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$ (so that $\gamma/Z_0 = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)}/\sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)} = G+j\omega C$, but we use the cleaner form): $$-(R+j\omega L)\,I = -\gamma V_0^+\,e^{-\gamma z} + \gamma V_0^-\,e^{+\gamma z}$$ $$I = \frac{\gamma}{R+j\omega L}\,V_0^+\,e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{\gamma}{R+j\omega L}\,V_0^-\,e^{+\gamma z} = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0}\,e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0}\,e^{+\gamma z}$$ $$\boxed{I(z) = \frac{V_0^+}{Z_0}\,e^{-\gamma z} - \frac{V_0^-}{Z_0}\,e^{+\gamma z}}$$ The negative sign on the reflected term is crucial: the reflected current wave flows in the **opposite** direction to the reflected voltage wave, even though both are propagating in $-z$. --- ### 3.4 Special Case — Lossless Line ($R=0, G=0$) For $R=G=0$: $$\gamma = \sqrt{(j\omega L)(j\omega C)} = j\omega\sqrt{LC} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \alpha=0,\;\beta=\omega\sqrt{LC}$$ $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega L}{j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}\;\text{(purely real)}$$ **Verification:** $v_p = \omega/\beta = \omega/(\omega\sqrt{LC}) = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, which is the speed of light in the dielectric medium ($c/n$, where $n$ is the refractive index). --- ### 3.5 Special Case — Distortionless Line ($R/L = G/C$) When $RC = LG$, expand $(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)$: $$\begin{aligned} (R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C) &= RG + RC\,j\omega + LG\,j\omega + LC\,(j\omega)^2 \\ &= (RG - \omega^2 LC) + j\omega(RC + LG) \end{aligned}$$ Using $RC = LG$: $$= (RG - \omega^2 LC) + j\omega\,2RC$$ Also using $G = RC/L$, we get $RG = R^2C/L$, so the real part simplifies to: $$\alpha = R\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}} \quad (\text{frequency-independent!})$$ **This is the key result:** with $R/L = G/C$, the attenuation $\alpha$ is **constant for all frequencies** — no phase distortion occurs. The line is "distortionless" even though it is lossy. --- ### 3.6 Boundary Condition Application — Matched, Shorted, Open Lines **Case 1: Matched line ($Z_L = Z_0$).** The reflected wave amplitude $V_0^- = 0$. Only the forward wave exists, and $|V(z)| = |V_0^+|\,e^{-\alpha z}$. **Case 2: Shorted line ($Z_L = 0$, i.e. $V(L) = 0$).** Setting $V(L)=0$: $V_0^+e^{-\gamma L} + V_0^-e^{+\gamma L} = 0 \Rightarrow V_0^- = -V_0^+ e^{-2\gamma L}$. Therefore $V(z) = V_0^+(e^{-\gamma z} - e^{-2\gamma L}e^{+\gamma z})$. **Case 3: Open-circuited line ($Z_L = \infty$, i.e. $I(L) = 0$).** Setting $I(L)=0$: $V_0^+/Z_0\,e^{-\gamma L} - V_0^-/Z_0\,e^{+\gamma L} = 0 \Rightarrow V_0^- = V_0^+ e^{-2\gamma L}$. --- ### 3.7 Standing Wave Ratio and Reflection Coefficient > [!IMPORTANT] > **Voltage Reflection Coefficient at the Load ($z=L$):** > $$\Gamma_L = \frac{V_0^-}{V_0^+}\,e^{2\gamma L} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$ > > At any point $z$ on the line: $\Gamma(z) = \dfrac{Z(z) - Z_0}{Z(z) + Z_0} = \Gamma_L\,e^{-2\gamma(L-z)}$ **Derivation of the input-impedance formula:** Using $V(z) = V_0^+(e^{-\gamma z} + \Gamma(z)\,e^{+\gamma z})$ and the analogous current expression, the ratio yields: $$Z_{in}(z) = \frac{V(z)}{I(z)} = Z_0\,\frac{1 + \Gamma(z)}{1 - \Gamma(z)} = Z_0\,\frac{Z_L + Z_0\tanh\!\bigl(\gamma(L-z)\bigr)}{Z_0 + Z_L\tanh\!\bigl(\gamma(L-z)\bigr)}$$ This is the **most-tested formula** in KTU ESE Module-4 problems. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Distributed-Parameter Model of an Elementary Section > The schematic below represents an infinitesimal slice of a two-wire transmission line. Series $R\Delta z$ and $L\Delta z$ model the conductor resistance and internal/mutual inductance; shunt $G\Delta z$ and $C\Delta z$ model the dielectric leakage and capacitance between the conductors. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["INPUT TERMINALS
v(z,t) i(z,t)"] --> B["SERIES R*dz
conductor loss"] B --> C["SERIES L*dz
magnetic flux linkage"] C --> D["NODE at z+dz"] D --> E["SHUNT G*dz
dielectric leakage"] D --> F["SHUNT C*dz
displacement current"] E --> G["RETURN PATH
ground conductor"] F --> G D --> H["OUTPUT TERMINALS
v(z+dz,t) i(z+dz,t)"] G --> H ``` ### 4.2 Forward/Backward Wave Interaction (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD SRC["Source
Vg, Zg"] --> TX["Transmitter
Matching Network"] TX --> TL["TRANSMISSION LINE
length L, gamma, Z0"] TL --> FW["Forward Wave
V+ e to the power of -gamma z"] TL --> BW["Backward Wave
V- e to the power of +gamma z"] FW --> LOAD["Load ZL"] BW --> REF["Reflection
Gamma L = ZL-Z0 over ZL+Z0"] REF --> TL LOAD --> RX["Receiver / Antenna
matched for max power"] ``` ### 4.3 Telegrapher's Equation Flow (Cause-Effect Chain) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["v variation in z"] --> B["Series R,L drops"] B --> C["KVL yields dV/dz equation"] D["i variation in z"] --> E["Shunt G,C losses"] E --> F["KCL yields dI/dz equation"] C --> G["Coupled 1st-order PDEs"] F --> G G --> H["Differentiate and substitute"] H --> I["2nd-order wave equation"] I --> J["Solution: V+ exp-gamma z + V- exp+gamma z"] ``` ### 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Simplification ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Is R and G both zero?"} -->|Yes| A1["LOSSLESS line: gamma = j*omega*sqrt LC, Z0 = sqrt L/C, alpha = 0"] Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Does R over L equal G over C?"} Q2 -->|Yes| A2["DISTORTIONLESS: alpha = R*sqrt C/L (freq-indep), beta = omega*sqrt LC, Z0 = sqrt L/C"] Q2 -->|No| A3["GENERAL lossy: full expression for gamma and Z0; both alpha and beta depend on frequency"] A1 --> Q3{"Length in wavelengths?"} A2 --> Q3 A3 --> Q3 Q3 -->|less than lambda/10| A4["Lumped-circuit model acceptable"] Q3 -->|greater than lambda/10| A5["Distributed model mandatory; use input-impedance formula"] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define characteristic impedance of a transmission line and write its expression in terms of the primary line constants.* **Model Answer:** The characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of a transmission line is the ratio of the voltage to the current of a single travelling wave on the line (with reflections absent, i.e. the line is infinitely long or matched). It is independent of position and represents the input impedance seen by a wave entering the line. $$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$ For a lossless line, $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ (purely resistive, e.g., $50\;\Omega$ for coaxial cables, $300\;\Omega$ for twin-lead). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State the Telegrapher's equations in phasor form and identify the physical meaning of each term.* **Model Answer:** The phasor Telegrapher's equations are: $$\frac{dV}{dz} = -(R + j\omega L)\,I$$ $$\frac{dI}{dz} = -(G + j\omega C)\,V$$ - $(R+j\omega L)$ is the **series impedance per unit length**: $R$ accounts for ohmic losses in the conductors, $j\omega L$ for the inductive (magnetic) voltage drop. - $(G+j\omega C)$ is the **shunt admittance per unit length**: $G$ accounts for leakage through the dielectric, $j\omega C$ for the displacement (capacitive) current between the conductors. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]* **(a)** Derive the Telegrapher's equations for a two-conductor transmission line in the time domain. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a lossless transmission line, obtain the expressions for (i) phase velocity, (ii) wavelength, and (iii) characteristic impedance. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** See detailed derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. *Key steps to be presented:* 1. Draw the equivalent circuit of an infinitesimal section $\Delta z$ with $R\Delta z$, $L\Delta z$, $G\Delta z$, $C\Delta z$. 2. Apply KVL: $v(z,t) - v(z+\Delta z,t) = R\Delta z\,i + L\Delta z\,\partial i/\partial t$. 3. Apply KCL: $i(z,t) - i(z+\Delta z,t) = G\Delta z\,v + C\Delta z\,\partial v/\partial t$. 4. Take $\Delta z \to 0$ to obtain the two coupled first-order PDEs. *Assumptions:* - Line is uniform (parameters independent of $z$). - Mode of propagation is TEM (transverse electromagnetic). - Line is lossless in the surrounding medium; only conductor and dielectric losses are included. **(b)** Lossless line: $R=0$, $G=0$. - Propagation constant: $\gamma = \sqrt{(j\omega L)(j\omega C)} = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$. Hence $\alpha = 0$ and $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$. - **(i) Phase velocity:** $v_p = \dfrac{\omega}{\beta} = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$. - **(ii) Wavelength:** $\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi}{\beta} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{v_p}{f}$. - **(iii) Characteristic impedance:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega L}{j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}\;$ (purely real). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]* **(a)** Starting from the phasor form of the Telegrapher's equations, derive the second-order wave equation for the voltage along the line. Hence obtain the general solution. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A distortionless transmission line has $R = 2\;\Omega/\text{m}$, $L = 0.5\;\mu\text{H}/\text{m}$, $C = 200\;\text{pF}/\text{m}$. Calculate (i) $G$, (ii) $\alpha$, (iii) $\beta$ at $f = 100\;\text{MHz}$, and (iv) the characteristic impedance. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** See derivations in **Sections 3.2 and 3.3**. *Differential step:* From $\dfrac{dV}{dz} = -(R+j\omega L)I$ and $\dfrac{dI}{dz} = -(G+j\omega C)V$, differentiating the first and substituting the second yields $$\frac{d^2 V}{dz^2} = (R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)\,V = \gamma^2 V$$ *General solution:* $V(z) = V_0^+\,e^{-\gamma z} + V_0^-\,e^{+\gamma z}$, $I(z) = \dfrac{V_0^+}{Z_0}\,e^{-\gamma z} - \dfrac{V_0^-}{Z_0}\,e^{+\gamma z}$. **(b)** For a distortionless line, the condition is $R/L = G/C$. - **(i)** $G = \dfrac{RC}{L} = \dfrac{2 \times 200 \times 10^{-12}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{400 \times 10^{-12}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 8 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{S/m} = 0.8\;\text{mS/m}$. - **(ii)** $\alpha = R\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{200 \times 10^{-12}}{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}} = 2\sqrt{4 \times 10^{-4}} = 2 \times 0.02 = 0.04\;\text{Np/m}$. - **(iii)** $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC} = 2\pi f \sqrt{LC}$. $\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-12}} = \sqrt{10^{-16}} = 10^{-8}$. $\beta = 2\pi \times 100 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-8} = 2\pi \times 10^{0} = 2\pi\;\text{rad/m} \approx 6.283\;\text{rad/m}$. - **(iv)** $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\;\Omega$. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MARK-LOSING MISTAKES — AVOID THESE IN KTU ESE:** > > 1. **Sign error in current solution:** Students frequently write $I(z) = \dfrac{V_0^+}{Z_0}e^{-\gamma z} + \dfrac{V_0^-}{Z_0}e^{+\gamma z}$ with a **plus** sign. The **minus** sign is mandatory because the backward current wave travels in the opposite direction to the backward voltage wave. (Loss: 2 marks) > > 2. **Confusing $\alpha$ in Np/m with dB/m:** When asked to "express attenuation in dB/m", students forget the conversion $1\;\text{Np} = 8.686\;\text{dB}$. (Loss: 1 mark) > > 3. **Writing the input-impedance formula with $z$ instead of $(L-z)$:** The standard form is $Z_{in}(z) = Z_0\dfrac{Z_L + Z_0\tanh(\gamma z_{\text{from load}})}{Z_0 + Z_L\tanh(\gamma z_{\text{from load}})}$ — always measure distance **from the load**, not from the source. (Loss: 2 marks) > > 4. **Forgetting the negative sign in KVL derivation:** The voltage drop equation is $v(z,t) - v(z+\Delta z,t) = ...$, leading to $\partial v/\partial z = -Ri - L\,\partial i/\partial t$. The negative sign is essential and frequently dropped. (Loss: 1 mark) > > 5. **Mixing time-domain and phasor notation:** Once the question says "in phasor form", all $v$ becomes $V$, $\partial/\partial t$ becomes $j\omega$, and only spatial derivatives remain. Examiners deduct 1 mark per mixed variable. > > 6. **Assuming lossless when not stated:** Only set $R=G=0$ if the problem says "lossless". Otherwise, use the full expressions. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Transmission Line Equations and Solutions** > > - The four primary constants are $R, L, G, C$ — all **per unit length**. > - **Telegrapher's equations** (time domain) are the *starting point* of all transmission line analysis. > - **Phasor form** replaces $\partial/\partial t$ with $j\omega$, giving ODEs in $z$. > - The **wave equation** $\dfrac{d^2V}{dz^2} = \gamma^2 V$ has the general solution with two exponentials — forward $e^{-\gamma z}$ and backward $e^{+\gamma z}$. > - $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ — memorize: $\alpha$ = attenuation (Np/m), $\beta$ = phase (rad/m). > - $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{R+j\omega L}{G+j\omega C}}$ — purely real only in lossless/distortionless cases. > - $v_p = \omega/\beta = \lambda f$ — phase velocity. > - $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta$ — wavelength on the line. > - **Lossless line:** $R=G=0 \Rightarrow Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$, $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, $\alpha = 0$. > - **Distortionless line:** $R/L = G/C \Rightarrow \alpha = R\sqrt{C/L}$ is frequency-independent, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$, $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$. > - **Reflection coefficient** at load: $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$. > - **VSWR:** $\rho = \dfrac{1+\vert\Gamma\vert}{1-\vert\Gamma\vert}$;
