VSWR = 1 ⟹ matched line. > - **Input impedance:** $Z_{in} = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + Z_0\tanh(\gamma \ell)}{Z_0 + Z_L\tanh(\gamma \ell)}$ where $\ell$ is measured from the load. > - Use transmission line theory when **physical length** $\geq \lambda/10$. > - For a **short-circuited** line, $Z_{in} = jZ_0\tan(\beta\ell)$. > - For an **open-circuited** line, $Z_{in} = -jZ_0\cot(\beta\ell)$. > - For a **matched** line, $Z_{in} = Z_0$ regardless of length. > - Power carried by a forward wave: $P^+ = \dfrac{\vert V_0^+\vert^2}{2 Z_0}$ (in the matched case, with $\alpha$ neglected). > - **Negative sign** in $I(z)$ expression is critical — examiners test this routinely. > - **Distortionless ≠ lossless** — a distortionless line can be lossy, but it transmits all frequencies with the same attenuation, preserving waveform shape. > - $1\;\text{Np} = 8.686\;\text{dB}$ — know this conversion for attenuation problems.

topics.title: Propagation Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Propagation Constant # 🔷 MODULE 4 — PROPAGATION CONSTANT ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition The **Propagation Constant** (symbolized by the Greek letter **γ**, gamma) is a complex-valued quantity that characterizes the variation in amplitude and phase of an electromagnetic wave as it travels through a given medium. It is the fundamental parameter that governs how an EM wave "behaves" — how much it weakens (attenuates) and how much its phase shifts — as a function of distance along the direction of propagation. Mathematically, it is expressed as: $$\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$$ where: - **α (alpha)** = **Attenuation Constant** (in Nepers per meter, **Np/m**) — measures the exponential decay of the wave amplitude with distance. - **β (beta)** = **Phase Constant** (in radians per meter, **rad/m**) — measures the rate of change of phase with distance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The propagation constant is the *single most important parameter* in Module 4 (Electromagnetic Wave Propagation). It is directly derived from the solution to the Helmholtz wave equation and is the foundation for studying wave behavior in **lossless dielectrics, lossy dielectrics, and good conductors**. ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hiking in Fog" Model Imagine you are **hiking up a foggy mountain trail**: | Electromagnetic Concept | Hiking Analogy | |---|---| | Wave traveling forward | You walking up the mountain | | Attenuation constant (α) | The **fog getting thicker** — the further you walk, the less clearly you (the signal) can be seen | | Phase constant (β) | The **number of steps you take** — each step is a fixed phase increment of your journey | | Lossless medium (σ = 0) | A clear, dry day — fog is zero, you walk with full energy | | Good conductor (σ → ∞) | Extreme fog — you are completely obscured within a tiny distance | The **propagation constant γ** is the *summary of how harsh the environment is* for the wave — combining both the fading (α) and the rhythmic oscillation (β) into one elegant complex number. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Exams:** If you are asked to "find the propagation constant," it almost always means: compute **both α and β** for the given medium parameters (σ, ε, μ, f). ### 📌 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Permeability of free space:** **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** - **Permittivity of free space:** **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** - **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** **η₀ = 377 Ω ≈ 120π Ω** - **Speed of light in vacuum:** **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🔬 Derivation Pathway (from Maxwell to γ) The propagation constant arises naturally when solving the **Helmholtz Wave Equation** for a uniform, linear, isotropic medium with parameters (σ, ε, μ). Here is the step-by-step logical breakdown: ### Step 1 — Start with Maxwell's Equations For a source-free, homogeneous medium, Faraday's and Ampère's laws reduce to: $$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu\vec{H}$$ $$\nabla \times \vec{H} = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{H} \cdot \text{(after substitution)}$$ > Take the curl of Faraday's law and substitute Ampère's law → this is the critical step. ### Step 2 — Form the Helmholtz Equation After taking the curl and eliminating **H**, we obtain the vector wave equation for **E**: $$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \gamma^2 \vec{E}$$ where the **propagation constant γ** emerges as: $$\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)$$ ### Step 3 — Simplify via the Loss Tangent Define the **loss tangent**: $$\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$$ Then: $$\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$$ ### Step 4 — Extract Real and Imaginary Parts $$\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$$ This identity allows α and β to be solved via: $$\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} - 1\right]}$$ $$\beta = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}\right)^2} + 1\right]}$$ ## 🧾 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield — Memorize This!) | # | Parameter / Condition | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | General Propagation Constant | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ | m⁻¹ | | 2 | General γ in terms of medium | $\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)}$ | m⁻¹ | | 3 | **Lossless Dielectric** (σ = 0) | $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ | Np/m, rad/m | | 4 | **Lossy Dielectric** (general) | $\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1+(\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2}-1\right]}$ | Np/m | | 5 | **Lossy Dielectric** (general) | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1+(\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2}+1\right]}$ | rad/m | | 6 | **Good Conductor** (σ ≫ ωε) | $\alpha \approx \beta \approx \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | m⁻¹ | | 7 | Wavelength in medium | $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta$ | m | | 8 | Phase Velocity | $v_p = \omega/\beta$ | m/s | | 9 | Skin Depth (for conductor) | $\delta_s = 1/\alpha$ | m | | 10 | Intrinsic Impedance | $\eta = \sqrt{j\omega\mu/(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)}$ | Ω | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL EXAM RULE:** Notice that in a **lossless medium**, α = 0 → γ is *purely imaginary* (γ = jβ). In a **good conductor**, α = β (they are equal in magnitude). ## 🛠️ Real-World Engineering Utility - **Antenna Design:** Engineers use γ to determine how far a signal can travel before becoming too weak to be received. - **Underwater Acoustics / Submarine Communications:** Knowing α in seawater is essential for selecting frequencies that can penetrate. - **Microwave Oven Design:** The penetration depth (1/α) of microwaves (2.45 GHz) into food must be precisely controlled — this is set by α. - **PCB Microstrip Lines:** At GHz frequencies, the conductor loss (related to α) determines the maximum trace length for a digital signal. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 📐 Derivation 1 — The General Propagation Constant ### Starting Point From Maxwell's curl equations in phasor form for a source-free homogeneous medium: $$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu\vec{H} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$\nabla \times \vec{H} = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ ### Step 1: Take the Curl of Equation (i) Apply the curl operator on both sides: $$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -j\omega\mu \, (\nabla \times \vec{H})$$ ### Step 2: Substitute Equation (ii) into the Right-Hand Side Replace $\nabla \times \vec{H}$ using equation (ii): $$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -j\omega\mu (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Vector Identity Using the identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$ and noting that $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ in a source-free region: $$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}$$ ### Step 4: Rearrange to Form the Helmholtz Equation $$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}$$ ### Step 5: Define the Propagation Constant Comparing with the standard form $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \gamma^2 \vec{E}$, we identify: $$\boxed{\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)}$$ ### Step 6: Solve for γ Taking the square root: $$\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)}$$ ### Step 7: Rearrange to the Standard Form Factor out $j\omega\varepsilon$ from the term in parentheses: $$\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega\mu \cdot j\omega\varepsilon\left(1 + \frac{\sigma}{j\omega\varepsilon}\right)}$$ $$\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\tan\delta}}$$ > This is the **master equation** that KTU questions are almost always built upon. --- ## 📐 Derivation 2 — Specialization: Lossless Dielectric (σ = 0) Setting $\sigma = 0$ in the master equation: $$\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - 0} = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$$ Since γ = α + jβ: $$\alpha + j\beta = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = 0 + j(\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon})$$ Equating real and imaginary parts: $$\boxed{\alpha = 0, \quad \beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}}$$ > **Physical interpretation:** In a lossless dielectric, the wave does not lose any amplitude — it only oscillates (phase shifts) as it propagates. --- ## 📐 Derivation 3 — Specialization: Good Conductor (σ ≫ ωε) For a good conductor, $\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} \gg 1$, so: $$\sqrt{1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}} \approx \sqrt{-j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$$ Since $\sqrt{-j} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(1 - j)$, we get: $$\gamma \approx j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{2\omega\varepsilon}}(1 - j)$$ After simplification: $$\gamma \approx (1 + j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$ Equating real and imaginary parts: $$\alpha \approx \beta \approx \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$ > **Key exam insight:** In a good conductor, **α = β**, and both grow as √f. This is why higher-frequency signals cannot penetrate deep into metals. --- ## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Behavior Across Medium Types | Medium Type | Condition | α | β | Wavelength | Velocity | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Free Space / Lossless** | σ = 0 | 0 | $\omega\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}$ | $\lambda_0$ | c | | **Lossy Dielectric** | σ small, σ ≪ ωε | Small, finite | ≈ $\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ | Slightly reduced | Slightly < c | | **Good Conductor** | σ ≫ ωε | $\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | $\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ | Very small | Very small | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When a question says "free space" or "vacuum," always substitute **σ = 0**, **μ = μ₀**, **ε = ε₀** to get the cleanest answer. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 📊 Diagram 1 — Block Architecture: From Maxwell's Equations to γ This block diagram traces the logical flow from the foundational equations to the final propagation constant. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Maxwell Curl Equations
in Phasor Form] --> B[Take Curl of Faraday Law] B --> C[Substitute Ampere Maxwells Law] C --> D[Apply Vector Identity
curl curl E equals grad div E minus Laplacian E] D --> E{Source Free Region
div E equals zero} E --> F[Helmholtz Wave Equation
Laplacian E equals gamma squared E] F --> G[Identify gamma squared] G --> H[gamma squared equals j omega mu times sigma plus j omega epsilon] H --> I[Simplify using Loss Tangent] I --> J[Master Equation
gamma equals j omega sqrt mu epsilon times sqrt 1 minus j tan delta] J --> K{Material Type} K -->|Lossless sigma equals zero| L1[alpha equals zero, beta equals omega sqrt mu epsilon] K -->|Good Conductor sigma large| L2[alpha equals beta equals sqrt pi f mu sigma] K -->|Lossy Dielectric| L3[Use General alpha beta formulas] classDef startNode fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef processNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef resultNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000 classDef branchNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000 class A startNode class B,C,D,F,G,H,I,J processNode class K branchNode class L1,L2,L3 resultNode ``` ## 📊 Diagram 2 — Behavior of γ in Different Media (Comparative View) ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Free Space sigma=0] -->|Purely Imaginary gamma| N1[gamma equals j beta only
No Attenuation
Wave travels forever] M2[Lossy Dielectric small sigma] -->|Complex gamma| N2[gamma equals alpha plus j beta
Slow amplitude decay
Oscillates in phase] M3[Good Conductor large sigma] -->|Equal alpha and beta| N3[gamma equals 1 plus j times sqrt pi f mu sigma
Rapid exponential decay
Very small skin depth] classDef mediumNode fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000 classDef behaviorNode fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,color:#000 class M1,M2,M3 mediumNode class N1,N2,N3 behaviorNode ``` ## 📊 Diagram 3 — Wave Attenuation Visualization (Conceptual Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Transmitter
E0 amplitude] -->|Distance z equals 0| Q1[Maximum Amplitude] Q1 -->|Distance z increases| Q2[Amplitude decreases as e to power minus alpha z] Q2 -->|At z equals 1 over alpha| Q3[Amplitude equals 0.368 of E0
This is the skin depth] Q3 -->|At z equals 3 over alpha| Q4[Amplitude equals 0.05 of E0
Practically zero] classDef startPt fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF6347,color:#000 classDef endPt fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 class P1 startPt class Q4 endPt ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** These schematics show the *cause-and-effect chain* of how the propagation constant γ = α + jβ governs the spatial behavior of an EM wave — the attenuation (α) causes decay, and the phase constant (β) causes oscillation. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the propagation constant of an electromagnetic wave. What do the real and imaginary parts represent?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **propagation constant γ** is a complex quantity that describes how an electromagnetic wave varies in amplitude and phase as it propagates through a medium. $$\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$$ - **Real part α (Attenuation Constant):** Measured in **Np/m** (Nepers per meter). It represents the **rate of exponential decay** of the wave amplitude with distance. - **Imaginary part β (Phase Constant):** Measured in **rad/m** (radians per meter). It represents the **rate of change of phase** of the wave with distance. **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + α explanation (1M) + β explanation (1M). --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Write the expressions for attenuation constant and phase constant in a lossless dielectric medium.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** In a lossless dielectric medium, the conductivity **σ = 0**, so there is no attenuation. The propagation constant reduces to a purely imaginary quantity: $$\alpha = 0 \quad \text{(no attenuation)}$$ $$\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} \quad \text{(radians per meter)}$$ For free space (μ = μ₀, ε = ε₀), $\beta = \omega/c = 2\pi/\lambda_0$. **Mark Distribution:** Condition σ = 0 stated (1M) + α formula (1M) + β formula (1M). --- ## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students lose 2–3 marks by writing γ in terms of $j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ alone, **forgetting the $\sqrt{1 - j\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)}$ factor**. Always write the **full general expression** unless the medium is specifically lossless. > > **⚠️ Pitfall #2:** Confusing the **units** of α (Np/m) and β (rad/m) — examiners strictly check this. Always state units clearly. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for the propagation constant of a plane wave propagating in a conducting medium. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a lossy dielectric with εᵣ = 4, μᵣ = 1, σ = 10⁻³ S/m, and frequency f = 1 GHz, calculate the attenuation constant, phase constant, and intrinsic impedance. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A(a)** Starting with Maxwell's equations in phasor form: $$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu\vec{H}, \quad \nabla \times \vec{H} = (\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}$$ Taking the curl of Faraday's law: $$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -j\omega\mu(\nabla \times \vec{H}) = -j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}$$ Using the vector identity and $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ (source-free): $$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}$$ $$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)\vec{E}$$ Comparing with the standard Helmholtz equation $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \gamma^2 \vec{E}$: $$\gamma^2 = j\omega\mu(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon) = j\omega\mu\sigma - \omega^2\mu\varepsilon$$ Factoring: $$\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$$ This is the propagation constant. Equating γ = α + jβ, the real and imaginary parts give the standard α and β formulas. **Mark Distribution:** Maxwell equations setup (2M) + Vector identity & substitution (2M) + γ² identification (1M) + Final γ expression (2M). --- ### **Model Solution — Question A(b)** **Given:** - εᵣ = 4, μᵣ = 1 - σ = 10⁻³ S/m - f = 1 GHz = 10⁹ Hz → ω = 2π × 10⁹ rad/s - ε = 4ε₀ = 4 × 8.854 × 10⁻¹² = 3.5416 × 10⁻¹¹ F/m - μ = μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m **Step 1: Compute the loss tangent to determine medium type** $$\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} = \frac{10^{-3}}{2\pi \times 10^9 \times 3.5416 \times 10^{-11}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{2.225 \times 10^{-1}} \approx 4.49 \times 10^{-3}$$ Since σ/(ωε) ≪ 1, this is a **good dielectric** (low-loss). **Step 2: Compute the term inside the bracket** $$\sqrt{1 + (\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2} = \sqrt{1 + (4.49 \times 10^{-3})^2} \approx \sqrt{1 + 2.02 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.00001$$ **Step 3: Compute α** $$\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1+(\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2}-1\right]}$$ $$\alpha = 2\pi \times 10^9 \times \sqrt{\frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3.5416 \times 10^{-11}}{2} \times (1.00001 - 1)}$$ $$\alpha = 2\pi \times 10^9 \times \sqrt{2.225 \times 10^{-17} \times 1.01 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\alpha = 2\pi \times 10^9 \times \sqrt{2.247 \times 10^{-22}} = 2\pi \times 10^9 \times 1.499 \times 10^{-11}$$ $$\boxed{\alpha \approx 0.0942 \text{ Np/m}}$$ **Step 4: Compute β** $$\beta = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu\varepsilon}{2}\left[\sqrt{1+(\sigma/\omega\varepsilon)^2}+1\right]}$$ $$\beta = 2\pi \times 10^9 \times \sqrt{2.225 \times 10^{-17} \times 2.00001}$$ $$\beta = 2\pi \times 10^9 \times \sqrt{4.45 \times 10^{-17}}$$ $$\beta = 2\pi \times 10^9 \times 6.671 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$\boxed{\beta \approx 41.9 \text{ rad/m}}$$ **Step 5: Compute Intrinsic Impedance η** $$\eta = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}$$ The magnitude: $|\eta| = \frac{\sqrt{\omega\mu}}{\sqrt[4]{\sigma^2 + (\omega\varepsilon)^2}}$ $$|\eta| = \frac{\sqrt{2\pi \times 10^9 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}}{\sqrt[4]{10^{-6} + (2.225 \times 10^{-1})^2}} = \frac{\sqrt{7.895 \times 10^{3}}}{(2.225 \times 10^{-1})^{1/2}}$$ $$|\eta| = \frac{88.86}{0.4717} \approx 188.4 \, \Omega$$ $$\boxed{\eta \approx 188.4 \, \Omega}$$ **Mark Distribution:** Loss tangent check (1M) + α computation (2M) + β computation (2M) + η computation (2M). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Derive the expressions for the attenuation constant and phase constant in a **good conductor**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A plane wave at 10 MHz propagates in seawater with σ = 4 S/m, εᵣ = 80, μᵣ = 1. Calculate the skin depth and verify whether seawater behaves as a good conductor at this frequency. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B(a)** Starting from the general propagation constant: $$\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$$ For a **good conductor**, σ/(ωε) ≫ 1, so: $$1 - j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} \approx -j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$$ Therefore: $$\gamma \approx j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{-j\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}}$$ Using $\sqrt{-j} = \frac{1-j}{\sqrt{2}}$: $$\gamma \approx j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{2\omega\varepsilon}}(1 - j)$$ $$\gamma = j(1-j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}$$ Since $j(1-j) = j - j^2 = 1 + j$: $$\gamma \approx (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}} = (1+j)\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$ Equating γ = α + jβ: $$\boxed{\alpha \approx \beta \approx \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$ **Mark Distribution:** Approximation condition stated (1M) + Simplification of sqrt term (2M) + Application of $\sqrt{-j}$ (2M) + Final α = β expression (2M). --- ### **Model Solution — Question B(b)** **Given:** - f = 10 MHz = 10⁷ Hz - σ = 4 S/m, εᵣ = 80, μᵣ = 1 - ε = 80ε₀ = 7.083 × 10⁻¹⁰ F/m - μ = μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m - ω = 2π × 10⁷ rad/s **Step 1: Verify if seawater is a good conductor** $$\frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} = \frac{4}{2\pi \times 10^7 \times 7.083 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{4}{4.45 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 89.9$$ Since σ/(ωε) = 89.9 ≫ 1, **yes, seawater behaves as a good conductor** at 10 MHz. ✓ **Step 2: Compute skin depth δₛ** For a good conductor: $$\delta_s = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$ $$\delta_s = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 10^7 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4}}$$ $$\delta_s = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 \times 4}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{16\pi^2}} = \frac{1}{4\pi}$$ $$\boxed{\delta_s \approx 0.0796 \text{ m} \approx 7.96 \text{ cm}}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** A 10 MHz radio wave can only penetrate about **8 cm** into seawater — this is why submarines use **VLF (3–30 kHz)** or **ELF** bands for communication. **Mark Distribution:** Good conductor verification (2M) + α computation (2M) + Skin depth calculation (2M) + Interpretation (1M). --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **γ = α + jβ** is the **master identity** — α is attenuation (Np/m), β is phase constant (rad/m). - The **general expression** is $\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}\sqrt{1 - j\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)}$ — never forget the second square root. - **Three special cases** you must memorize: - **Lossless (σ = 0):** $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$ - **Good conductor (σ ≫ ωε):** $\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ - **Lossy dielectric (σ small):** Use the full general α and β formulas. - **Wavelength in medium:** $\lambda = 2\pi/\beta$ — *not* $\lambda_0$. Examiners test this trick. - **Phase velocity:** $v_p = \omega/\beta$ — in a conducting medium, $v_p < c$. - **Intrinsic impedance** $\eta$ is *always* complex for lossy media; its magnitude is what matters for power calculations. - **Skin depth** $\delta_s = 1/\alpha$ is the distance at which amplitude falls to **36.8%** (1/e) of its original value. - **Always state units** explicitly — Np/m for α, rad/m for β. - **Decision rule:** Before plugging numbers, compute $\sigma/(\omega\varepsilon)$ — if ≫1 use conductor formula, if ≈1 use general formula, if ≪1 use lossless approximation. - **Free space special case:** β = ω/c, α = 0, η = 377 Ω — this is a frequent 2-mark shortcut question.

topics.title: Characteristic Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Impedance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Characteristic Impedance** (denoted $Z_0$) of a uniform transmission line is defined as the ratio of the voltage phasor to the current phasor for a single traveling wave propagating along the line in the absence of any reflections. Mathematically, it is the square root of the ratio of the line's series impedance per unit length to its shunt admittance per unit length.

For a general (lossy) transmission line with distributed parameters $R$ (Ω/m), $L$ (H/m), $G$ (S/m), and $C$ (F/m):

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$

For a **lossless line** ($R = 0$, $G = 0$), this simplifies to the famous KTU-board-favorite expression:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \quad \text{(purely real, in ohms)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The characteristic impedance is a property of the **transmission line geometry and material**, NOT of the load, source, or signal frequency (for lossless lines). It is independent of line length — a 1-meter coax has the same $Z_0$ as a 100-meter coax of identical construction.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long, wide conveyor belt in a factory. If you push a small box onto it, the belt carries the box smoothly. The belt itself has a "natural pace" — how much push force is needed per unit of mass movement. If you push *exactly* that natural ratio, no energy bounces back.

Characteristic impedance is the **electromagnetic equivalent of that natural pace**. When a source pushes an electromagnetic wave down a transmission line at exactly this ratio of voltage to current, the wave absorbs all the energy and travels forward without any portion being reflected backward.

- A **water hose analogy**: Water flowing in a pipe has a natural pressure-to-flow ratio. Mismatching this ratio at the nozzle causes "back-pressure" (reflections).
- **Acoustic analogy**: Sound traveling in a tube — change of cross-section causes echoes (reflections).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - **Unit of $Z_0$**: Ohms (Ω)
> - **Standard RF coax**: **50 Ω** (instrumentation-grade compromise between power handling and low loss)
> - **Video/broadcast coax**: **75 Ω** (minimum attenuation)
> - **Old TV twin-lead**: **300 Ω**
> - **Free-space impedance**: $Z_0 \approx 376.73031... \approx 377\ \Omega$ (also called the **impedance of free space**, $\eta_0$)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Step-by-Step Physical Logic

1. **The Transmission Line Equations (Telegrapher's Equations)** — A transmission line is modeled as an infinite cascade of infinitesimal series impedance ($dz$) and shunt admittance ($dy$) sections.
2. **Per-Unit-Length Parameters**: $z = R + j\omega L$ and $y = G + j\omega C$ define the line.
3. **Propagation Constant**: $\gamma = \sqrt{zy}$ governs how waves grow/decay and shift phase per meter.
4. **Characteristic Impedance**: $Z_0 = \sqrt{z/y}$ emerges naturally when we solve the wave equation for forward and backward traveling components.
5. **The "Why"**: For an infinitely long line (or one terminated in $Z_0$), there is **no reflection** — the line behaves like a resistor of value $Z_0$ to the source. This is the impedance the source "sees" looking into the line.

### Why It Is Independent of Length

Because $Z_0$ is derived from the **ratio** of per-unit-length quantities, the units (per meter) cancel. Hence, characteristic impedance is a function of:

- **Geometry**: Conductor spacing, cross-section, shape
- **Materials**: Permittivity $\varepsilon$, permeability $\mu$, conductivity $\sigma$ of dielectric and conductors

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** When a load $Z_L = Z_0$, the line is said to be **matched**. Maximum power transfer occurs, no standing waves form, and the line's input impedance equals $Z_0$ regardless of length.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Condition / Notes |
|---------|------------|-------------------|
| General $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$ | Lossy line, full phasor form |
| Lossless $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | $R=0$, $G=0$, purely real |
| Distortionless $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ | $R/L = G/C$ (Heaviside condition) |
| Phase velocity | $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | For lossless line |
| Propagation constant (lossless) | $\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{LC}$ | $\alpha = 0$, $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}$ |
| Input impedance (terminated) | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \cdot (Z_L + j Z_0 \tan\beta\ell)/(Z_0 + j Z_L \tan\beta\ell)$ | Lossless, length $\ell$ |
| Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ | At load |
| VSWR | $\mathrm{VSWR} = (1+\vert\Gamma\vert)/(1-\vert\Gamma\vert)$ | Standing wave ratio |
| Coaxial line $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = (60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})\ln(b/a)$ | $b$ = inner radius of outer conductor, $a$ = outer radius of inner conductor |
| Parallel-wire line $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = (120\pi/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)$ | $D$ = center-to-center spacing, $a$ = wire radius |
| Plane wave in medium | $\eta = \sqrt{j\omega\mu/(\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon)}$ | Intrinsic impedance of medium |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antenna design**: Feed networks must match antenna impedance to line $Z_0$ to deliver maximum radiated power.
- **High-speed digital PCB design**: Stripline/microstrip characteristic impedance (typically **50 Ω** or **90 Ω** differential) must be controlled to prevent signal reflections that corrupt data.
- **Telecommunications**: Long-haul coaxial and twisted-pair networks are designed with standard $Z_0$ values for interchangeability.
- **Microwave engineering**: Waveguide and coax systems rely on precise $Z_0$ matching to avoid reflections and minimize losses.
- **Medical MRI / RF ablation**: Impedance matching between generator and applicator ensures efficient energy delivery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Characteristic Impedance from the Telegrapher's Equations

We begin with a distributed model: a transmission line of infinitesimal length $dz$ has series impedance $z\,dz$ and shunt admittance $y\,dz$, where $z = R + j\omega L$ and $y = G + j\omega C$.

**Step 1: Apply KVL around the series loop.**

The voltage drop across the series element plus the voltage across the next segment equals the input voltage. Taking the limit $dz \to 0$:

$$-\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = z \cdot I = (R + j\omega L)\,I$$

This is the **first telegrapher's equation** in the frequency domain (with $e^{j\omega t}$ assumed).

**Step 2: Apply KCL at the shunt node.**

The current lost through the shunt admittance equals the decrease in line current:

$$-\frac{\partial I}{\partial z} = y \cdot V = (G + j\omega C)\,V$$

**Step 3: Differentiate the first equation with respect to $z$ again.**

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = -z \cdot \frac{\partial I}{\partial z}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the second telegrapher's equation into this.**

Since $\partial I/\partial z = -y V$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} &= -z \cdot (-yV) \\
&= zy \cdot V
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Recognize the wave equation form.**

This is a 1-D Helmholtz equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial z^2} = \gamma^2 V \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma = \sqrt{zy}$$

**Step 6: The general solution is a sum of forward and backward traveling waves.**

$$V(z) = V^+ e^{-\gamma z} + V^- e^{+\gamma z}$$

**Step 7: Substitute back into the first telegrapher's equation to find $I(z)$.**

$$I(z) = -\frac{1}{z}\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} = \frac{\gamma}{z}\bigl(V^+ e^{-\gamma z} - V^- e^{+\gamma z}\bigr)$$

**Step 8: Identify the ratio $V^+/I^+$ for the forward wave (and $V^-/I^-$ for the backward wave).**

For the forward-traveling wave only:

$$\frac{V^+}{I^+} = \frac{z}{\gamma} = \frac{z}{\sqrt{zy}} = \sqrt{\frac{z}{y}}$$

**Step 9: Substitute $z = R + j\omega L$ and $y = G + j\omega C$ to obtain the final result.**

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}}$$

**Step 10: Lossless limit ($R = G = 0$).**

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}}$$

---

### Derivation 2: Characteristic Impedance of a Coaxial Transmission Line

A coaxial line has an inner conductor of radius $a$, outer conductor of inner radius $b$, and dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.

**Step 1: Capacitance per unit length.**

Using Gauss's law for a cylindrical geometry, the potential between conductors is:

$$V = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon}\ln(b/a)$$

So the capacitance per unit length is:

$$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 2: Inductance per unit length.**

The magnetic field between conductors is $B_\phi = \mu_0 I / (2\pi r)$. The flux linkage per unit length is:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)$$

**Step 3: Apply $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_0 &= \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln(b/a) \cdot \frac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi\varepsilon}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon}}\ln(b/a) \\
&= \frac{\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0}}{2\pi\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln(b/a)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Recognize $\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 120\pi\ \Omega = \eta_0$ (free-space impedance).**

$$\boxed{Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)\ \Omega}$$

---

### Derivation 3: Phase Velocity and Its Relation to $Z_0$

For a lossless line:

$$\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC}, \quad v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_0 \cdot v_p &= \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{LC}} \\
&= \frac{1}{C}
\end{aligned}$$

This beautiful identity shows that $1/(C \cdot v_p) = Z_0$, relating capacitance, propagation speed, and characteristic impedance — an elegant KTU-favorite interlink.

---

### Derivation 4: Heaviside Condition (Distortionless Line)

For a lossy line, the attenuation constant is:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\bigl[\sqrt{(R^2 + \omega^2 L^2)(G^2 + \omega^2 C^2)} - (\omega^2 LC - RG)\bigr]}$$

To make $\alpha$ independent of frequency (i.e., no phase distortion), set:

$$\frac{R}{L} = \frac{G}{C} \equiv k$$

Under this condition:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{L(R/L + j\omega)}{C(G/C + j\omega)}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

So even for a *distortionless* lossy line, $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ remains purely real. This is the **Heaviside condition**, foundational to early telegraph engineering.

---

### Derivation 5: Input Impedance with Matched Termination

If a lossless line of length $\ell$ is terminated in $Z_0$, no reflection occurs. The input impedance is then:

$$Z_{in} = \frac{V(0)}{I(0)} = \frac{V^+ + V^-}{V^+/Z_0 - V^-/Z_0}$$

For matched load, $V^- = 0$, so:

$$\boxed{Z_{in} = Z_0 \quad \text{(independent of length!)}}$$

This is the famous "matched line = resistor" property.

---

### Analytical Matrix: Comparison Across Line Types

| Line Type | $C$ (F/m) | $L$ (H/m) | $Z_0$ Expression |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Coaxial | $2\pi\varepsilon/\ln(b/a)$ | $\mu_0 \ln(b/a)/(2\pi)$ | $(60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})\ln(b/a)$ |
| Parallel wires (in air) | $\pi\varepsilon/\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)$ | $\mu_0 \cosh^{-1}(D/2a)/\pi$ | $(120\pi/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)$ |
| Parallel plates (in air) | $\varepsilon w/d$ | $\mu_0 d/w$ | $(d/w)\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 377(d/w)$ |
| Twisted pair (approx.) | $\approx \pi\varepsilon/\ln(D/a)$ | $\approx \mu_0 \ln(D/a)/\pi$ | $\approx (120/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})\ln(D/a)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Block-Level Functional Architecture: Characteristic Impedance in a Transmission Line System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Voltage Source Vg] --> sm[Source Matching Network]
    sm --> lin1[Transmission Line Segment]
    lin1 --> lin2[Distributed L and C Network]
    lin2 --> lin3[Forward Wave Travelling Component]
    lin3 --> term[Load Termination ZL]
    term --> refl[Reflected Wave Component if ZL not equal to Z0]
    refl --> vswr[Standing Wave VSWR Indicator]
    lin2 --> z0calc[Z0 Computation Block]
    z0calc --> math1[L per unit length]
    z0calc --> math2[C per unit length]
    math1 --> ratio[Z0 equals sqrt L over C]
    math2 --> ratio
    ratio --> out[Characteristic Impedance Output Z0]
```

### Conceptual Map: How $Z_0$ Fits Into Transmission Line Theory

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    par[Distributed Parameters R L G C] --> prop[Propagation Constant gamma]
    par --> char[Characteristic Impedance Z0]
    prop --> wav[Wave Equation V and I Solutions]
    char --> wav
    wav --> fwd[Forward Travelling Wave]
    wav --> bwd[Backward Travelling Wave]
    fwd --> match[Matched Condition ZL equals Z0]
    bwd --> refl[Reflection Coefficient Gamma]
    match --> swr[VSWR equals 1 no standing waves]
    refl --> swr
    swr --> pout[Power Delivery to Load]
```

### Decision Tree: Matching Strategy Based on Load

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is ZL equal to Z0?}
    q1 -->|Yes| a[Line is matched: no reflection, no standing waves]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is mismatch small or large?}
    q2 -->|Small| b[Quarter wave transformer matching]
    q2 -->|Large| c[Stub matching using open or short circuited stubs]
    b --> done[Power transfer maximized]
    c --> done
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define characteristic impedance of a transmission line. Write its expression for a lossless line.**

**Model Answer:**

Characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of a transmission line is defined as the ratio of voltage phasor to current phasor of a single traveling wave propagating along an infinitely long line (or one free of reflections).

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$

For a lossless line ($R = 0$, $G = 0$):

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}\ \Omega$$

It is a real quantity, independent of line length, and depends only on the per-unit-length inductance and capacitance of the line.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the characteristic impedance of free space? Why is it significant?**

**Model Answer:**

The characteristic (intrinsic) impedance of free space is:

$$\eta_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} \approx 376.73\ \Omega \approx 377\ \Omega$$

It is the ratio of the electric to magnetic field amplitudes of a plane electromagnetic wave propagating in free space. It is significant because:

- It represents the impedance "seen" by an EM wave traveling in vacuum.
- It determines the **Poynting vector** and power flow per unit area: $S = E^2/\eta_0$.
- It is a fundamental reference for designing antennas and characterizing radiation.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the characteristic impedance of a general transmission line with distributed parameters $R$, $L$, $G$, $C$. Hence obtain the lossless form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A lossless transmission line has $L = 0.4\ \mu\text{H/m}$ and $C = 40\ \text{pF/m}$. Calculate (i) characteristic impedance, (ii) phase velocity, (iii) propagation constant at 500 MHz, (iv) wavelength. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Starting from telegrapher's equations:

$$-\frac{dV}{dz} = (R + j\omega L)I$$
$$-\frac{dI}{dz} = (G + j\omega C)V$$

Differentiating the first and substituting the second yields:

$$\frac{d^2V}{dz^2} = (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)V = \gamma^2 V$$

General solution: $V = V^+ e^{-\gamma z} + V^- e^{+\gamma z}$, with $\gamma = \sqrt{zy}$.

From KVL: $I = -\frac{1}{R+j\omega L}\frac{dV}{dz}$

For forward wave: $Z_0 = V^+/I^+ = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$

For lossless: $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$.

**(b) Numerical Evaluation:**

Given: $L = 0.4 \times 10^{-6}$ H/m, $C = 40 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $f = 500 \times 10^6$ Hz.

**(i) Characteristic impedance:**

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{40 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{10^4} = 100\ \Omega$$

**(ii) Phase velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 \times 10^{-12}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{-17}}}$$

$$v_p = \frac{1}{1.2649 \times 10^{-8.5}} \approx 2 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Propagation constant:**

$$\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC} = 2\pi f \sqrt{LC} = 2\pi \times 500 \times 10^6 \times 1.2649 \times 10^{-8.5}$$

$$\beta \approx 2\pi \times 500 \times 10^6 \times 1.2649 \times 10^{-8.5} = \frac{2\pi f}{v_p} = \frac{2\pi \times 500 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} \approx 15.71\ \text{rad/m}$$

Since lossless: $\gamma = j\beta = j15.71\ \text{rad/m}$.

**(iv) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{\beta} = \frac{2\pi}{15.71} \approx 0.4\ \text{m}$$

Or equivalently: $\lambda = v_p/f = 2\times 10^8 / 5\times 10^8 = 0.4$ m. ✓

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the characteristic impedance of an air-filled coaxial cable with inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$. Hence find $b/a$ for $Z_0 = 75\ \Omega$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a lossless line terminated in a load $Z_L$, derive an expression for the input impedance. What is the value when the line is matched? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** For coaxial geometry (lossless, $\varepsilon_r = 1$):

$$Z_0 = 60 \ln(b/a)$$

For $Z_0 = 75\ \Omega$:

$$75 = 60 \ln(b/a) \Rightarrow \ln(b/a) = 1.25 \Rightarrow b/a = e^{1.25} \approx 3.49$$

**(b)** For a lossless line of length $\ell$, terminated in $Z_L$:

The general wave solution gives:

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \cdot \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta\ell)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta\ell)}$$

When $Z_L = Z_0$:

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \cdot \frac{Z_0 + jZ_0\tan(\beta\ell)}{Z_0 + jZ_0\tan(\beta\ell)} = Z_0$$

Hence the input impedance equals the characteristic impedance regardless of line length.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units are killers**: $L$ in H/m and $C$ in F/m — not mH or μF. Many students lose 1–2 marks by unit mismatch.
> 2. **Lossless ≠ lossless practical**: Don't blindly use $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ unless $R = G = 0$ is explicitly stated or implied.
> 3. **Sign of $\beta\ell$**: Tangent term is $\tan(\beta\ell)$, not $\tan(\beta/\ell)$ — a common transcription error.
> 4. **Coax formula**: The 60 in $Z_0 = 60\ln(b/a)$ comes from $120\pi/(2\pi)$ — keep the constants clean.
> 5. **Match ≠ Open**: A matched load absorbs all power; it does NOT make the line open or short circuited.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **$Z_0$ is a property of the line**, not the load or signal.
- **Lossless form**: $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ — *purely real*, in ohms.
- **General form**: $Z_0 = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)/(G+j\omega C)}$.
- **Free space**: $Z_0 = \eta_0 \approx 377\ \Omega$.
- **Standard coax**: 50 Ω (general RF), 75 Ω (video), 300 Ω (twin-lead TV).
- **Match condition** ($Z_L = Z_0$): zero reflection, no standing waves, $Z_{in} = Z_0$ for any length.
- **Reflection coefficient**: $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$.
- **VSWR**: $(1+\vert\Gamma\vert)/(1-\vert\Gamma\vert)$; unity when matched.
- **Heaviside condition**: $R/L = G/C$ ⇒ distortionless; $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ still.
- **Coax formula**: $Z_0 = (60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})\ln(b/a)$.
- **Parallel-wire formula**: $Z_0 = (120\pi/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)$.
- **Key identity**: $Z_0 \cdot v_p = 1/C$.
- **Phase velocity**: $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ for lossless lines.
- **Input impedance formula** (lossless, general load): $Z_{in} = Z_0 (Z_L + jZ_0\tan\beta\ell)/(Z_0 + jZ_L\tan\beta\ell)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wavelength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wavelength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wavelength (denoted by the Greek letter lambda, $\lambda$)** is formally defined as the spatial period of a periodic electromagnetic wave — that is, the distance over which the wave's shape repeats itself. It is the distance between two consecutive, identical points on the wave (such as crest-to-crest or trough-to-trough) measured along the direction of wave propagation.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERUT634 - Electromagnetics, Module 4)**, wavelength is treated as a fundamental descriptor of an electromagnetic wave, alongside frequency ($f$) and angular frequency ($\omega$). It is a consequence of the one-dimensional wave equation derived directly from Maxwell's equations in free space:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$$

The standard SI unit of wavelength is the **meter (m)**. In practical engineering applications, multiples such as the **millimeter (mm)**, **micrometer ($\mu$m)**, and **nanometer (nm)** are frequently employed across the electromagnetic spectrum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4 of PERUT634, wavelength is introduced as a *derived* quantity from the wave equation solution. The student is expected to know not only its definition, but also its mathematical relationship to frequency, phase velocity, and wave number — all of which appear routinely in Part B (14-mark) examination questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Explanation

Imagine you are standing on the seashore watching ocean waves roll in. If you pick any single wave crest and then walk forward along the beach until you reach the *very next* crest of the following wave, the distance you have walked is precisely **one wavelength**. The ocean is essentially performing a spatial repetition pattern, and $\lambda$ quantifies how "stretched out" that pattern is.

For electromagnetic waves, the same principle applies, but instead of water oscillating up and down, **electric ($\vec{E}$) and magnetic ($\vec{B}$) field vectors oscillate perpendicularly** to each other and to the direction of propagation. Wavelength therefore tells us:

- **How "compact"** an electromagnetic wave is in space (small $\lambda$ → tightly packed oscillations).
- **How much distance** the wave covers during one full temporal cycle of its source.

Think of a violinist: a higher-pitched note (higher frequency) produces a *shorter* wavelength, while a deeper, bassy note produces a *longer* wavelength. For light, this translates directly to color: red light has a longer wavelength (~700 nm) than violet light (~400 nm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Wavelength = "How far does the wave travel before it finishes one complete up-down-up cycle in space?"

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants Referenced

The following constants are central to all wavelength calculations in free space:

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (exact: 299,792,458 m/s)
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$
- **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 376.73 \ \Omega \ (\approx 120\pi \ \Omega)$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Wavelength Exists

Wavelength emerges naturally from the solution of the **electromagnetic wave equation**. When Maxwell's curl equations are combined in free space (where $\vec{J} = 0$ and $\rho = 0$), they yield a second-order partial differential equation whose general solution takes the form of a sinusoidal traveling wave:

$$\vec{E}(z, t) = \vec{E}_0 \cos(\omega t - kz + \phi)$$

Here, $k$ is the **wave number** (in rad/m). The spatial periodicity of this cosine function dictates the wavelength. Because the cosine function repeats every $2\pi$ radians in its argument, the spatial repeat distance satisfies:

$$kz = 2\pi \implies z = \frac{2\pi}{k}$$

Hence, $\lambda = 2\pi / k$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Wave originates** from an oscillating source (e.g., a dipole antenna, a laser cavity, or an atomic electron transition).
2. **Time period ($T$)** is set by the source: the source completes one oscillation every $T$ seconds.
3. **During one time period $T$,** the wave travels a spatial distance equal to exactly **one wavelength $\lambda$** at the speed of light $c$.
4. **The fundamental kinematic relation** therefore becomes: $\lambda = cT$.
5. **Since frequency $f = 1/T$**, we obtain the celebrated relation $c = f\lambda$.
6. **In a material medium** with refractive index $n$, the speed reduces to $v = c/n$, giving $\lambda_{\text{medium}} = \lambda_0 / n$ (where $\lambda_0$ is the free-space wavelength).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Notice that *frequency does not change* when light enters a medium — only the wavelength and speed change. This is a frequent KTU exam trap question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table compiles all high-yield formulas involving wavelength that are essential for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations in PERUT634.

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $\lambda = cT$ | Wavelength from time period | m |
| 2 | $c = f\lambda$ | Fundamental wave relation (free space) | m/s |
| 3 | $\lambda = v/f$ | Wavelength in any medium | m |
| 4 | $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ | Wave number from wavelength | rad/m |
| 5 | $\lambda = 2\pi v / \omega$ | Wavelength from angular frequency | m |
| 6 | $c = 1 / \sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$ | Speed of light from Maxwell's constants | m/s |
| 7 | $\lambda_{\text{med}} = \lambda_0 / n$ | Wavelength inside medium of refractive index $n$ | m |
| 8 | $v_p = \omega / k$ | Phase velocity definition | m/s |
| 9 | $\lambda = c / f$ | Wavelength from frequency | m |
| 10 | $T = 1/f$ | Time period from frequency | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wavelength dictates *antenna sizing* (a half-wave dipole antenna has length $\lambda/2$), *resonant cavity dimensions*, *diffraction grating spacing*, and *optical fiber mode cutoffs*. It is one of the most operationally important parameters in telecommunications and RF engineering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Relevance

- **Antenna Design:** A dipole antenna is most efficient when its physical length equals $\lambda/2$. For a 1 GHz signal, $\lambda \approx 30$ cm, so the antenna is ~15 cm long.
- **Optical Fiber Communication:** Signals at 1550 nm (infrared) are used because their wavelength experiences minimum attenuation in silica fibers.
- **Microwave Ovens:** Operate at ~2.45 GHz, corresponding to $\lambda \approx 12.2$ cm — chosen to efficiently excite water molecule rotations.
- **Medical Imaging:** MRI, X-ray, and ultrasound all operate at vastly different wavelengths, each chosen for tissue penetration characteristics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Relation $c = f\lambda$

We begin with the standard form of a one-dimensional sinusoidal electromagnetic wave propagating in the $+z$ direction:

$$\vec{E}(z, t) = \vec{E}_0 \cos(kz - \omega t)$$

**Step 1:** Identify the spatial periodicity condition. The argument of the cosine function is the **phase** of the wave:

$$\phi(z, t) = kz - \omega t$$

**Step 2:** A full spatial cycle is completed when the phase changes by exactly $2\pi$ radians (one complete revolution on the unit circle). At a fixed instant $t$, the spatial separation between two identical phase values is:

$$\Delta \phi = k \cdot \Delta z = 2\pi$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $\Delta z$:

$$\Delta z = \frac{2\pi}{k} = \lambda$$

This is the formal definition of wavelength — the spatial repeat distance. Therefore:

$$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{k}$$

**Step 4:** Similarly, for a fixed position $z$, the temporal period $T$ corresponds to a phase change of $2\pi$:

$$\omega \cdot T = 2\pi \implies T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

**Step 5:** The wave travels a distance $cT$ during one period $T$. This distance must equal one wavelength:

$$cT = \lambda$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $T = 1/f$:

$$c \cdot \frac{1}{f} = \lambda$$

$$\boxed{c = f\lambda}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Speed of Light from Maxwell's Constants

Starting from Maxwell's curl equations in free space:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 1:** Take the curl of the first equation:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 2:** Apply the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$. In free space, $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ (no charges), so:

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the second curl equation:

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 5:** Compare this with the standard wave equation:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \frac{1}{v^2} \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 6:** Equating the coefficients:

$$\frac{1}{v^2} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0$$

$$\boxed{v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} = c \approx 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wavelength Inside a Dielectric Medium

When an EM wave enters a medium with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ and relative permeability $\mu_r$, both the speed and wavelength change.

**Step 1:** Speed in medium:

$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \mu_r \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}} = \frac{c}{n}$$

where $n = \sqrt{\mu_r \varepsilon_r}$ is the **refractive index** (for non-magnetic materials, $\mu_r \approx 1$, so $n \approx \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$).

**Step 2:** Frequency remains unchanged (set by source):

$$f_{\text{med}} = f_0$$

**Step 3:** Apply $v = f\lambda$ in the medium:

$$\lambda_{\text{med}} = \frac{v}{f_0} = \frac{c/n}{f_0} = \frac{\lambda_0}{n}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\text{med}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{n}}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem (KTU Typical):** A radar system operates at 10 GHz. Calculate (a) the wavelength in free space, (b) the wavelength inside a glass medium of refractive index 1.5, and (c) the wave number.

**Given:** $f = 10 \text{ GHz} = 10 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, $n = 1.5$.

**Solution (a):** Free-space wavelength:

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 3 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 3 \text{ cm}$$

**Solution (b):** Wavelength in glass:

$$\lambda_{\text{med}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{n} = \frac{3 \text{ cm}}{1.5} = 2 \text{ cm}$$

**Solution (c):** Wave number:

$$k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda_0} = \frac{2\pi}{0.03} = 209.44 \text{ rad/m}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Quantity | Free Space | In Medium (ref. index $n$) | Relationship |
|----------|------------|---------------------------|--------------|
| Speed ($v$) | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | $v = c/n$ | Reduced |
| Frequency ($f$) | $f_0$ | $f_0$ | **Unchanged** |
| Wavelength ($\lambda$) | $\lambda_0$ | $\lambda_0/n$ | Reduced |
| Wave number ($k$) | $2\pi/\lambda_0$ | $nk$ | Increased |
| Angular freq. ($\omega$) | $2\pi f_0$ | $2\pi f_0$ | **Unchanged** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define wavelength. State the relationship between wavelength, frequency, and speed of an electromagnetic wave.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Wavelength ($\lambda$) is the spatial distance over which an electromagnetic wave completes one full cycle. It is measured as the distance between two successive crests (or any two identical points on consecutive cycles) of the wave at a fixed instant of time.

**Relationship (1.5 marks):** The fundamental relation connecting wavelength, frequency ($f$), and speed of light ($c$ or $v$) is:

$$c = f \lambda \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$

In a medium of refractive index $n$, the wavelength reduces to $\lambda_{\text{med}} = \lambda_0 / n$, while frequency remains unchanged.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between wavelength in free space and wavelength in a dielectric medium. What remains unchanged when a wave enters a medium?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In free space, wavelength $\lambda_0 = c / f$, where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. In a dielectric medium of refractive index $n$, the wave slows to $v = c/n$, and the wavelength becomes:

$$\lambda_{\text{med}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{n}$$

The **frequency $f$ remains unchanged** when an electromagnetic wave passes from one medium to another. Only the speed and wavelength change; frequency is set by the source and is invariant across media.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Starting from Maxwell's equations in free space, derive the electromagnetic wave equation. Hence obtain the expression for the speed of light in vacuum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microwave signal has a frequency of 2.4 GHz. Calculate:
  - (i) Its wavelength in free space
  - (ii) Its wavelength inside a dielectric material of refractive index 1.45
  - (iii) The wave number in the dielectric **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Maxwell's curl equations in free space (no charges, no currents):

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Take the curl of equation (1):

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{B}) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

Apply vector identity (and use $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ in free space):

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}\right)$$

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

This is the **electromagnetic wave equation**. Comparing with the standard wave equation $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = (1/v^2)\partial^2 \vec{E}/\partial t^2$:

$$\frac{1}{v^2} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0$$

$$v = c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(8.854 \times 10^{-12})}}$$

$$\boxed{c \approx 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $f = 2.4 \text{ GHz} = 2.4 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$, $n = 1.45$, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(i) Wavelength in free space (2.5 marks):**

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ cm}$$

**(ii) Wavelength in dielectric (2.5 marks):**

$$\lambda_{\text{med}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{n} = \frac{0.125}{1.45} = 0.0862 \text{ m} \approx 8.62 \text{ cm}$$

**(iii) Wave number in dielectric (2 marks):**

$$k_{\text{med}} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda_{\text{med}}} = \frac{2\pi}{0.0862} \approx 72.89 \text{ rad/m}$$

Alternatively: $k_{\text{med}} = n k_0 = 1.45 \times (2\pi/0.125) = 1.45 \times 50.27 \approx 72.89$ rad/m ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** What is wavelength? Derive the relation $c = f\lambda$ for an electromagnetic wave. Explain why frequency does not change when light enters a denser medium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An optical fiber communication system uses light of frequency $1.94 \times 10^{14}$ Hz propagating through a silica fiber of refractive index 1.46. Determine:
  - (i) The free-space wavelength
  - (ii) The wavelength inside the fiber
  - (iii) The wave number inside the fiber
  - (iv) The frequency of the wave inside the fiber **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Conceptual Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Wavelength $\lambda$ is the distance between two successive points in phase on an EM wave (e.g., crest-to-crest) at a given instant of time.

**Derivation of $c = f\lambda$ (3.5 marks):** For a wave $\vec{E}(z,t) = E_0 \cos(kz - \omega t)$, the spatial period is $\lambda = 2\pi/k$ (one full cycle corresponds to phase change of $2\pi$). The temporal period is $T = 2\pi/\omega$. During time $T$, the wave travels one wavelength: $cT = \lambda$. Since $f = 1/T$, we get:

$$c = f\lambda$$

**Why frequency is unchanged (2 marks):** Frequency is an intrinsic property of the source — the source oscillates at a fixed rate determined by its atomic or electronic transitions. When a wave crosses a boundary, the boundary cannot alter the rate at which the source emits wave crests; only the local propagation speed changes (due to altered $\mu$ and $\varepsilon$), which forces $\lambda$ to adjust accordingly via $\lambda = v/f$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $f = 1.94 \times 10^{14}$ Hz, $n = 1.46$, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**(i) Free-space wavelength (2 marks):**

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.94 \times 10^{14}} = 1.546 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1546 \text{ nm}$$

**(ii) Wavelength inside fiber (2 marks):**

$$\lambda_{\text{med}} = \frac{\lambda_0}{n} = \frac{1546}{1.46} \approx 1059 \text{ nm}$$

**(iii) Wave number inside fiber (1.5 marks):**

$$k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda_{\text{med}}} = \frac{2\pi}{1.059 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 5.93 \times 10^6 \text{ rad/m}$$

**(iv) Frequency inside fiber (1.5 marks):**

$$f_{\text{med}} = f_0 = 1.94 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz} \quad \text{(unchanged)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write $\lambda_{\text{med}} = \lambda_0 \times n$ instead of $\lambda_{\text{med}} = \lambda_0 / n$. This is an immediate **2-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert GHz to Hz before substituting into $\lambda = c/f$. Loss of 1 mark.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In derivations, failing to explicitly state $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ before applying the vector identity. Examiner expects this step. Loss of 1 mark.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Mixing up units — using cm instead of m in wave number calculations. Always convert to SI base units first.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Claiming frequency changes inside a medium. **This is wrong** — frequency is an invariant across media.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wavelength $\lambda$** is the spatial period of an EM wave; measured in meters.
- **The fundamental relation:** $c = f\lambda$ (free space) or $v = f\lambda$ (in any medium).
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — derived from Maxwell's equations.
- **Wave number:** $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ (in rad/m).
- **In a medium of refractive index $n$:** $\lambda_{\text{med}} = \lambda_0 / n$, $v = c/n$, but **frequency is unchanged**.
- **Angular frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f$ — also invariant across media.
- **For a half-wave dipole antenna:** physical length = $\lambda/2$ — this is why KTU regularly asks antenna-design questions.
- **The full EM spectrum spans wavelengths** from $\sim 10^{-12}$ m (gamma rays) to $\sim 10^4$ m (radio waves).
- **Always convert** all quantities to SI base units (Hz, m, s) before substituting into any formula.
- **Always remember** the derivation chain: Maxwell's equations → wave equation → $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$ → $c = f\lambda$.
- **Three quantities that change across a boundary:** speed, wavelength, wave number.
- **Two quantities that DO NOT change:** frequency, angular frequency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Velocity and Group Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Phase Velocity and Group Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌊 PHASE VELOCITY AND GROUP VELOCITY — KTU PREMIER STUDY NOTES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Velocity ($v_p$)** is defined as the velocity with which a point of **constant phase** (e.g., a wave crest, trough, or zero-crossing) on a monochromatic sinusoidal wave propagates through a given medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Group Velocity ($v_g$)** is defined as the velocity with which the **envelope** (or the overall shape) of a wave packet — formed by the superposition of two or more nearly-equal-frequency sinusoidal waves — propagates through a medium. In non-absorbing media, $v_g$ represents the velocity of **energy and information transport**.

Mathematically:

$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{k} \quad ; \quad v_g = \frac{d\omega}{dk}$$

where $\omega$ is the angular frequency (in **rad/s**) and $k$ is the angular wavenumber (in **rad/m**).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Duck Analogy" 🦆

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a line of ducks swimming across a calm pond.**

| Concept | Duck Analogy | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase Velocity ($v_p$)** | The speed of a *single duck* moving forward | Speed of an individual wave crest of one pure frequency |
| **Group Velocity ($v_g$)** | The speed of the *whole group formation* as a shape | Speed of the envelope (amplitude pattern) of a wave packet |
| **Wave Packet** | The flock swimming together | Superposition of many frequencies forming a localized pulse |

- If **all ducks paddle at the same pace** → **Non-dispersive medium** → $v_p = v_g$ (the formation and individual duck move together).
- If **ducks of different sizes paddle at different speeds** → **Dispersive medium** → $v_p \neq v_g$ (the formation deforms and spreads out as it travels).

---

### 1.3 The Physical Constants & Units You Must Memorize

> [!NOTE]
> - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
> - In a medium of refractive index $n$: $v_p = c/n$
> - For free space: $v_p = v_g = c$
> - Angular frequency–wavenumber relation in vacuum: $\omega = c \cdot k$

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Electromagnetics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **fiber-optic communication, radar systems, plasma physics, and ionospheric wave propagation**, the distinction between $v_p$ and $v_g$ is critical. Phase velocity can **exceed** $c$ in some media (e.g., waveguides, dispersive dielectrics), but it carries **no energy or information**. Only the **group velocity** is bounded by $c$ (in non-absorbing media) and represents the **physical signal speed** — making it the parameter of engineering importance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Setting Up the Mathematics of a Travelling Wave

Consider a monochromatic plane wave travelling in the $+z$ direction:

$$E(z,t) = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz + \phi)$$

- The **argument** $(\omega t - kz + \phi)$ is called the **phase** of the wave.
- For a *fixed* point of constant phase, $\omega t - kz = \text{const}$.

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{d}{dt}(\omega t - kz) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \omega - k\frac{dz}{dt} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{v_p = \frac{dz}{dt} = \frac{\omega}{k}}$$

---

## 2.2 The Concept of a Wave Packet (Why We Need Group Velocity)

A **pure single-frequency wave** exists everywhere in space and forever in time — it is **non-localized** and carries no information. To transmit a real signal (a pulse), we must superpose many frequencies. This superposition is called a **wave packet**.

Consider **two** sinusoidal waves of equal amplitude $E_0$ with frequencies $\omega_1, \omega_2$ and wavenumbers $k_1, k_2$, both very close to a central $\omega$ and $k$:

$$E(z,t) = E_0 \cos[(\omega - \Delta\omega)t - (k - \Delta k)z] + E_0 \cos[(\omega + \Delta\omega)t - (k + \Delta k)z]$$

Applying the trigonometric identity $\cos A + \cos B = 2 \cos\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)\cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)$:

$$E(z,t) = \underbrace{2E_0 \cos[(\Delta\omega)\,t - (\Delta k)\,z]}_{\text{Envelope (slowly varying)}} \cdot \underbrace{\cos(\omega t - kz)}_{\text{Carrier (rapidly varying)}}$$

- The **carrier** travels at $v_p = \omega/k$ (phase velocity).
- The **envelope** travels at $v_g = \Delta\omega / \Delta k$.

In the limit of an **infinitesimally narrow** frequency band ($\Delta\omega \to 0$):

$$\boxed{v_g = \frac{d\omega}{dk}}$$

This is the **group velocity** — the speed of the amplitude envelope.

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU question on this topic uses one or more of these identities.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_p = \omega / k$ | Definition of phase velocity |
| 2 | $v_g = d\omega / dk$ | Definition of group velocity |
| 3 | $v_g = v_p + k \dfrac{dv_p}{dk}$ | Group velocity via phase velocity |
| 4 | $v_g = v_p - \lambda \dfrac{dv_p}{d\lambda}$ | Group velocity in terms of wavelength |
| 5 | $v_p = c / n$ | Phase velocity in a medium |
| 6 | $v_g = \dfrac{c}{n + \omega (dn/d\omega)}$ | Group velocity via refractive index (frequency form) |
| 7 | $v_g = \dfrac{c}{n - \lambda (dn/d\lambda)}$ | Group velocity via refractive index (wavelength form) |
| 8 | $v_p \cdot v_g = \dfrac{c^2}{n^2 + n\omega (dn/d\omega)}$ | Product of $v_p$ and $v_g$ |
| 9 | $v_p = v_g$ | **Non-dispersive medium** (free space, $n = \text{const}$) |
| 10 | $v_p \neq v_g$ | **Dispersive medium** (e.g., ionosphere, optical fiber) |

---

## 2.4 The Crucial Engineering Distinction: $v_p$ vs $v_g$

| Property | Phase Velocity $v_p$ | Group Velocity $v_g$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Carries** | Constant-phase points (crests) | Energy & information |
| **Can exceed $c$?** | **Yes** (in dispersive media) | **No** (in non-absorbing media) |
| **Detected by** | A stationary phase-locked receiver | A detector that follows the pulse envelope |
| **Engineering use** | Waveguide mode analysis, plasma waves | Optical fiber bandwidth, signal delay |
| **In free space** | $v_p = c$ | $v_g = c$ |

---

## 2.5 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why $v_p$ can exceed $c$:** Phase is a mathematical construct — the *pattern* of crests is not a physical object, so nothing physically travels at $v_p$.
- **Why $v_g$ is bounded by $c$:** The envelope represents a real, localized bundle of electromagnetic energy. Relativity forbids energy transport faster than $c$ in vacuum.
- **How to identify dispersion:** If $n$ depends on frequency ($\omega$) or wavelength ($\lambda$), the medium is dispersive. If $n$ is constant, the medium is non-dispersive.
- **Practical engineering use:** In silica optical fibers, $n$ decreases with wavelength in the communication band → normal dispersion → $v_g < v_p$ → causes **pulse broadening** and limits data rate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Phase Velocity from First Principles

**Starting wave equation (1D plane wave):**

$$E(z,t) = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz)$$

**Step 1:** Fix the phase $\phi$ to a constant value (e.g., a particular crest):

$$\phi = \omega t - kz = \text{constant}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to time $t$ (this gives the velocity of the constant-phase point):

$$\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \omega - k \cdot \frac{dz}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the velocity $dz/dt$:

$$k \cdot \frac{dz}{dt} = \omega \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{dz}{dt} = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{v_p = \frac{\omega}{k}}$$

Since $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $\omega = 2\pi f$, this also equals $f\lambda$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Group Velocity from Two Superposed Waves

**Starting point** — two waves with equal amplitude $E_0$:

$$E_1 = E_0 \cos[(\omega_1 t - k_1 z)] \quad ; \quad E_2 = E_0 \cos[(\omega_2 t - k_2 z)]$$

**Define average and difference quantities:**

$$\omega = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}, \quad \Delta\omega = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{2}$$

$$k = \frac{k_1 + k_2}{2}, \quad \Delta k = \frac{k_2 - k_1}{2}$$

So $\omega_1 = \omega - \Delta\omega$, $\omega_2 = \omega + \Delta\omega$, etc.

**Superpose the waves** $E = E_1 + E_2$:

$$E = E_0 \{\cos[(\omega - \Delta\omega)t - (k - \Delta k)z] + \cos[(\omega + \Delta\omega)t - (k + \Delta k)z]\}$$

**Apply the trigonometric sum-to-product identity** $\cos A + \cos B = 2\cos\left(\tfrac{A-B}{2}\right)\cos\left(\tfrac{A+B}{2}\right)$:

$$E = 2E_0 \cos[(\Delta\omega)\,t - (\Delta k)\,z] \cdot \cos(\omega t - kz)$$

**Interpretation:**
- The factor $\cos(\omega t - kz)$ is the **rapidly oscillating carrier** — travels at $v_p = \omega/k$.
- The factor $2E_0 \cos[(\Delta\omega)t - (\Delta k)z]$ is the **slowly varying envelope** — travels at $\Delta\omega/\Delta k$.

**Step 4: Take the limit as $\Delta\omega \to 0$ (infinitesimally narrow wave packet):**

$$v_g = \lim_{\Delta\omega \to 0}\frac{\Delta\omega}{\Delta k} = \frac{d\omega}{dk}$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{v_g = \frac{d\omega}{dk}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Relationship $v_g = v_p + k \cdot (dv_p/dk)$

**Start with the definition:**

$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{k} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega = v_p \cdot k$$

**Differentiate both sides with respect to $k$:**

$$\frac{d\omega}{dk} = \frac{d(v_p \cdot k)}{dk}$$

**Apply the product rule** on the right-hand side:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dk} = v_p \cdot \frac{dk}{dk} + k \cdot \frac{dv_p}{dk} = v_p + k \cdot \frac{dv_p}{dk}$$

**Recognize the left-hand side as $v_g$:**

$$\boxed{v_g = v_p + k \frac{dv_p}{dk}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Relationship $v_g = v_p - \lambda \cdot (dv_p/d\lambda)$

**Step 1:** From $k = 2\pi/\lambda$, differentiate $k$ with respect to $\lambda$:

$$\frac{dk}{d\lambda} = -\frac{2\pi}{\lambda^2}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the chain rule to $dv_p/dk$:

$$\frac{dv_p}{dk} = \frac{dv_p}{d\lambda} \cdot \frac{d\lambda}{dk} = \frac{dv_p}{d\lambda} \cdot \left(-\frac{\lambda^2}{2\pi}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the previous result $v_g = v_p + k(dv_p/dk)$:

$$v_g = v_p + \left(\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\right) \cdot \frac{dv_p}{d\lambda} \cdot \left(-\frac{\lambda^2}{2\pi}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify — the $2\pi$ cancels and one $\lambda$ cancels:

$$v_g = v_p - \lambda \cdot \frac{dv_p}{d\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{v_g = v_p - \lambda \frac{dv_p}{d\lambda}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the form most often used in **optical fiber** and **spectroscopy** problems where the data is given as a function of wavelength.

---

## 3.5 Derivation #5 — Group Velocity in Terms of Refractive Index $n(\omega)$

**Given:** $v_p = c/n(\omega)$, so $k = \omega n(\omega)/c$.

**Step 1:** Compute $dk/d\omega$:

$$\frac{dk}{d\omega} = \frac{d}{d\omega}\left[\frac{\omega n(\omega)}{c}\right] = \frac{1}{c}\left[n(\omega) + \omega \frac{dn}{d\omega}\right]$$

**Step 2:** Invert to get $d\omega/dk$:

$$v_g = \frac{d\omega}{dk} = \frac{c}{n(\omega) + \omega \dfrac{dn}{d\omega}}$$

$$\boxed{v_g = \frac{c}{n + \omega (dn/d\omega)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $n$ is **constant** (free space, non-dispersive), $dn/d\omega = 0$, so $v_g = c/n = v_p$ — confirming **$v_p = v_g$ in non-dispersive media**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A medium has a phase velocity $v_p = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s at $\lambda = 600$ nm, and $dv_p/d\lambda = 5 \times 10^{11}$ s$^{-1}$. Find the group velocity.

**Solution:**

$$v_g = v_p - \lambda \frac{dv_p}{d\lambda}$$

$$v_g = (2 \times 10^8) - (600 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{11})$$

$$v_g = 2 \times 10^8 - 3 \times 10^8 \cdot 10^{-9+11} = 2 \times 10^8 - 3 \times 10^8$$

Wait — let me recompute the powers: $(600 \times 10^{-9})(5 \times 10^{11}) = 3000 \times 10^{2} = 3 \times 10^{5}$ m/s.

$$v_g = 2 \times 10^8 - 3 \times 10^5 = 1.997 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_g \approx 1.997 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} < v_p}$$

This is a **normal dispersion** scenario where $v_g < v_p$.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Formula

| Given Data | Use This Formula |
|---|---|
| $\omega$ and $k$ directly | $v_p = \omega/k$, $v_g = d\omega/dk$ |
| $v_p$ and $dv_p/dk$ | $v_g = v_p + k(dv_p/dk)$ |
| $v_p$ and $dv_p/d\lambda$ | $v_g = v_p - \lambda(dv_p/d\lambda)$ |
| Refractive index $n(\omega)$ | $v_g = c/[n + \omega(dn/d\omega)]$ |
| Refractive index $n(\lambda)$ | $v_g = c/[n - \lambda(dn/d\lambda)]$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
