# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define phase velocity and group velocity. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Phase velocity ($v_p$)** is the velocity with which a point of constant phase (e.g., a wave crest) travels in a medium. It is given by $v_p = \omega/k$.
>
> **Group velocity ($v_g$)** is the velocity with which the envelope of a wave packet (formed by superposition of two or more waves of nearly equal frequency) propagates. It is given by $v_g = d\omega/dk$.
>
> The relationship between them is:
> $$v_g = v_p + k \frac{dv_p}{dk} = v_p - \lambda \frac{dv_p}{d\lambda}$$
>
> In a **non-dispersive medium** (e.g., free space), $v_p = v_g = c$. In a **dispersive medium**, $v_p \neq v_g$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is a dispersive medium? Distinguish between normal and anomalous dispersion.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **dispersive medium** is one in which the phase velocity $v_p$ of a wave depends on its frequency (or wavelength). The refractive index $n = n(\omega)$ is not constant.
>
> | Type | Condition | Consequence |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Normal Dispersion** | $dv_p/d\lambda > 0$ (equivalently $dn/d\lambda < 0$) | $v_g < v_p$ |
> | **Anomalous Dispersion** | $dv_p/d\lambda < 0$ (equivalently $dn/d\lambda > 0$) | $v_g > v_p$ |
>
> Examples: Glass (normal), Ionosphere near plasma frequency (anomalous).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Derive the expressions for phase velocity and group velocity of a plane electromagnetic wave. Explain the physical significance of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a non-magnetic medium, the refractive index varies with wavelength as $n^2 = 1 + \dfrac{A\lambda^2}{\lambda^2 - B}$, where $A$ and $B$ are constants. Show that the group velocity is given by $v_g = \dfrac{c}{n + \lambda(dn/d\lambda)}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Phase Velocity Derivation:**

Consider a monochromatic plane wave:

$$E(z,t) = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz)$$

For a point of constant phase $\phi$:

$$\phi = \omega t - kz = \text{const}$$

Differentiating with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \omega - k\frac{dz}{dt} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{v_p = \frac{\omega}{k}}$$

**Physical significance of $v_p$:** It is the speed at which the *pattern* of crests and troughs of a single-frequency wave moves. It can exceed $c$ in dispersive media because no energy or information is carried at this speed.

**Group Velocity Derivation:**

Consider two waves of equal amplitude $E_0$ with $\omega_1 = \omega - \Delta\omega$, $\omega_2 = \omega + \Delta\omega$, $k_1 = k - \Delta k$, $k_2 = k + \Delta k$:

$$E = E_0 \cos[(\omega - \Delta\omega)t - (k - \Delta k)z] + E_0 \cos[(\omega + \Delta\omega)t - (k + \Delta k)z]$$

Applying the identity $\cos A + \cos B = 2\cos\left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)\cos\left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)$:

$$E = 2E_0 \cos[(\Delta\omega)t - (\Delta k)z] \cdot \cos(\omega t - kz)$$

- Carrier travels at $v_p = \omega/k$
- Envelope travels at $v_g = \Delta\omega/\Delta k$

In the limit $\Delta\omega \to 0$:

$$\boxed{v_g = \frac{d\omega}{dk}}$$

**Physical significance of $v_g$:** It is the speed of the envelope of a wave packet and represents the velocity of energy/information transport. In non-absorbing media, $v_g \leq c$.

---

#### Part (b) — Refractive Index Relation (7 marks)

**Given:** $n^2 = 1 + \dfrac{A\lambda^2}{\lambda^2 - B}$

**Step 1:** Start from the relation $v_g = v_p - \lambda(dv_p/d\lambda)$, where $v_p = c/n$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate $v_p$ with respect to $\lambda$:

$$\frac{dv_p}{d\lambda} = \frac{d}{d\lambda}\left(\frac{c}{n}\right) = -\frac{c}{n^2}\frac{dn}{d\lambda}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the group velocity formula:

$$v_g = \frac{c}{n} - \lambda \cdot \left(-\frac{c}{n^2}\frac{dn}{d\lambda}\right)$$

$$v_g = \frac{c}{n} + \frac{c\lambda}{n^2}\frac{dn}{d\lambda}$$

**Step 4:** Take common denominator $n^2$:

$$v_g = \frac{cn + c\lambda(dn/d\lambda)}{n^2} = \frac{c}{n^2}\left[n + \lambda\frac{dn}{d\lambda}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $n$:

$$v_g = \frac{cn}{n^2 + n\lambda(dn/d\lambda)}$$

**Alternative compact form:**

$$\boxed{v_g = \frac{c}{n + \lambda(dn/d\lambda)}}$$

This matches the required form. **QED.** ✓

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> **(a)** What is group velocity? Obtain the relation $v_g = v_p + k(dv_p/dk)$. Discuss what happens in a non-dispersive medium. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The refractive index of a medium is given by $n(\lambda) = 1.5 + 0.02\lambda^2$ (with $\lambda$ in micrometers). Calculate the phase velocity and group velocity at $\lambda = 0.6\ \mu$m. Take $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Group Velocity Definition & Derivation (7 marks)

**Definition:** Group velocity $v_g$ is the velocity with which the **envelope** of a wave packet (or the modulation superimposed on a carrier wave) propagates through a medium. It represents the velocity of energy and information.

**Derivation of $v_g = v_p + k(dv_p/dk)$:**

From the definitions:

$$v_p = \frac{\omega}{k} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega = v_p \cdot k$$

Differentiate both sides with respect to $k$:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dk} = \frac{d(v_p k)}{dk} = v_p \cdot \frac{dk}{dk} + k \cdot \frac{dv_p}{dk}$$

Since $dk/dk = 1$:

$$v_g = \frac{d\omega}{dk} = v_p + k\frac{dv_p}{dk}$$

**Non-dispersive medium case:** In a non-dispersive medium, the phase velocity is **independent of frequency**, so $dv_p/dk = 0$ (since $k$ is a function of $\omega$). Therefore:

$$v_g = v_p$$

Both velocities are equal — e.g., in **free space** $v_p = v_g = c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $n(\lambda) = 1.5 + 0.02\lambda^2$, $\lambda = 0.6\ \mu$m $= 0.6 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 1: Compute refractive index at $\lambda = 0.6\ \mu$m:**

$$n = 1.5 + 0.02 \times (0.6)^2 = 1.5 + 0.02 \times 0.36 = 1.5 + 0.0072 = 1.5072$$

**Step 2: Compute phase velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{c}{n} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5072}$$

$$\boxed{v_p \approx 1.9904 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 3: Compute $dn/d\lambda$:**

$$\frac{dn}{d\lambda} = \frac{d}{d\lambda}(1.5 + 0.02\lambda^2) = 0.04\lambda$$

At $\lambda = 0.6\ \mu$m:

$$\frac{dn}{d\lambda} = 0.04 \times 0.6 = 0.024 \text{ per } \mu\text{m} = 0.024 \times 10^6 \text{ per m} = 2.4 \times 10^4 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Compute group velocity using $v_g = c/[n - \lambda(dn/d\lambda)]$:**

$$v_g = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5072 - (0.6 \times 10^{-6})(2.4 \times 10^4)}$$

$$v_g = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5072 - 0.0144} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.4928}$$

$$\boxed{v_g \approx 2.0096 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the result:**

Since $v_g > v_p$, this is a case of **anomalous dispersion** at this wavelength.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert $\lambda$ from micrometers to meters (or vice versa) when computing $dn/d\lambda$. A mismatch of units gives a wildly incorrect answer and costs **up to 3 marks**. Always write units explicitly in every step.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top reasons students lose marks on this topic in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\omega$ (rad/s) with $f$ (Hz)** — formulas use $\omega$, not $f$. Using $f$ gives an answer off by a factor of $2\pi$.
> 2. **Wrong sign in $v_g = v_p - \lambda(dv_p/d\lambda)$** — the minus sign is *not* a typo; it comes from the chain rule with $k = 2\pi/\lambda$.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units** — particularly $\lambda$ in nm/$\mu$m to meters, and $\omega$ in GHz to rad/s.
> 4. **Stating $v_g = v_p$ in a dispersive medium** — this is only true in vacuum or a non-dispersive medium.
> 5. **Confusing the carrier with the envelope** — the carrier is the high-frequency term $\cos(\omega t - kz)$; the envelope is the low-frequency term $\cos(\Delta\omega t - \Delta k z)$.
> 6. **Not distinguishing normal vs. anomalous dispersion** — examiner specifically tests the sign of $dv_p/d\lambda$ or $dn/d\lambda$.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Phase & Group Velocity**

- ✅ **Phase velocity** = velocity of a *point of constant phase* on a single-frequency wave.
- ✅ **Group velocity** = velocity of the *envelope* of a wave packet (modulation).
- ✅ Key formulas to **memorize verbatim**:
  - $v_p = \omega/k$
  - $v_g = d\omega/dk$
  - $v_g = v_p + k(dv_p/dk) = v_p - \lambda(dv_p/d\lambda)$
  - $v_g = c/[n + \omega(dn/d\omega)] = c/[n - \lambda(dn/d\lambda)]$
- ✅ **Free space / non-dispersive:** $v_p = v_g = c$ (no pulse spreading).
- ✅ **Dispersive medium:** $v_p \neq v_g$ (pulse spreads as it propagates).
- ✅ **Normal dispersion** ($dv_p/d\lambda > 0$, $dn/d\lambda < 0$): $v_g < v_p$.
- ✅ **Anomalous dispersion** ($dv_p/d\lambda < 0$, $dn/d\lambda > 0$): $v_g > v_p$.
- ✅ **Phase velocity can exceed $c$** in dispersive media — but it carries **no energy**.
- ✅ **Group velocity never exceeds $c$** in non-absorbing media — it carries the energy.
- ✅ Engineering impact: **optical fiber bandwidth, ionospheric radio wave bending, plasma physics, waveguide modes** all depend critically on $v_p$ vs $v_g$ distinction.
- ✅ When the question gives $n$ as a function of $\omega$, use $v_g = c/[n + \omega(dn/d\omega)]$.
- ✅ When the question gives $n$ as a function of $\lambda$, use $v_g = c/[n - \lambda(dn/d\lambda)]$.
- ✅ Always show the **trig identity step** in derivations (the examiner awards full marks only when this step is visible).

---

*End of KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 Notes — Phase Velocity and Group Velocity*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Input Impedance of Transmission Line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Input Impedance of Transmission Line

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Input Impedance (Z_in)** of a transmission line is the ratio of the phasor voltage to the phasor current observed at the **sending end (input port)** of the line, when the **receiving end (load end)** is terminated by a load impedance **Z_L**.

Mathematically, it is the impedance "seen" by the source looking into the line:

$$Z_{in} = \left.\frac{V(d)}{I(d)}\right|_{d = l}$$

where **l** is the physical length of the transmission line and **d** is the distance measured from the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The input impedance of a transmission line depends on three critical factors — **(i) the characteristic impedance Z₀, (ii) the electrical length βl (or γl for lossy lines), and (iii) the load termination Z_L.** It is **NOT** equal to Z₀ unless the line is matched (Z_L = Z₀) or is of infinite length.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Echo Tube" Intuition

Imagine shouting into a long, narrow **cylindrical pipe** (like a hollow metal tube). The sound waves travel down the pipe, hit the closed or open far end, and **reflect back**. The acoustic pressure (analogous to voltage) and air-particle velocity (analogous to current) at your mouth (the input) depend entirely on:

- The **shape and material of the pipe** → analogous to the **characteristic impedance Z₀**.
- The **length of the pipe** → analogous to **electrical length βl**.
- What is at the **far end** (open, closed, or partially blocked) → analogous to **load impedance Z_L**.

Even if your mouth produces a steady tone, the impedance you "feel" while blowing into the pipe changes with length, just as the input impedance of a transmission line varies with its electrical length and load.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Z₀** | Characteristic Impedance | **Ohm (Ω)** | 50 Ω, 75 Ω, 300 Ω |
| **β** | Phase Constant | rad/m | 2π/λ |
| **γ** | Propagation Constant | 1/m | α + jβ |
| **α** | Attenuation Constant | Np/m (Neper/meter) | 0.001 – 0.1 Np/m |
| **f** | Operating Frequency | Hz (Hertz) | kHz to GHz range |
| **λ** | Wavelength | meter (m) | Speed of light dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** For **lossless** lines, α = 0 and γ = jβ. For **low-loss** lines, both α and β appear, but α is small. KTU questions on input impedance frequently target the lossless case.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Underlying Physics

The derivation rests on the **Telegrapher's Equations**, which model the distributed nature of any real transmission line as a cascade of infinitesimal series impedance (R + jωL) and shunt admittance (G + jωC) per unit length.

The chain of reasoning proceeds as follows:

- **Step 1 — Wave Decomposition:** Any voltage/current on the line is the superposition of an **incident wave** (V⁺, I⁺ travelling toward the load) and a **reflected wave** (V⁻, I⁻ travelling back to the source).
- **Step 2 — Characteristic Impedance Definition:** The ratio of incident voltage to incident current defines Z₀ = √(Z/Y), which is a property of the line geometry and materials only.
- **Step 3 — Reflection at the Load:** The load cannot, in general, absorb all the incident energy; the reflection coefficient **Γ_L = (Z_L − Z₀)/(Z_L + Z₀)** quantifies the mismatch.
- **Step 4 — Standing Wave Formation:** The interference of incident and reflected waves creates a position-dependent standing-wave pattern.
- **Step 5 — Input Plane Measurement:** At distance **l** from the load, the source sees the result of all the round-trip wave interactions, giving the **input impedance Z_in**.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why wave decomposition?** Because Maxwell's equations in 1-D yield second-order wave equations whose solutions are naturally forward and backward travelling sinusoids.
- **Why a reflection coefficient?** Because any impedance discontinuity (the load) creates a boundary condition that requires two waves to satisfy it.
- **Why hyperbolic functions?** Because **cosh(γl)** and **sinh(γl)** naturally arise when converting between travelling-wave exponentials and the input/load voltage-current relationship.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question bank on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$ | Characteristic impedance | General (lossy) line |
| 2 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Simplified Z₀ | Lossless line (R=G=0) |
| 3 | $\gamma = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)} = \alpha + j\beta$ | Propagation constant | General line |
| 4 | $\Gamma_L = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | Reflection coefficient at load | All lines |
| 5 | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \dfrac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}$ | **Input Impedance (Lossy)** | General (lossy) line |
| 6 | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \dfrac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)}$ | **Input Impedance (Lossless)** | Lossless line (α=0) |
| 7 | $V(d) = V^+ e^{-\gamma d} + V^- e^{+\gamma d}$ | Voltage wave equation | General line |
| 8 | $I(d) = \dfrac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma d} - \dfrac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma d}$ | Current wave equation | General line |
| 9 | $\Gamma(d) = \Gamma_L e^{-2\gamma d}$ | Reflection coefficient at distance d from load | All lines |
| 10 | $Z_{in} = Z_0 \cdot \dfrac{1 + \Gamma_{in}}{1 - \Gamma_{in}}$ | Input impedance from input reflection coefficient | All lines |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The input impedance concept is the **backbone of RF and microwave engineering**. It is used in:

- **Antenna matching networks** — ensuring maximum power transfer from transmitter to antenna.
- **Stub matching in microstrip lines** — quarter-wave and single-stub tuners rely entirely on Z_in variations.
- **Filter design** — quarter-wave open/short-circuited stubs behave as reactive elements.
- **Time-domain reflectometry (TDR)** — measuring cable faults by observing Z_in discontinuities.
- **High-speed digital PCB design** — controlled-impedance routing uses input impedance to avoid signal reflections.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Starting Point — The Telegrapher's Voltage & Current Solutions

For a general (lossy) transmission line, the steady-state sinusoidal solutions of the telegrapher's equations at a distance **d** from the load are:

$$V(d) = V^+ e^{-\gamma d} + V^- e^{+\gamma d}$$

$$I(d) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma d} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma d}$$

> **Logic Note:** Here **V⁺** and **V⁻** are complex phasor amplitudes. The term $e^{-\gamma d}$ represents the wave travelling **towards the load**, while $e^{+\gamma d}$ represents the wave travelling **away from the load** (reflected). The minus sign in the current equation arises from the directional definition of current flow in the two waves.

## 3.2 Step 1 — Apply Boundary Condition at the Load (d = 0)

At the load end, $d = 0$, so $e^{-\gamma d} = e^{+\gamma d} = 1$. The voltage and current become:

$$V_L = V(0) = V^+ + V^-$$

$$I_L = I(0) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} = \frac{V^+ - V^-}{Z_0}$$

> **Logic Note:** The load boundary condition ties the two unknown phasors V⁺ and V⁻ to the known load quantities V_L and I_L (or equivalently to Z_L = V_L / I_L).

## 3.3 Step 2 — Solve for V⁺ and V⁻ in Terms of Load Quantities

From the two equations above, add and subtract to isolate the phasors:

$$V^+ = \frac{V_L + Z_0 I_L}{2}$$

$$V^- = \frac{V_L - Z_0 I_L}{2}$$

> **Logic Note:** This is simply algebraic inversion. We solve the linear system of two equations in two unknowns (V⁺ and V⁻).

## 3.4 Step 3 — Substitute Back at the Input Plane (d = l)

At the input end of the line, $d = l$. Substituting the expressions for V⁺ and V⁻ into the general voltage equation:

$$V_{in} = V(l) = \frac{V_L + Z_0 I_L}{2} e^{-\gamma l} + \frac{V_L - Z_0 I_L}{2} e^{+\gamma l}$$

Similarly for the current:

$$I_{in} = I(l) = \frac{V_L + Z_0 I_L}{2 Z_0} e^{-\gamma l} - \frac{V_L - Z_0 I_L}{2 Z_0} e^{+\gamma l}$$

## 3.5 Step 4 — Group Terms and Introduce Hyperbolic Functions

Use the identities $e^{+\gamma l} + e^{-\gamma l} = 2\cosh(\gamma l)$ and $e^{+\gamma l} - e^{-\gamma l} = 2\sinh(\gamma l)$.

**Voltage at input:**

$$V_{in} = \frac{V_L}{2}\left(e^{-\gamma l} + e^{+\gamma l}\right) + \frac{Z_0 I_L}{2}\left(e^{-\gamma l} - e^{+\gamma l}\right)$$

$$V_{in} = V_L \cosh(\gamma l) - Z_0 I_L \sinh(\gamma l)$$

> **Logic Note:** The minus sign in front of the second term comes from the order of the subtraction in the hyperbolic sine identity. Take care: $e^{-\gamma l} - e^{+\gamma l} = -2\sinh(\gamma l)$.

**Current at input:**

$$I_{in} = \frac{V_L}{2 Z_0}\left(e^{-\gamma l} - e^{+\gamma l}\right) + \frac{I_L}{2}\left(e^{-\gamma l} + e^{+\gamma l}\right)$$

$$I_{in} = -\frac{V_L}{Z_0} \sinh(\gamma l) + I_L \cosh(\gamma l)$$

## 3.6 Step 5 — Take the Ratio V_in / I_in to Obtain Z_in

$$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} = \frac{V_L \cosh(\gamma l) - Z_0 I_L \sinh(\gamma l)}{I_L \cosh(\gamma l) - \dfrac{V_L}{Z_0} \sinh(\gamma l)}$$

## 3.7 Step 6 — Eliminate Load Quantities Using Z_L = V_L / I_L

Divide numerator and denominator by $I_L$:

$$Z_{in} = \frac{Z_L \cosh(\gamma l) - Z_0 \sinh(\gamma l)}{\cosh(\gamma l) - \dfrac{Z_L}{Z_0} \sinh(\gamma l)}$$

## 3.8 Step 7 — Convert to tanh(γl) by Dividing by cosh(γl)

Divide both numerator and denominator by $\cosh(\gamma l)$ and use the identity $\tanh(\gamma l) = \dfrac{\sinh(\gamma l)}{\cosh(\gamma l)}$:

$$Z_{in} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{1 - \dfrac{Z_L}{Z_0} \tanh(\gamma l)}$$

> **Logic Note:** Dividing by $\cosh(\gamma l)$ is allowed because the line is in its normal operating regime and $\cosh(\gamma l) \neq 0$ for any real γl of physical interest.

## 3.9 Step 8 — Final Form for Lossy Transmission Line

Multiply numerator and denominator by $Z_0$ to obtain the **standard KTU textbook form**:

$$\boxed{\,Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master equation** for a lossy line. Memorize it precisely, including the sign and the placement of Z₀ vs Z_L.

## 3.10 Step 9 — Specialization to Lossless Line (α = 0, γ = jβ)

For a lossless transmission line, the propagation constant is purely imaginary: $\gamma = j\beta$. Using the identity $\tanh(j\beta l) = j\tan(\beta l)$:

$$\boxed{\,Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)}\,}$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the form that appears in **over 80% of KTU university exam questions** on this topic. The student must be able to derive it from the lossy case in a single line.

## 3.11 Step 10 — Important Special Cases (KTU Favourites)

| Condition | Value of $\tan(\beta l)$ | Resulting $Z_{in}$ | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $l = \lambda/4$ | $\tan(\beta l) = \infty$ | $Z_{in} = \dfrac{Z_0^2}{Z_L}$ | Quarter-wave impedance inverter |
| $l = \lambda/2$ | $\tan(\beta l) = 0$ | $Z_{in} = Z_L$ | Half-wave line repeats the load |
| $Z_L = Z_0$ (matched) | Any | $Z_{in} = Z_0$ | Matched line, no reflection |
| $Z_L = 0$ (short) | Any | $Z_{in} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)$ | Purely reactive short-circuited line |
| $Z_L = \infty$ (open) | Any | $Z_{in} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta l)$ | Purely reactive open-circuited line |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Input Impedance Computation Pipeline

The following Mermaid block-diagram shows the **logical computation pipeline** used to derive and apply the input impedance formula, mapping the relationships between incident waves, reflection, and impedance transformation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Source Generator"] --> B["Sending End of Line d equals l"]
    B --> C["Distributed Parameter Line R L G C per unit length"]
    C --> D["Incident Wave V plus travelling toward load"]
    C --> E["Reflected Wave V minus travelling toward source"]
    D --> F["Load Termination ZL"]
    E --> F
    F --> G{"Reflection Coefficient Gamma L equals ZL minus Z0 over ZL plus Z0"}
    G --> H["Standing Wave Pattern along line"]
    H --> I["Measure Voltage and Current at d equals l"]
    I --> J["Compute Zin equals Vl over Il"]
    J --> K["Apply Formula Zin equals Z0 times ZL plus Z0 tanh gamma l over Z0 plus ZL tanh gamma l"]
    K --> L["Lossless Specialization Zin equals Z0 times ZL plus jZ0 tan beta l over Z0 plus jZL tan beta l"]
    L --> M["Use Zin for Matching Network Design"]
```

## 4.2 Logic-Flow Diagram — Derivation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: General Vd and Id equations"] --> S2["Step 2: Boundary at d equals 0 gives VL and IL"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Solve for V plus and V minus"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Substitute at d equals l"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Use cosh and sinh identities"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Take ratio V_in over I_in"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Replace VL and IL with ZL"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: Convert to tanh by dividing by cosh"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 9: Multiply through by Z0 to get lossy Zin"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 10: Set alpha equals 0 gamma equals j beta for lossless Zin"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Interpretation Note

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The left-to-right flow mirrors the mathematical derivation sequence. The two central decision diamonds represent the two **physical discontinuities** in any transmission-line problem — the load mismatch (where Γ_L is non-zero) and the measurement plane (where Z_in is evaluated). The final two blocks convert the result into the two most useful engineering forms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Insight:** Note how the input impedance emerges from the **interplay of two counter-propagating waves** that interfere to produce a position-dependent standing wave. The hyperbolic tangent in the final equation is a direct mathematical signature of this standing-wave phenomenon — it has no analogue in lumped-circuit theory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define input impedance of a transmission line. On what factors does it depend?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The input impedance of a transmission line is defined as the ratio of the phasor voltage to the phasor current measured at the sending end (input port) of the line, i.e.,

$$Z_{in} = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} = \frac{V(l)}{I(l)}$$

It depends on three factors: **(1)** the characteristic impedance Z₀ of the line, **(2)** the electrical length of the line (βl for lossless, γl for lossy), and **(3)** the load impedance Z_L. (1 + 1 + 1 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the general expression for the input impedance of a lossy transmission line terminated in a load Z_L.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a lossy transmission line of length *l*, terminated in a load Z_L, the input impedance is given by:

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}$$

where Z₀ is the characteristic impedance and γ = α + jβ is the complex propagation constant. (2 marks for the formula, 1 mark for explaining symbols)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Derive the expression for the input impedance of a lossless transmission line terminated in an arbitrary load Z_L. Hence, find the input impedance for (a) a short-circuited line and (b) a quarter-wave line.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Lossless Line Input Impedance (7 marks)

Starting with the general voltage and current solutions for a transmission line at distance *d* from the load:

$$V(d) = V^+ e^{-\gamma d} + V^- e^{+\gamma d}$$
$$I(d) = \frac{V^+}{Z_0} e^{-\gamma d} - \frac{V^-}{Z_0} e^{+\gamma d}$$

At $d = 0$ (load): $V_L = V^+ + V^-$ and $I_L = \frac{V^+ - V^-}{Z_0}$

Solving: $V^+ = \frac{V_L + Z_0 I_L}{2}$ and $V^- = \frac{V_L - Z_0 I_L}{2}$

At $d = l$ (input), applying the lossy result (derived in Section 3):

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}$$

For a **lossless** line, $\gamma = j\beta$ and $\tanh(j\beta l) = j\tan(\beta l)$:

$$\boxed{\,Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)}\,}$$

**(Marks distribution: 2 for general setup, 2 for boundary conditions, 2 for hyperbolic manipulation, 1 for final specialization)**

#### Part (b) — Special Cases (7 marks)

**Case (i): Short-circuited line** — set $Z_L = 0$:

$$Z_{in}^{SC} = Z_0 \, \frac{0 + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + 0} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)$$

**Case (ii): Quarter-wave line** — set $l = \lambda/4$, so $\beta l = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2}$, hence $\tan(\beta l) \to \infty$. Taking the limit carefully:

$$Z_{in} = \lim_{\tan(\beta l) \to \infty} Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)} = Z_0 \cdot \frac{jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{jZ_L \tan(\beta l)} = \frac{Z_0^2}{Z_L}$$

So the quarter-wave line acts as an **impedance inverter**: $Z_{in} = \dfrac{Z_0^2}{Z_L}$. (3 + 4 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A lossless transmission line of characteristic impedance 50 Ω and electrical length βl = 60° is terminated in a load Z_L = (25 − j50) Ω. Calculate: (a) the reflection coefficient at the load and (b) the input impedance of the line.**

#### Part (a) — Reflection Coefficient at the Load (7 marks)

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0} = \frac{(25 - j50) - 50}{(25 - j50) + 50} = \frac{-25 - j50}{75 - j50}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{(-25 - j50)(75 + j50)}{(75 - j50)(75 + j50)} = \frac{-1875 - j1250 - j3750 - j^2 2500}{75^2 + 50^2}$$

$$\Gamma_L = \frac{(-1875 + 2500) + j(-1250 - 3750)}{5625 + 2500} = \frac{625 - j5000}{8125}$$

$$\Gamma_L = 0.0769 - j0.6154$$

Magnitude: $|\Gamma_L| = \sqrt{0.0769^2 + 0.6154^2} = \sqrt{0.0059 + 0.3787} = \sqrt{0.3846} \approx 0.620$

Angle: $\angle \Gamma_L = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{-0.6154}{0.0769}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-8.0) \approx -82.87°$

So $\Gamma_L \approx 0.620 \angle -82.87°$. (7 marks)

#### Part (b) — Input Impedance (7 marks)

Using the lossless input impedance formula with $\beta l = 60°$:

$$Z_{in} = Z_0 \, \frac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)}$$

Compute $\tan(60°) = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$:

**Numerator:** $Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(60°) = (25 - j50) + j(50)(1.732) = 25 + j(86.6 - 50) = 25 + j36.6$

**Denominator:** $Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(60°) = 50 + j(1.732)(25 - j50) = 50 + j43.3 + j^2 86.6 = (50 - 86.6) + j43.3 = -36.6 + j43.3$

$$Z_{in} = 50 \cdot \frac{25 + j36.6}{-36.6 + j43.3}$$

Rationalize by multiplying by the conjugate:

$$Z_{in} = 50 \cdot \frac{(25 + j36.6)(-36.6 - j43.3)}{(-36.6)^2 + (43.3)^2}$$

**Denominator magnitude squared:** $36.6^2 + 43.3^2 = 1339.56 + 1874.89 = 3214.45$

**Numerator expansion:** $(25)(-36.6) + (25)(-j43.3) + (j36.6)(-36.6) + (j36.6)(-j43.3)$
$= -915 - j1082.5 - j1339.56 + 1584.78$
$= (1584.78 - 915) + j(-1082.5 - 1339.56)$
$= 669.78 - j2422.06$

$$Z_{in} = 50 \cdot \frac{669.78 - j2422.06}{3214.45} = 50 \cdot (0.2084 - j0.7535)$$

$$\boxed{\,Z_{in} \approx 10.42 - j37.68 \, \Omega\,}$$

(7 marks: 2 for formula setup, 3 for numerator/denominator calculation, 2 for final rationalization and result)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in tanh Identity:** Students frequently write $\tanh(j\beta l) = -j\tan(\beta l)$. The correct identity is $+\,j\tan(\beta l)$ — a minus sign here will flip all your impedances into the wrong half-plane.
> 2. **Forgetting to Rationalize:** KTU examiners specifically check whether the final complex impedance is presented in **rectangular form (R + jX)**, not as a ratio of complex numbers. Always rationalize.
> 3. **Misidentifying βl:** Ensure you compute $\beta l = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot l$ correctly. A common slip is using the **physical length** *l* directly in place of the **electrical length** βl inside the tangent function.
> 4. **Quarter-Wave Limit:** For $l = \lambda/4$, directly substituting $\tan(\pi/2) = \infty$ is undefined. Always take the limit as $\tan(\beta l) \to \infty$ by dividing numerator and denominator by the divergent term first.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Input Impedance Definition:** $Z_{in} = V_{in}/I_{in}$ measured at the sending end — depends on Z₀, βl (or γl), and Z_L. ✓
- **Master Formula (Lossy):** $Z_{in} = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + Z_0 \tanh(\gamma l)}{Z_0 + Z_L \tanh(\gamma l)}$ ✓
- **Master Formula (Lossless):** $Z_{in} = Z_0 \dfrac{Z_L + jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)}{Z_0 + jZ_L \tan(\beta l)}$ ✓
- **Key Identity:** $\tanh(j\beta l) = j\tan(\beta l)$ — used in **every** KTU specialization. ✓
- **Quarter-Wave Result:** $Z_{in} = Z_0^2 / Z_L$ — impedance inversion property. ✓
- **Half-Wave Result:** $Z_{in} = Z_L$ — line repeats the load exactly. ✓
- **Short-Circuit Result:** $Z_{in}^{SC} = jZ_0 \tan(\beta l)$ — purely reactive. ✓
- **Open-Circuit Result:** $Z_{in}^{OC} = -jZ_0 \cot(\beta l)$ — purely reactive. ✓
- **Matched Line Result:** When $Z_L = Z_0$, $Z_{in} = Z_0$ for **all** line lengths. ✓
- **Derivation Sequence:** Wave equation → boundary conditions → solve V⁺, V⁻ → evaluate at $d = l$ → use cosh/sinh identities → take ratio → convert to tanh → specialize. ✓
- **KTU Hot Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, examiners expect you to **derive** the formula from the telegrapher's equations, not just state it. Practice writing the full chain of substitutions cleanly. ✓
- **Engineering Applications:** Antenna matching, stub tuners, microstrip filters, TDR fault location, and PCB controlled-impedance routing. ✓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflection Coefficient and VSWR
Transmission Line as Circuit Elements (L and C)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmission Line as Circuit Elements (L and C)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **transmission line** is a pair of parallel conductors used to guide electromagnetic energy from a source to a load. When the physical length of the line becomes a significant fraction of the operating wavelength (typically $\geq 0.01\lambda$ to $0.1\lambda$), the line can no longer be treated as a simple "wire connection." Instead, it must be modeled as a **distributed parameter network** consisting of an infinite cascade of infinitesimal circuit elements.

The two fundamental distributed parameters of any two-conductor transmission line are:

- **Inductance per unit length** $L$ (in Henries per meter, H/m): Represents the magnetic energy storage capability of the line due to the current flowing through the conductors.
- **Capacitance per unit length** $C$ (in Farads per meter, F/m): Represents the electric energy storage capability of the line due to the potential difference between the conductors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The transmission line is treated as a **lumped-element equivalent circuit** in which each infinitesimal segment $\Delta z$ of the line is represented by a series inductance $L\,\Delta z$ and a shunt capacitance $C\,\Delta z$. This is the foundational model upon which the Telegrapher's Equations are built.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Intuition

Imagine a long, flexible **garden hose** carrying water from a tap to a sprinkler:

- The **water pressure** at the tap and the **water pressure** at the sprinkler are not identical. The hose itself stores a tiny amount of water under pressure. This is analogous to the **capacitance** $C$ between the two conductors, which "stores" electric field energy.

- The **flow of water** in the hose has inertia. If you suddenly close the tap, the water does not stop instantly; the momentum of the water column resists the change. This inertia is analogous to the **inductance** $L$ of the conductors, which opposes any sudden change in current.

- The longer the hose, the more water it stores and the more inertia it has. Similarly, the longer the transmission line, the more inductance and capacitance it possesses. This is why we express $L$ and $C$ **per unit length** — they are intrinsic, geometrical properties of the line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m} \approx \dfrac{1}{36\pi \times 10^{9}}\ \text{F/m}$
> - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$

## 1.3 Why Do We Need an L–C Model?

A "short" wire at low frequencies (say, 50 Hz power wiring or audio cables) can be approximated as a perfect short circuit because the wavelength ($\lambda = c/f$) is enormous. However, at **radio frequencies (RF)**, **microwave frequencies**, and in **digital high-speed circuits**, the wavelength becomes comparable to the line length. The voltage and current waves then vary appreciably along the length of the line, and the **propagation delay** $\tau = \sqrt{LC} \cdot \ell$ becomes non-negligible. This forces us to abandon lumped-circuit intuition and embrace the distributed L–C model.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a KTU question mentions a line of length comparable to $\lambda$, or asks about "high-frequency" or "transient" behavior, immediately invoke the distributed L–C model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — How L and C Arise

A transmission line has two conductors separated by a dielectric. When a voltage $V$ is applied:

1. **Charge Separation → Capacitance**: A positive charge $+Q$ accumulates on one conductor and a negative charge $-Q$ on the other. The electric field $\vec{E}$ established between them stores electrostatic energy $W_E = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$. The geometry of the conductors determines $C$ per unit length.

2. **Current Flow → Inductance**: A current $I$ flowing in one conductor (and returning in the other) generates a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ encircling the conductors. The flux linkage per unit length defines the inductance $L$ per unit length. The magnetic energy stored is $W_M = \dfrac{1}{2}LI^2$.

3. **Coupling Effect**: $L$ and $C$ are not independent. For a TEM (Transverse Electromagnetic) transmission line in a homogeneous medium, they satisfy the elegant relationship $LC = \mu\varepsilon$, which directly yields the phase velocity $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC} = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$.

## 2.2 The Distributed L–C Equivalent Circuit

A transmission line of length $\ell$ is conceptually divided into $N$ infinitesimal segments. As $N \to \infty$ and $\Delta z \to 0$:

- Each segment contains a **series inductor** $L\,\Delta z$ (representing the magnetic field).
- Each segment contains a **shunt capacitor** $C\,\Delta z$ (representing the electric field).
- The shunt conductance $G$ and series resistance $R$ are neglected in the **lossless** approximation (the focus of this KTU module).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Topology:**
> The **series** placement of $L$ is because the magnetic flux encircles the conductors in a closed loop, and Faraday's law demands that the induced EMF be in series with the conductor path. The **shunt** placement of $C$ is because the displacement current between the conductors is transverse to the direction of propagation, exactly as a parallel-plate capacitor would behave.

## 2.3 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Two-Wire Line (D >> a) | Coaxial Line (b > a) | Units |
|---|----------|------------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Capacitance per unit length $C$ | $\dfrac{\pi \varepsilon}{\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)} \approx \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}$ | $\dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$ | F/m |
| 2 | Inductance per unit length $L$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{\pi} \cosh^{-1}(D/2a) \approx \dfrac{\mu}{\pi} \ln(D/a)$ | $\dfrac{\mu}{2\pi} \ln(b/a)$ | H/m |
| 3 | Phase velocity $v_p$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}$ | m/s |
| 4 | Characteristic impedance $Z_0$ | $\sqrt{L/C} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} \cosh^{-1}(D/2a)$ | $\sqrt{L/C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} \ln(b/a)$ | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | Product $LC$ | $\mu \varepsilon$ | $\mu \varepsilon$ | $\text{H} \cdot \text{F} / \text{m}^2$ |
| 6 | Ratio $L/C$ | $\dfrac{\mu \varepsilon}{C^2} = Z_0^2$ | $\dfrac{\mu \varepsilon}{C^2} = Z_0^2$ | $\Omega^2$ |

Where:
- $D$ = center-to-center separation between the two wires of a two-wire line.
- $a$ = radius of each wire in a two-wire line.
- $b$ = inner radius of the outer conductor in a coaxial line.
- $a$ = outer radius of the inner conductor in a coaxial line.
- $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ (permittivity of the dielectric).
- $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ (permeability of the dielectric; usually $\mu_r = 1$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coaxial cables (RG-58, RG-174):** Used in cable TV, Ethernet (10Base-2), and RF test equipment. The $L$ and $C$ per unit length directly determine the cable's $Z_0$ (typically 50 $\Omega$ or 75 $\Omega$).
- **Twisted-pair telephony:** The twisted geometry (with separation $D$ and radius $a$) sets the characteristic impedance for DSL and Ethernet (100 $\Omega$ for Cat-5/6).
- **Microstrip and stripline PCBs:** High-speed digital designers use the $L$–$C$ model to control impedance discontinuities and avoid signal reflections.
- **Power transmission lines:** Although 50/60 Hz is low frequency, the long distances (hundreds of km) make the line's $L$ critical for voltage-drop and stability calculations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $C$ for a Two-Wire Transmission Line

**Setup:** Two parallel cylindrical conductors of radius $a$ with center-to-center separation $D$, embedded in a dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$. We assume $D \gg a$ so that the charge distribution on each wire remains approximately uniform (a thin-wire approximation).

**Step 1 — Potential due to a single line charge:**

A uniform line charge $+\rho_L$ (C/m) located on the z-axis produces a radial electric field given by Gauss's law:

$$
\vec{E}_1 = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon \rho}\,\hat{a}_\rho
$$

The potential difference between a point at radial distance $\rho_2$ and a point at $\rho_1$ (with $\rho_2 > \rho_1$) is:

$$
V_{12} = -\int_{\rho_1}^{\rho_2} \vec{E}_1 \cdot d\vec{\rho} = -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon} \int_{\rho_1}^{\rho_2} \frac{d\rho}{\rho}
$$

Performing the integral:

$$
V_{12} = -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon} \ln\!\left(\frac{\rho_2}{\rho_1}\right) = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon} \ln\!\left(\frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Superpose potentials from both wires:**

Let wire 1 carry charge $+\rho_L$ at position $x = +D/2$ and wire 2 carry charge $-\rho_L$ at position $x = -D/2$. Consider an arbitrary point $P$ at the surface of wire 1. The distances from $P$ to the centers of the two wires are:
- To center of wire 1: $\rho_1 = a$
- To center of wire 2: $\rho_2 = D - a \approx D$ (since $D \gg a$)

The potential at the surface of wire 1 due to **both** wires is therefore:

$$
V_1 = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon} \ln\!\left(\frac{a}{D}\right) + \frac{(-\rho_L)}{2\pi \varepsilon} \ln\!\left(\frac{\text{to wire 2 center}}{\text{to wire 1 center}}\right)
$$

Using the exact symmetric geometry and taking the surface points diametrically opposite on each wire, the potential of wire 1 relative to wire 2 is computed carefully. By symmetry, the potential difference between the two wire surfaces is twice the contribution from one wire acting on the other:

$$
V = V_1 - V_2 = 2 \cdot \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) = \frac{\rho_L}{\pi \varepsilon} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Define Capacitance per unit length:**

By definition, the capacitance per unit length is the charge per unit length on one conductor divided by the voltage between the two conductors:

$$
C = \frac{\rho_L}{V} = \frac{\rho_L}{\dfrac{\rho_L}{\pi \varepsilon} \ln(D/a)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,C = \frac{\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}\,,\quad \text{(for } D \gg a \text{)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Exact form using inverse hyperbolic cosine:**

For finite $D/a$, the exact result (without the $D \gg a$ approximation) is:

$$
C = \frac{\pi \varepsilon}{\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two forms are related by the identity $\cosh^{-1}(x) = \ln(x + \sqrt{x^2 - 1})$, which for $x = D/2a \gg 1$ reduces to $\ln(D/a)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of $L$ for a Two-Wire Transmission Line

**Setup:** Same geometry as above. Both wires carry current $I$ in opposite directions (a closed loop).

**Step 1 — Magnetic field from a single long wire:**

A current $I$ in the $z$-direction produces a magnetic field encircling the wire:

$$
\vec{H}_1 = \frac{I}{2\pi \rho}\,\hat{a}_\phi
$$

By Ampere's law applied to a circular loop of radius $\rho$:

$$
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{\ell} = I \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_\phi (2\pi \rho) = I
$$

Hence $H_\phi = \dfrac{I}{2\pi \rho}$ and $B_\phi = \mu H_\phi = \dfrac{\mu I}{2\pi \rho}$.

**Step 2 — Flux linkage per unit length:**

The magnetic flux per unit length passing through the area bounded by the two conductors (i.e., between the surfaces of the two wires along a length $\ell = 1$ m) is found by integrating $B$ from $\rho = a$ (surface of wire 1) to $\rho = D - a \approx D$ (surface of wire 2):

$$
\Phi = \int_{a}^{D} B_\phi \, d\rho = \int_{a}^{D} \frac{\mu I}{2\pi \rho}\, d\rho
$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
\Phi = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Inductance per unit length:**

By definition, $L = \Phi / I$:

$$
L = \frac{\mu}{2\pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

This is the **external inductance** per unit length. The **internal inductance** (flux inside the conductors) contributes an additional $\mu/(8\pi)$ per conductor, but at high frequencies the skin effect confines current to the surface and internal inductance vanishes. For KTU problems, we use:

$$
\boxed{\,L = \frac{\mu}{\pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) = \frac{\mu}{\pi} \cosh^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D}{2a}\right)\,}
$$

where the factor of 2 in the numerator arises because **both** wires contribute flux.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check via $LC$ Product:**
> 
> $$LC = \frac{\mu}{\pi}\ln(D/a) \cdot \frac{\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)} = \mu \varepsilon$$
> 
> Hence $v_p = 1/\sqrt{LC} = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon} = c/n$, which is the speed of light in the dielectric. **This is a celebrated result that confirms Maxwell's prediction using only circuit theory!**

---

## 3.3 Derivation of $C$ and $L$ for a Coaxial Transmission Line

**Setup:** Inner conductor of radius $a$, outer conductor of inner radius $b$, dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon$ filling the region $a \le \rho \le b$.

### 3.3.1 Capacitance per unit length

**Step 1 — Electric field by Gauss's law:**

Charge $+\rho_L$ on the inner conductor. By cylindrical symmetry, the flux through a Gaussian cylinder of radius $\rho$ (where $a < \rho < b$) is:

$$
\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = \rho_L \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_\rho (2\pi \rho) = \rho_L
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_\rho = \frac{D_\rho}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon \rho}
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage between conductors:**

$$
V = -\int_{b}^{a} \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{\rho} = \int_{a}^{b} \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon \rho}\, d\rho = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon} \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance:**

$$
\boxed{\,C = \frac{\rho_L}{V} = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}\,,\quad \text{(coaxial, F/m)}}
$$

### 3.3.2 Inductance per unit length

**Step 1 — Magnetic field by Ampere's law:**

Current $I$ in the inner conductor (returning via the outer conductor). Inside the dielectric ($a < \rho < b$):

$$
H_\phi (2\pi \rho) = I \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_\phi = \frac{I}{2\pi \rho}
$$

**Step 2 — Flux per unit length:**

The flux per unit length enclosed between the conductors is:

$$
\Phi = \int_{a}^{b} B_\phi \, d\rho = \int_{a}^{b} \frac{\mu I}{2\pi \rho}\, d\rho = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Inductance:**

$$
\boxed{\,L = \frac{\mu}{2\pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)\,,\quad \text{(coaxial, H/m)}}
$$

**Verification of $LC$ Product:**

$$
LC = \frac{\mu}{2\pi} \ln(b/a) \cdot \frac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)} = \mu \varepsilon \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind the Geometry Factors:** Notice that the coaxial line has $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ in both $L$ and $C$, while the two-wire line has $\frac{1}{\pi}$ in $L$ and $\pi$ in $C$. This is because the coaxial line has perfect cylindrical symmetry (a $2\pi$ geometry), whereas the two-wire line has a weaker symmetry (essentially a $\pi$ geometry from the image-method analysis).

---

## 3.4 Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$ — The Most Important Derived Quantity

The **characteristic impedance** is defined for a lossless line as:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}
$$

**For the two-wire line:**

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln(D/a)}{\dfrac{\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}}} = \frac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

For an air-dielectric line: $Z_0 = \dfrac{120}{\pi}\ln(D/a) \approx 120\,\ln(D/a)\ \Omega$ (numerical, since $\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377\ \Omega$).

**For the coaxial line:**

$$
Z_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

For air dielectric: $Z_0 \approx 60\,\ln(b/a)\ \Omega$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $Z_0 = \sqrt{LC}$ instead of $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$. The correct formula is the **square root of the ratio**, not the product. The product $LC$ gives $1/v_p^2$, not $Z_0^2$.

# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Distributed L–C Model of a Transmission Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc[Source Vg Zg] --> nodeSeg1
    nodeSeg1[L dz Segment 1] --> nodeSeg2
    nodeSeg2[L dz Segment 2] --> nodeSeg3
    nodeSeg3[L dz Segment N] --> nodeLoad
    nodeSeg1 -.-> nodeC1[C dz Shunt 1]
    nodeSeg2 -.-> nodeC2[C dz Shunt 2]
    nodeSeg3 -.-> nodeC3[C dz Shunt N]
    nodeC1 -.-> nodeGnd1[Reference Conductor]
    nodeC2 -.-> nodeGnd2[Reference Conductor]
    nodeC3 -.-> nodeGnd3[Reference Conductor]
    nodeLoad[Load ZL] --> nodeEnd[Return Path]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows a finite segmentation. As the number of segments $N \to \infty$ and the segment length $dz \to 0$, the discrete ladder network becomes the **continuous** distributed-parameter transmission line. Each infinitesimal section stores energy in the magnetic field (via $L\,dz$) and the electric field (via $C\,dz$).

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow: Energy Propagation Along a Transmission Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeIn[Input Voltage and Current at z=0] --> nodeFieldE[Transverse Electric Field E Established]
    nodeFieldE --> nodeCap[Energy Storage in Capacitance C per dz]
    nodeCap --> nodeFieldB[Transverse Magnetic Field B Established]
    nodeFieldB --> nodeInd[Energy Storage in Inductance L per dz]
    nodeInd --> nodeCoupling[Cross Coupling between E and B]
    nodeCoupling --> nodeWave[TEM Wave Propagates in plus z direction]
    nodeWave --> nodeOut[Output at z equals l]
    nodeOut --> nodeTerm[Termination: Matched, Open, or Short]
    nodeTerm --> nodeRefl{Reflected Wave Present?}
    nodeRefl -->|Yes| nodeVSWR[VSWR greater than 1, Standing Waves]
    nodeRefl -->|No| nodeMatch[Perfectly Matched ZL equals Z0]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Two-Wire vs Coaxial Geometry (Text-Based)

### Two-Wire Line Cross-Section

```
        O--------------------------------------------------O
        |                    D                              |
        |   <----------- center to center ----------->      |
        |                                                  |
       ( • ) wire 1, radius a         ( • ) wire 2, radius a
              \                          /
               \   dielectric eps, mu   /
                \______________________/
```

### Coaxial Line Cross-Section

```
   _____________________________________
  /                                     \
 |  (((((((((  inner conductor  )))))))) |   <- radius a
 |  ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~|   <- dielectric eps, mu
 |  \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\ |   <- radius b
  \_____________________________________/
   \\\\\\ outer conductor /////////////
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visualization Trick:** In the exam, you can quickly draw the cross-section and label $D$, $a$ (for two-wire) or $a$, $b$ (for coaxial) to earn easy methodology marks even if the final calculation goes astray.
