# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 🎯 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the terms "inductance per unit length" and "capacitance per unit length" of a transmission line. Why are these quantities defined per unit length rather than as totals?**

**Model Answer:**

**Inductance per unit length ($L$):** It is defined as the magnetic flux linkage per unit length per unit current flowing through the conductors. Mathematically:

$$
L = \frac{\Phi}{I} \quad \text{(H/m)}
$$

**Capacitance per unit length ($C$):** It is defined as the charge per unit length on one conductor per unit potential difference between the two conductors. Mathematically:

$$
C = \frac{Q}{V} \quad \text{(F/m)}
$$

**Why per unit length?** A transmission line is a **distributed** system. The total inductance and capacitance grow linearly with line length, so expressing them per unit length gives an **intrinsic, geometry-dependent** property that is independent of the line's physical length. This makes analysis scalable — the engineer can compute total $L_{\text{total}} = L \cdot \ell$ and $C_{\text{total}} = C \cdot \ell$ for any line of length $\ell$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition of $L$: **1 mark**. Definition of $C$: **1 mark**. Justification for "per unit length": **1 mark**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**For a lossless transmission line, show that the product $LC = \mu\varepsilon$ and hence the phase velocity is $v_p = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$.**

**Model Answer:**

For a two-wire line, we have already derived:

$$
L = \frac{\mu}{\pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right), \qquad C = \frac{\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}
$$

Multiplying:

$$
LC = \frac{\mu}{\pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) \cdot \frac{\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}
$$

The $\ln(D/a)$ terms cancel and the $\pi$ terms cancel:

$$
LC = \mu \varepsilon
$$

For a coaxial line, the same product yields:

$$
LC = \frac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a) \cdot \frac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)} = \mu \varepsilon
$$

The phase velocity of a lossless line is:

$$
v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}
$$

For free space ($\mu = \mu_0$, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$):

$$
v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} = c = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Conclusion:** The result is **geometry-independent** — it depends only on the medium, not on the line dimensions. This is a profound consequence of Maxwell's equations and is verified experimentally in TEM wave propagation.

---

## 5.2 📘 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`

**Derive the expressions for the inductance per unit length and capacitance per unit length of a two-wire transmission line consisting of two parallel conductors of radius $a$ separated by a center-to-center distance $D$ (with $D \gg a$) in a medium of permittivity $\varepsilon$ and permeability $\mu$. Also determine the characteristic impedance of the line.**

#### **Part (a) — Capacitance per unit length (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Field due to a single line charge:**

Apply Gauss's law to a cylindrical Gaussian surface of radius $\rho$ around a wire carrying line charge density $\rho_L$:

$$
\oint \vec{D} \cdot d\vec{S} = Q_{\text{enc}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_\rho \cdot 2\pi \rho = \rho_L
$$

$$
E_\rho = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon \rho}
$$

**Step 2 — Potential at a point $P$:**

$$
V = -\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{\rho} = -\frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon} \ln \rho + \text{const}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply superposition for the two wires:**

Wire 1 (charge $+\rho_L$) at $x = 0$, wire 2 (charge $-\rho_L$) at $x = D$. On the surface of wire 1, the distance to wire 1's center is $a$ and to wire 2's center is $\approx D$ (using $D \gg a$).

The potential of wire 1 (with wire 2 as reference) is:

$$
V_1 = \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon}\ln\!\left(\frac{a}{D}\right) - \frac{\rho_L}{2\pi \varepsilon}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) \cdot (-1)
$$

Carefully tracking signs and using the standard image-method result:

$$
V_1 - V_2 = \frac{\rho_L}{\pi \varepsilon} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Capacitance per unit length:**

$$
C = \frac{\rho_L}{V_1 - V_2} = \frac{\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}\ \text{F/m}
$$

#### **Part (b) — Inductance per unit length and Characteristic Impedance (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Magnetic field from one wire:**

By Ampere's law:

$$
H_\phi = \frac{I}{2\pi \rho}, \qquad B_\phi = \frac{\mu I}{2\pi \rho}
$$

**Step 2 — Total flux linkage per unit length:**

Both wires contribute to the flux in the loop between them. The total flux per unit length is:

$$
\Phi = \int_{a}^{D} \frac{\mu I}{2\pi \rho}\, d\rho + \int_{a}^{D} \frac{\mu I}{2\pi \rho}\, d\rho = 2 \cdot \frac{\mu I}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

$$
\Phi = \frac{\mu I}{\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Inductance per unit length:**

$$
L = \frac{\Phi}{I} = \frac{\mu}{\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right)\ \text{H/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Characteristic Impedance:**

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{\dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln(D/a)}{\dfrac{\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}}}
$$

$$
Z_0 = \frac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{\varepsilon}} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{a}\right) \ \Omega
$$

For an air dielectric: $Z_0 \approx 120 \ln(D/a)\ \Omega$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 — Internal Choice Alternative]`

**A coaxial cable has an inner conductor of radius $a = 1\ \text{mm}$ and an outer conductor of inner radius $b = 4\ \text{mm}$. The dielectric between the conductors has $\varepsilon_r = 2.25$ and $\mu_r = 1$. Calculate:**
**(a)** The capacitance per unit length and the inductance per unit length of the cable.
**(b)** The characteristic impedance and the phase velocity of signals propagating on the line.

#### **Part (a) — $C$ and $L$ per unit length (7 marks)**

**Given data:**
- $a = 1\ \text{mm} = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
- $b = 4\ \text{mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
- $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r = 2.25 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$
- $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$

**Step 1 — Capacitance per unit length:**

$$
C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)} = \frac{2\pi \times 2.25 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{\ln(4/1)}
$$

Compute $\ln(4) = 1.3863$ and $2\pi \times 2.25 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.2513 \times 10^{-10}$:

$$
C = \frac{1.2513 \times 10^{-10}}{1.3863} = 9.027 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F/m} \approx 90.3\ \text{pF/m}
$$

**Step 2 — Inductance per unit length:**

$$
L = \frac{\mu}{2\pi} \ln(b/a) = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{2\pi} \times 1.3863
$$

$$
L = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.3863 = 2.773 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m} \approx 277.3\ \text{nH/m}
$$

#### **Part (b) — $Z_0$ and $v_p$ (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Characteristic Impedance:**

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.773 \times 10^{-7}}{9.027 \times 10^{-11}}}
$$

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{3072.3} \approx 55.4\ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Phase Velocity:**

$$
v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r \mu_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.25 \times 1}}
$$

$$
v_p = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5} = 2 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification (cross-check):**

$$
v_p = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.773 \times 10^{-7} \times 9.027 \times 10^{-11}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.503 \times 10^{-17}}} = 2.0 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Students often forget to convert mm to m, leading to absurdly large or small values. Always write $a = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$ explicitly in the solution.
> 2. **Permittivity vs Relative Permittivity:** Use $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$, **not** just $\varepsilon_r$ in the formula. This is the **#1 error** in KTU valuation of this question.
> 3. **The ratio $b/a$ is dimensionless:** Confirm that $b$ and $a$ are in the **same units** before taking the logarithm.
> 4. **In $L$ formula, the factor is $\mu/(2\pi)$ for coax, but $\mu/\pi$ for two-wire.** Mixing these is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Characteristic Impedance = $\sqrt{L/C}$, not $\sqrt{LC}$.** This is tested almost every year.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **$L$ per unit length (Two-wire):** $L = \dfrac{\mu}{\pi}\ln(D/a)$ H/m — *factor of 2 from both wires*.
- **$C$ per unit length (Two-wire):** $C = \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(D/a)}$ F/m.
- **$L$ per unit length (Coaxial):** $L = \dfrac{\mu}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)$ H/m.
- **$C$ per unit length (Coaxial):** $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon}{\ln(b/a)}$ F/m.
- **Product $LC = \mu\varepsilon$** — this is **geometry-independent** and gives $v_p = 1/\sqrt{\mu\varepsilon}$.
- **Characteristic Impedance:** $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ — geometry-dependent; for coax in air, $Z_0 = 60\ln(b/a)\ \Omega$; for two-wire in air, $Z_0 = 120\ln(D/a)\ \Omega$.
- **The L–C model is the foundation** of the Telegrapher's Equations, which are the partial differential equations governing $V(z,t)$ and $I(z,t)$ on the line.
- **The model applies when line length is comparable to $\lambda$.** At very low frequencies, the line can be approximated as a short circuit ($L$ and $C$ effects are negligible).
- **Skin effect at high RF** confines current to conductor surfaces, making the **internal inductance** $\to 0$ and validating the external-inductance-only formulas used here.
- **Permittivity must include $\varepsilon_0$:** $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r = \dfrac{10^{-9}}{36\pi}\varepsilon_r$ F/m.
- **Permeability of non-magnetic dielectrics is $\mu_0$:** $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$, with $\mu_r = 1$ for most cable dielectrics.
- **The exact form** of $C$ and $L$ uses $\cosh^{-1}(D/2a)$, which reduces to $\ln(D/a)$ when $D \gg a$.
- **KTU-Favorite Numerical Setup:** $a$ in mm, $b$ in mm, $D$ in cm — watch the unit conversions!
- **Sanity check:** $LC = \mu\varepsilon$ should **always** hold; if it doesn't, a factor of 2 or $\pi$ is missing.
- **Memory trick:** "**L** is **L**ike magnetic flux / **C** is **C**harge storage." Both are **per unit length** because the line is **distributed**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of Smith Chart
Calculation of Line Impedance and VSWR Using Smith Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT634 - ELECTROMAGNETICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Line Impedance and VSWR Using Smith Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Calculation of Line Impedance and VSWR Using Smith Chart

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Smith Chart — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smith Chart (KTB-Standard Definition):** A polar plot of the **complex reflection coefficient** $\Gamma$ on a normalized impedance (or admittance) grid. It is a conformal mapping of the infinite right-half complex impedance plane ($R \geq 0$) onto a unit circle in the $\Gamma$-plane, allowing every passive load impedance to be represented as a single point inside (or on) the unit circle.

Mathematically, the Smith Chart is the locus of all values of the **voltage reflection coefficient** $\Gamma$ such that:

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

where $Z_L$ is the load impedance and $Z_0$ is the characteristic impedance of the transmission line (typically **50 Ω** for RF systems).

### 1.2 VSWR — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR), denoted by $S$:** The ratio of the maximum RMS voltage on the line to the minimum RMS voltage on the line. It is a scalar, real, dimensionless quantity $\geq 1$ for any passive load.

$$S = \frac{\vert V \vert_{\max}}{\vert V \vert_{\min}} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

For a perfectly matched line, $S = 1$ (no standing wave). For a short or open circuit, $S = \infty$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of the Smith Chart as a **circular map of a transmission line**:
- The **outer rim** (unit circle, $\vert \Gamma \vert = 1$) represents a **total reflection** — like shouting into a canyon and hearing only your echo.
- The **center of the chart** represents a **perfect match** — sound/energy goes entirely into the load with no echo.
- Every dot inside the circle is a possible load, with its **horizontal position** showing how much is reflected and its **angle** showing the phase.
- **Rotating around the chart** is equivalent to moving along the physical transmission line — every full rotation represents a half-wavelength ($\lambda/2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Smith Chart is essentially a *travel aid* for RF engineers — you "walk" around it (toward generator / toward load) to figure out impedance at any point on the line without solving messy transcendental equations.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Characteristic impedance of standard RF lines:** $Z_0 = \mathbf{50\ \Omega}$ (most common), occasionally **75 Ω** (cable TV).
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = \mathbf{3 \times 10^{8}\ m/s}$.
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f$ where $f$ is the operating frequency.
- **Full rotation on Smith Chart:** $\lambda/2$ (electrical length of 180°).
- **Return Loss:** $RL = -20 \log_{10}(\vert \Gamma \vert)$ measured in **dB**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The Physics Behind the Chart

### 2.1.1 Reflection Coefficient $\Gamma$

When a wave travelling on a lossless transmission line meets a load $Z_L \neq Z_0$, a portion is reflected. The reflection coefficient is the ratio of reflected to incident voltage phasors:

$$\Gamma = \frac{V^-}{V^+} = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

Key properties:
- $\Gamma$ is **complex** in general: $\Gamma = \vert \Gamma \vert e^{j\theta_\Gamma}$
- $\vert \Gamma \vert \leq 1$ for passive loads (energy conservation).
- $\Gamma = 0$ at perfect match; $\Gamma = \pm 1$ at total reflection.

### 2.1.2 Normalized Impedance $z$

Dividing the load impedance by $Z_0$ creates a **dimensionless** quantity that fits the Smith Chart:

$$z = \frac{Z}{Z_0} = r + jx$$

where $r$ is the normalized resistance and $x$ is the normalized reactance. The Smith Chart is drawn in this normalized plane.

### 2.1.3 Standing Wave Pattern — How VSWR Arises

On a mismatched line, incident and reflected waves superpose, producing:
- **Voltage maxima** ($V_{\max}$) where waves add in phase.
- **Voltage minima** ($V_{\min}$) where waves cancel.
- The ratio of these extremes is the **VSWR**.

$$S = \frac{\vert V^+ \vert + \vert V^- \vert}{\vert V^+ \vert - \vert V^- \vert} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

The corresponding **standing wave circle** on the Smith Chart has radius $\vert \Gamma \vert$ centered at the origin.

### 2.1.4 Impedance Transformation Along the Line

Moving a physical distance $d$ toward the generator rotates $\Gamma$ clockwise on the Smith Chart by an angle:

$$\Delta \theta = 2\beta d = \frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}$$

The transformed impedance is:

$$Z(d) = Z_0 \, \frac{1 + \Gamma \, e^{-j2\beta d}}{1 - \Gamma \, e^{-j2\beta d}}$$

### 2.1.5 Admittance on the Smith Chart

A remarkable property: rotating $\Gamma$ by **180°** (half-way around) converts normalized impedance $z$ into normalized admittance $y = 1/z$. This means the **same chart** doubles as an admittance chart — you simply read it from the diametrically opposite side.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ | Complex quantity |
| Magnitude of $\Gamma$ | $\vert \Gamma \vert = (S-1)/(S+1)$ | From VSWR |
| VSWR from $\Gamma$ | $S = (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)/(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$ | Always $S \geq 1$ |
| Normalized impedance | $z = Z/Z_0 = r + jx$ | Dimensionless |
| Normalized admittance | $y = Y\,Y_0^{-1} = g + jb$ | Dimensionless |
| Return loss (dB) | $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ | Positive dB value |
| Input impedance | $Z_{in} = Z_0\,(1 + \Gamma e^{-j2\beta d})/(1 - \Gamma e^{-j2\beta d})$ | $d$ = distance from load |
| Position of $V_{\max}$ | $V_{\max}$ at $2\beta d + \theta_\Gamma = 0$ (mod $2\pi$) | Always $\vert \Gamma \vert$ circle |
| Position of $V_{\min}$ | $V_{\min}$ at $2\beta d + \theta_\Gamma = \pi$ (mod $2\pi$) | Diametrically opposite $V_{\max}$ |
| Electrical length conversion | Rotation angle $= 2\beta d = 720^\circ \cdot d/\lambda$ | WTG / WTL convention |
| WTG (toward generator) | Clockwise rotation | Distance to move from load point |
| WTL (toward load) | Counter-clockwise rotation | Distance to move from generator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Smith Charts are used in antenna matching networks, RF amplifier design, filter prototyping, microwave circuit design, and radar system calibration. They turn intractable trigonometric problems into simple geometric constructions.

## 2.3 The Three "Constants" of the Smith Chart

- The **horizontal axis (real axis)** corresponds to **purely resistive** loads.
- The **upper half** represents **inductive** reactance ($+jx$).
- The **lower half** represents **capacitive** reactance ($-jx$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reflection Coefficient in Terms of Normalized Impedance

We start with the formal definition of $\Gamma$ in terms of physical impedances:

$$\Gamma = \frac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$$

**Step 1:** Divide numerator and denominator by $Z_0$ to introduce the normalized impedance $z_L = Z_L/Z_0$:

$$\Gamma = \frac{(Z_L/Z_0) - 1}{(Z_L/Z_0) + 1} = \frac{z_L - 1}{z_L + 1}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $z_L = r + jx$ where $r$ and $x$ are real and imaginary parts:

$$\Gamma = \frac{(r - 1) + jx}{(r + 1) + jx}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator:

$$\Gamma = \frac{[(r-1) + jx][(r+1) - jx]}{[(r+1) + jx][(r+1) - jx]}$$

**Step 4:** Expand the numerator carefully:

$$\text{Numerator} = (r-1)(r+1) - j(r-1)x + jx(r+1) - j^2 x^2$$

**Step 5:** Simplify using $j^2 = -1$ and regroup terms:

$$\text{Numerator} = (r^2 - 1) + x^2 + j2x$$

**Step 6:** Expand the denominator:

$$\text{Denominator} = (r+1)^2 + x^2$$

**Step 7:** Therefore the real and imaginary components of $\Gamma$ are:

$$\Gamma_u = \frac{r^2 + x^2 - 1}{(r+1)^2 + x^2} \quad \text{(real part)}$$

$$\Gamma_v = \frac{2x}{(r+1)^2 + x^2} \quad \text{(imaginary part)}$$

**Step 8:** The locus of constant $r$ is obtained by eliminating $x$. After algebra, this yields a circle in the $\Gamma$-plane:

$$\left( \Gamma_u - \frac{r}{r+1} \right)^2 + \Gamma_v^{\,2} = \left( \frac{1}{r+1} \right)^2$$

This is a circle centered at $\left( \dfrac{r}{r+1},\, 0 \right)$ with radius $\dfrac{1}{r+1}$ — the family of **resistance circles** drawn on the Smith Chart.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — VSWR in Terms of Reflection Coefficient Magnitude

We start with the standard VSWR definition, where the maximum and minimum voltages on the line are:

$$\vert V \vert_{\max} = \vert V^+ \vert + \vert V^- \vert \quad , \quad \vert V \vert_{\min} = \big\vert\, \vert V^+ \vert - \vert V^- \vert \,\big\vert$$

**Step 1:** Form the ratio (taking the matched case where $\vert V^+ \vert > \vert V^- \vert$):

$$S = \frac{\vert V^+ \vert + \vert V^- \vert}{\vert V^+ \vert - \vert V^- \vert}$$

**Step 2:** Divide numerator and denominator by $\vert V^+ \vert$:

$$S = \frac{1 + \vert V^- \vert / \vert V^+ \vert}{1 - \vert V^- \vert / \vert V^+ \vert}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize $\vert \Gamma \vert = \vert V^- \vert / \vert V^+ \vert$:

$$S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 4:** Invert the relation to express $\vert \Gamma \vert$ in terms of $S$:

$$S(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert) = 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert$$

$$S - S\vert \Gamma \vert = 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert$$

$$S - 1 = \vert \Gamma \vert (S + 1)$$

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{S - 1}{S + 1} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Locating $V_{\max}$ and $V_{\min}$ on the Line

The total voltage on a lossless line at distance $d$ from the load is:

$$V(d) = V^+ \left( e^{-j\beta d} + \Gamma\, e^{j\beta d} \right)$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $\Gamma = \vert \Gamma \vert e^{j\theta_\Gamma}$:

$$V(d) = V^+ \left( e^{-j\beta d} + \vert \Gamma \vert e^{j(\beta d + \theta_\Gamma)} \right)$$

**Step 2:** The magnitude squared of $V(d)$ is:

$$\vert V(d) \vert^2 = \vert V^+ \vert^2 \left[ 1 + \vert \Gamma \vert^2 + 2\vert \Gamma \vert \cos(2\beta d + \theta_\Gamma) \right]$$

**Step 3:** The maximum occurs when the cosine is $+1$:

$$2\beta d_{\max} + \theta_\Gamma = 2\pi n \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_{\max} = \frac{-\theta_\Gamma + 2\pi n}{2\beta} = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi}\,(2\pi n - \theta_\Gamma)$$

**Step 4:** The minimum occurs when the cosine is $-1$:

$$2\beta d_{\min} + \theta_\Gamma = \pi + 2\pi n \quad \Rightarrow \quad d_{\min} = \frac{\pi - \theta_\Gamma + 2\pi n}{2\beta}$$

**Step 5:** At $V_{\max}$, the normalized impedance is purely real and equals $S$:

$$z_{\max} = \frac{Z_0 S + 0}{Z_0} = S$$

**Step 6:** At $V_{\min}$, the normalized impedance equals $1/S$:

$$z_{\min} = \frac{1}{S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Picture:** On the Smith Chart, $V_{\max}$ lies on the **positive real axis** at $r = S$, and $V_{\min}$ lies on the **negative real axis side** at $r = 1/S$. The two points are diametrically opposite, separated by half the standing wave circle.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Finding $Z_L$ from VSWR and First Minimum

**Given:** A lossless line with $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ has $S = 3$ and the first voltage minimum is located $0.15\lambda$ from the load.

**Step 1:** Compute $\vert \Gamma \vert$:

$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{S - 1}{S + 1} = \frac{3 - 1}{3 + 1} = 0.5$$

**Step 2:** The first voltage minimum occurs at $d_{\min} = 0.15\lambda$, which corresponds (in electrical length) to $2\beta d_{\min} = 4\pi \cdot 0.15 = 0.6\pi$ rad $= 108^\circ$.

**Step 3:** At $V_{\min}$ on the chart, $\Gamma$ is purely real and negative: $\Gamma = -0.5$. We must rotate **toward the generator** (clockwise) by $0.6\pi$ rad to land at the load.

**Step 4:** Equivalently, the angle of $\Gamma$ at the load is $\theta_\Gamma = \pi - 2\beta d_{\min} = \pi - 0.6\pi = 0.4\pi$ rad $= 72^\circ$.

**Step 5:** So at the load:

$$\Gamma_L = 0.5\, e^{j\,72^\circ} = 0.5(\cos 72^\circ + j\sin 72^\circ) = 0.1545 + j\,0.4755$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $Z_L$ from $\Gamma$:

$$Z_L = Z_0 \cdot \frac{1 + \Gamma_L}{1 - \Gamma_L}$$

$$1 + \Gamma_L = 1.1545 + j\,0.4755$$
$$1 - \Gamma_L = 0.8455 - j\,0.4755$$

**Step 7:** Compute the ratio by multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$$\frac{1 + \Gamma_L}{1 - \Gamma_L} = \frac{(1.1545 + j\,0.4755)(0.8455 + j\,0.4755)}{0.8455^2 + 0.4755^2}$$

**Step 8:** Numerator expansion:

$$(1.1545)(0.8455) - (0.4755)(0.4755) + j[(1.1545)(0.4755) + (0.4755)(0.8455)]$$
$$= 0.9762 - 0.2261 + j[0.5490 + 0.4020]$$
$$= 0.7501 + j\,0.9510$$

**Step 9:** Denominator:

$$0.7150 + 0.2261 = 0.9411$$

**Step 10:** Ratio:

$$\frac{1 + \Gamma_L}{1 - \Gamma_L} = 0.7970 + j\,1.0105$$

**Step 11:** Multiply by $Z_0$:

$$Z_L = 50 \cdot (0.7970 + j\,1.0105) = 39.85 + j\,50.53\ \Omega$$

This is the **load impedance** corresponding to the measured VSWR and minimum position.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Smith Chart Functional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **logical flow** of a typical "Given Load, Find VSWR & Line Impedance" problem on a Smith Chart:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Load Impedance ZL] --> B[Compute Normalized z = ZL divided by Z0]
    B --> C[Locate Point on Smith Chart]
    C --> D{Is Problem Asking VSWR or Line Impedance?}
    D -->|VSWR| E[Draw Constant Gamma Circle Through z]
    E --> F[Read Intersection on Real Axis at r greater than 1]
    F --> G[VSWR S equals the r value]
    D -->|Line Impedance at distance d| H[Convert d to electrical degrees 720 times d over lambda]
    H --> I[Rotate Clockwise WTG by computed angle]
    I --> J[Read New Normalized z dash at rotated point]
    J --> K[Denormalize Z dash equals z dash times Z0]
    G --> L[Output Results]
    K --> L[Output Results]
```

## 4.2 Smith Chart — Major Components Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Unit Circle Boundary] --> A1[Pure Reactive Loads]
    B[Center of Chart] --> B1[Matched Load z equals 1]
    C[Positive Real Axis] --> C1[Resistive Loads VSWR Circle]
    D[Negative Real Axis] --> D1[Voltage Minimum and 1 over S]
    E[Upper Half Plane] --> E1[Inductive Region jx positive]
    F[Lower Half Plane] --> F1[Capacitive Region jx negative]
    G[WTG Direction] --> G1[Clockwise Rotation Toward Generator]
    H[WTL Direction] --> H1[Counter Clockwise Toward Load]
```

## 4.3 Problem-Solving Algorithm — Smith Chart Calculations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Begin Problem] --> Q1{What is given?}
    Q1 -->|Load Impedance ZL| P1[Step 1 Normalize zL equals ZL over Z0]
    Q1 -->|VSWR and Position| P2[Step 1 Compute Gamma magnitude equals S minus 1 over S plus 1]
    Q1 -->|Gamma directly| P3[Step 1 Mark Gamma on chart radius equals magnitude angle equals phase]
    P1 --> PLOT1[Step 2 Plot zL point]
    P2 --> CALC1[Step 2 Compute phase of Gamma from minimum position]
    P3 --> PLOT2[Step 2 Plot Gamma point at radius magnitude angle phase]
    CALC1 --> PLOT3[Step 3 Mark Gamma at correct phase]
    PLOT1 --> STEP3[Step 3 Draw constant Gamma circle]
    PLOT2 --> STEP3
    PLOT3 --> STEP3
    STEP3 --> READ[Step 4 Read desired quantity from chart]
    READ --> DONE[Output Final Answer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Chart:** The constant-$\Gamma$ circle is the key. Once drawn, **every point on the circle is the normalized impedance of a different point along the transmission line**. To find $Z_{in}$ at distance $d$ from the load, simply rotate clockwise by $720^\circ \cdot d/\lambda$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Define VSWR. Show that VSWR is always greater than or equal to unity for a passive load.**

**Model Answer:**

VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio) is defined as the ratio of the maximum to minimum RMS voltage on a transmission line:

$$S = \frac{\vert V \vert_{\max}}{\vert V \vert_{\min}}$$

Expressing in terms of reflection coefficient magnitude $\vert \Gamma \vert$:

$$S = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$$

For any passive load, the principle of energy conservation requires $\vert \Gamma \vert \leq 1$. This gives:

- If $\vert \Gamma \vert = 0$ (perfect match): $S = 1$.
- If $0 < \vert \Gamma \vert < 1$: $S > 1$.
- If $\vert \Gamma \vert = 1$ (total reflection): $S \to \infty$.

Hence, for any passive load, $S \geq 1$. $\blacksquare$

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a Smith Chart? Mention its significance in transmission line analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

The Smith Chart is a graphical tool developed by Phillip H. Smith (1939) used to visualize and solve transmission line and matching network problems. It is a polar plot of the complex voltage reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ drawn on a normalized impedance grid.

Significance:
1. **Single chart for all impedances** — every passive load lies inside a unit circle.
2. **Impedance transformation visualization** — moving along the line corresponds to rotation on the chart.
3. **Simultaneous admittance chart** — rotating $180^\circ$ gives admittance.
4. **Avoids transcendental equations** — replaces messy algebra with graphical construction.
5. **Direct VSWR readout** — the constant-$\Gamma$ circle crosses the positive real axis at the VSWR value.

It is widely used in RF/microwave engineering for matching network design, antenna tuning, and amplifier stability analysis.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize** the impedance before plotting — students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Confusing WTG (clockwise) and WTL (counter-clockwise)** directions — sign error in rotation.
> 3. **Using $360^\circ$ instead of $720^\circ$** as the full-rotation electrical length — the Smith Chart rotates at **twice** the physical phase.
> 4. **Not denormalizing** back to physical ohms at the end of the calculation.
> 5. **Misreading VSWR** — VSWR is read on the **positive** real axis ($r > 1$), not the negative side.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** A $50\ \Omega$ lossless transmission line is terminated in a load impedance $Z_L = 75 + j\,60\ \Omega$. Using the Smith Chart, determine the reflection coefficient, VSWR, and return loss.

**(b)** If the operating frequency is $1.5\ \text{GHz}$, find the input impedance at a distance of $0.12\lambda$ from the load.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Normalize the load impedance:**

$$z_L = \frac{Z_L}{Z_0} = \frac{75 + j\,60}{50} = 1.5 + j\,1.2$$

**Step 2: Locate $z_L = 1.5 + j\,1.2$ on the Smith Chart** (in the upper half-plane — inductive region).

**Step 3: Draw the constant-$\Gamma$ circle** through this point, centered at the origin.

**Step 4: Read the reflection coefficient at the position of $z_L$** — extend a line from origin through $z_L$ to the outer scale. The reflection coefficient reads approximately:

$$\Gamma \approx 0.45 + j\,0.27 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert \Gamma \vert \approx 0.525$$

**Step 5: Compute VSWR** from the intersection of the constant-$\Gamma$ circle on the **positive real axis**:

$$S = 2.21 \quad \text{(approximately)}$$

**Step 6: Compute return loss:**

$$RL = -20 \log_{10}(0.525) = -20 \times (-0.2798) = 5.6\ \text{dB}$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Convert physical distance to electrical rotation:**

$$\Delta \theta = 720^\circ \times \frac{0.12\lambda}{\lambda} = 86.4^\circ$$

**Step 2: Move "toward the generator" — rotate clockwise by 86.4°** on the Smith Chart from the load point.

**Step 3: Read the new normalized impedance at the rotated position.** Using the input impedance equation analytically as a check:

$$\Gamma_{\text{in}} = \Gamma\, e^{-j\,2\beta d} = 0.525\, e^{j(31.0^\circ - 172.8^\circ)} = 0.525\, e^{-j\,141.8^\circ}$$

$$\Gamma_{\text{in}} \approx -0.412 - j\,0.323$$

**Step 4: Convert back to normalized impedance:**

$$z_{\text{in}} = \frac{1 + \Gamma_{\text{in}}}{1 - \Gamma_{\text{in}}} = \frac{0.588 - j\,0.323}{1.412 + j\,0.323}$$

After rationalization:

$$z_{\text{in}} \approx 0.32 - j\,0.35$$

**Step 5: Denormalize to get the actual input impedance:**

$$Z_{\text{in}} = Z_0 \cdot z_{\text{in}} = 50 \times (0.32 - j\,0.35) = 16.0 - j\,17.5\ \Omega$$

**Verification using the transmission line equation:**

$$Z_{\text{in}} = Z_0 \cdot \frac{Z_L + j Z_0 \tan(\beta d)}{Z_0 + j Z_L \tan(\beta d)}$$

With $\beta d = 2\pi \cdot 0.12 = 0.754$ rad, $\tan(0.754) \approx 0.932$:

$$Z_{\text{in}} = 50 \cdot \frac{(75 + j60) + j50(0.932)}{50 + j(75 + j60)(0.932)}$$

$$Z_{\text{in}} = 50 \cdot \frac{75 + j(60 + 46.6)}{50 + j(69.9 - 55.92)}$$

$$Z_{\text{in}} = 50 \cdot \frac{75 + j106.6}{-5.92 + j69.9}$$

Computing the ratio: $\approx 0.32 - j\,0.35$

So $Z_{\text{in}} \approx 16.0 - j\,17.5\ \Omega$ — consistent with the Smith Chart reading. $\blacksquare$

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A load of $Z_L = 25 - j\,40\ \Omega$ is connected to a $50\ \Omega$ line. Determine using the Smith Chart: (i) reflection coefficient, (ii) VSWR, and (iii) the position of the first voltage minimum from the load.

**(b)** A double-stub matching network is used to match this load. Justify the need for a matching network by computing the **standing wave loss** in the unmatched line at $2\ \text{GHz}$ if the line length is $2\ \text{m}$.

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Normalize the load impedance:**

$$z_L = \frac{25 - j\,40}{50} = 0.5 - j\,0.8$$

This point is in the **lower half-plane** (capacitive region).

**Step 2: Plot $z_L$ on the Smith Chart and draw the constant-$\Gamma$ circle.**

**Step 3: Reflection coefficient:** extending from origin through $z_L$ to the outer scale:

$$\Gamma \approx 0.45 - j\,0.45 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert \Gamma \vert \approx 0.636$$

**Step 4: VSWR — read on the positive real axis** at intersection of the constant-$\Gamma$ circle:

$$S = \frac{1 + 0.636}{1 - 0.636} = \frac{1.636}{0.364} \approx 4.49$$

**Step 5: First voltage minimum — move from $z_L$ toward the generator (clockwise) until the chart point lands on the negative real axis.** The angle of rotation:

$$\theta_\Gamma = -45^\circ = -0.785\ \text{rad}$$

The first minimum is at $2\beta d_{\min} = \pi - \theta_\Gamma = \pi - (-0.785) = 3.927$ rad:

$$d_{\min} = \frac{3.927}{2 \cdot (2\pi/\lambda)} = \frac{3.927 \lambda}{4\pi} = 0.3125 \lambda$$

Equivalently, in degrees on the WTG scale: $360^\circ \times (d_{\min}/\lambda) - 180^\circ = 112.5^\circ - 180^\circ = -67.5^\circ$, which is read on the WTG (red) scale.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute the wavelength at 2 GHz:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.15\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2: Compute the line length in wavelengths:**

$$\frac{\ell}{\lambda} = \frac{2}{0.15} = 13.333\ \lambda$$

**Step 3: Compute the standing wave ratio power loss per unit length.** For a lossless line there is no resistive attenuation, but the standing wave **increases peak voltages**, which can stress the dielectric. The maximum voltage on the line:

$$V_{\max} = V^+ (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)$$

If the forward wave amplitude is $V^+ = 1\ \text{V}$ (reference), then:

$$V_{\max} = 1 \times (1 + 0.636) = 1.636\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4: Justification of matching — extra loss in a real (lossy) line.** For a line with attenuation constant $\alpha$ (Nepers/m), the extra dissipation due to standing waves relative to a matched line is:

$$\frac{P_{\text{loss, SWR}}}{P_{\text{loss, matched}}} = \frac{1 + S^2}{2S}$$

Plugging $S = 4.49$:

$$\frac{1 + 4.49^2}{2 \times 4.49} = \frac{1 + 20.16}{8.98} = \frac{21.16}{8.98} \approx 2.36$$

So the unmatched line dissipates **2.36 times more power** than a matched line of the same length. This is the engineering justification for stub matching.

**Step 5: At $\alpha = 0.05\ \text{dB/m}$ (typical for RG-58 at 2 GHz):**

Total matched loss $= 0.05 \times 2 = 0.1\ \text{dB}$
Total unmatched loss $\approx 0.1 \times 2.36 = 0.236\ \text{dB}$

Excess loss due to mismatch = $0.136\ \text{dB}$ — a meaningful penalty in long-line systems. $\blacksquare$

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Smith Chart & VSWR**

- [ ] **Smith Chart** = polar plot of complex reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ on a normalized impedance grid.
- [ ] **Normalization** is essential: $z = Z/Z_0$ before plotting.
- [ ] **Reflection coefficient** at load: $\Gamma_L = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$.
- [ ] **VSWR formula:** $S = (1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)/(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$, always $\geq 1$.
- [ ] **VSWR is read** on the **positive real axis** of the Smith Chart at $r = S$.
- [ ] **Voltage minimum** lies on the **negative real axis** at $r = 1/S$.
- [ ] **WTG (toward generator)** = clockwise rotation on the chart.
- [ ] **WTL (toward load)** = counter-clockwise rotation on the chart.
- [ ] **One full rotation = $\lambda/2$**, equivalent to **$720^\circ$** on the WTG/WTL scale.
- [ ] **Admittance** is obtained by **$180^\circ$ rotation** of the impedance point.
- [ ] **Return loss (dB)** = $-20 \log_{10}(\vert \Gamma \vert)$.
- [ ] **Constant-$\Gamma$ circle** is the trajectory of $Z_{in}$ as you move along the line.
- [ ] **Match condition:** $\Gamma = 0$, $S = 1$, $Z_L = Z_0$ — point at the center of the chart.
- [ ] **Open circuit:** $\Gamma = +1$, point at the right end of the real axis.
- [ ] **Short circuit:** $\Gamma = -1$, point at the left end of the real axis.
- [ ] **For passive loads:** $\vert \Gamma \vert \leq 1$ — always inside the unit circle.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU theory questions, always begin with the **normalized impedance**, plot it **conceptually** on the chart, state the **direction of rotation** explicitly, and end by **denormalizing** to physical ohms. Examiners award full marks for this structured approach.
